title: MFA completely bypassed on the primary (web/Vercel) login route
location: apps/web/src/server/services/auth.ts:40-83 (login), reached via apps/web/src/app/api/auth/login/route.ts and apps/web/src/lib/api-client.ts:7 (API_BASE defaults to "" -> /api/auth/login on Vercel)
evidence: The Express login() returns mfaRequired:true + a 5-min mfaPendingToken when user.mfaEnabled (auth.service.ts:94-105) and withholds the real token pair until /auth/mfa/verify. The web login() never reads user.mfaEnabled/mfaSecret at all — after bcrypt.compare succeeds it immediately returns `token: signSession({uid,email})` (auth.ts:79-82). The shipped client login page calls api("/auth/login") (login/page.tsx:19) and API_BASE defaults to "" (api-client.ts:7), so the request hits the Vercel route, not Railway. A user who enrolled MFA via the API gets full account access with password only. There is no MFA code reference anywhere under apps/web (grep for mfa returned nothing in the web auth path).
recommendation: Mirror the Express MFA challenge in the web login service: if user.mfaEnabled, return mfaRequired + a signed short-TTL pending ticket and add a /api/auth/mfa/verify web route; do not issue a session token on password-only when MFA is enabled. Ideally consolidate to a single auth backend so the two implementations cannot drift.
verifyNote: Verified: web login() (apps/web/src/server/services/auth.ts:40-83) never reads user.mfaEnabled/mfaSecret and returns signSession({uid,email}) immediately after bcrypt.compare. API login() (apps/api/src/services/auth.service.ts:94-105) DOES gate on mfaEnabled and returns mfaRequired+mfaPendingToken. login/page.tsx:19 calls api('/auth/login'); API_BASE defaults to '' (api-client.ts:7) so it hits the Vercel route — confirmed live: GET https://communitysafe.app/api/auth/login returns 405 (a Next route handler defining only POST), and /api/auth/me returns 401. The web app has ZERO MFA routes (no enroll/verify under apps/web/src/app/api/auth — only anonymous/register/login/me), so MFA can only be enrolled via the Railway API, but the shared User table means any such user logging in on the primary web surface bypasses the second factor with password only. Critical is correct.

title: Two divergent auth stacks; production web uses the weaker one and the entire Express MFA/refresh/logout system is unreachable dead code
location: apps/web/src/lib/api-client.ts:7,51 (same-origin /api only) vs apps/api/src/routes/auth.routes.ts (full stack); web auth at apps/web/src/server/{lib/jwt.ts,lib/auth.ts,services/auth.ts}
evidence: The web client sets API_BASE = '' (same-origin) and only ever POSTs to /api/auth/anonymous, /api/auth/login, GET /api/auth/me. grep across apps/web/src for 'auth/logout|auth/refresh|auth/mfa|mfaRequired|refreshToken|tokenVersion' returns ZERO client calls. The full Express auth surface (POST /auth/logout, /auth/refresh, /auth/mfa/*, /auth/account/delete with soft-delete, account lockout, tokenVersion revocation) lives on a different host (communitysafe-api-production.up.railway.app, confirmed live in the page CSP connect-src) that the web app never calls for auth. Live: GET https://communitysafe.app/api/auth/me with a freshly minted device token succeeds, proving the same-origin web stack is the one in use.
recommendation: Pick one canonical auth implementation. Either route the web app's logout/MFA/refresh/delete through the Express service, or port tokenVersion-based revocation + MFA into the web stack. Delete or clearly quarantine the unused stack so future maintainers don't assume MFA/logout protect production users.
verifyNote: Verified. Web client apps/web/src/lib/api-client.ts:7 sets API_BASE='' (same-origin /api) and only ever POSTs /auth/anonymous, /auth/login, GET /auth/me; grep across apps/web/src (excluding generated/.next) for auth/logout|auth/refresh|auth/mfa|mfaRequired|mfaPendingToken|refreshToken returns ZERO client calls, and grep for 'mfa' returns nothing. The full Express surface (logout/refresh/mfa/account-delete) exists at apps/api/src/routes/auth.routes.ts:82-180 on communitysafe-api-production.up.railway.app (confirmed present in live CSP connect-src). Live: POST /api/auth/anonymous minted a same-origin token, GET /api/auth/me with it succeeded (uid cmpx5h8ui..., displayName 'Anonymous device'), proving the same-origin web stack is in use and the Express auth endpoints are unreachable from the only UI. One nuance: the web stack is no longer purely 'weaker' — v106 added account lockout (services/auth.ts:37-77) and deleted/banned revocation (lib/auth.ts:20-40) — but the structural dead-code/divergence claim holds. High retained given the MFA-bypass implication.

title: Canonical web JWT has no tokenVersion and no logout endpoint — a leaked/compromised token cannot be revoked for its full 24h lifetime
location: apps/web/src/server/lib/jwt.ts:5-8,17-28 (payload is only {uid,email}); apps/web/src/server/lib/auth.ts:8-15 (comment admits gap); no /api/auth/logout route exists
evidence: Web signSession encodes only {uid,email}; live token decode confirms payload {uid,email,iat,exp} with exp-iat = 86400s (24h, JWT_EXPIRES_IN default '24h' in apps/web/src/server/lib/env.ts:23). apps/web/src/server/lib/auth.ts comment: 'The JWT only carries {uid,email} (no tokenVersion)... a soft-deleted or permanently-banned user's token stayed valid for the full 24h TTL'. There is NO web logout route (find apps/web/src/app/api/auth shows only anonymous/login/register/me). isSessionRevoked only catches deleted/banned accounts, NOT 'sign me out / sign out everywhere' for a still-valid account. The Express tokenVersion revocation (apps/api/src/services/auth.service.ts:161) never applies to web tokens.
recommendation: Add a logout/revocation path on the web stack. Minimum: add a tokenVersion column check to web verifySession (the User model already has tokenVersion) and a POST /api/auth/logout that bumps it; or shorten TTL drastically and issue refresh tokens. Until then, document that web sessions cannot be force-revoked except via account ban/delete.
verifyNote: Verified. apps/web/src/server/lib/jwt.ts:5-8 SessionPayload is only {uid,email}; signSession encodes only that. Live token decode confirms payload {uid,email,iat,exp} with exp-iat = 86400 (24h); JWT_EXPIRES_IN default '24h' at env.ts:23. apps/web/src/server/lib/auth.ts:7-15 comment explicitly admits 'The JWT only carries {uid,email} (no tokenVersion)... stayed valid for the full 24h TTL'. No web logout route exists (apps/web/src/app/api/auth has only anonymous/login/me/register). isSessionRevoked (auth.ts:20-40) catches only deleted/banned, not voluntary sign-out for a still-valid account. Express tokenVersion revocation (auth.service.ts:161-168) never applies to web tokens. A leaked still-valid web token cannot be force-revoked for its 24h life. High is appropriate (mitigated partly by 24h TTL + deleted/banned check, but no logout/sign-out-everywhere).

title: MFA is fully built on the Express side but completely unreachable from the web UI, and the web login service doesn't implement it at all
location: apps/web/src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx:19-24 (ignores mfaRequired); apps/web/src/server/services/auth.ts:40-83 (web login has no mfaEnabled branch); apps/api/src/services/mfa.service.ts + auth.routes.ts:123-180 (full TOTP flow, never called by web)
evidence: The web login page reads only `r.token` from the response and never handles the `mfaRequired:true` / `mfaPendingToken` shape that the Express login returns (apps/api/src/services/auth.service.ts:94-105). The web login service (apps/web/src/server/services/auth.ts) has NO `if (user.mfaEnabled)` branch — it returns a full token regardless of mfaEnabled. So if a user enrolled MFA via the Express API, logging in through the web (the only UI) would bypass MFA entirely and still mint a session. There is no MFA enroll/verify UI anywhere in apps/web (grep for 'mfa' in components/app returns nothing user-facing).
recommendation: If MFA is a product feature, implement the mfaEnabled branch in web services/auth.ts login() and add enroll/challenge UI; if not, remove the MFA endpoints and the mfaEnabled column so the feature isn't half-wired (currently a user CAN set mfaEnabled and then have it silently ignored on the real login path — a security-expectation violation).
verifyNote: Verified. apps/web/src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx:19-24 reads only r.token and router.push('/threats') — no mfaRequired/mfaPendingToken handling. Web login service apps/web/src/server/services/auth.ts:40-83 has NO 'if (user.mfaEnabled)' branch — returns a full session token regardless. Express login (apps/api/src/services/auth.service.ts:94-105) DOES branch on mfaEnabled and returns mfaRequired+mfaPendingToken. So a user who enrolled MFA via the Express API would, on the web (the only UI), get a full session with MFA silently bypassed. No MFA enroll/challenge UI exists in web (grep 'mfa' returns nothing). This is a real security-expectation violation, high is correct.

title: SMS/Twilio channel unconfigured in production — phone-based SOS, live-share, and check-in alerts silently never send
evidence: Live test: POST /api/safety/live-share with {"contact":"+14155551212"} returned 201 with delivery {"kind":"phone","sent":false,"reason":"sms_not_configured"}. The same SMS path backs SOS (sos.ts notifyContact) and check-in expiry (check-in.ts triggerExpiry). So any trusted contact who provided only a phone number will never be alerted on SOS or a missed check-in. The live-share UI surfaces 'Twilio not configured' (honest), but the SOS receipt list and the daily check-in expiry have no equivalent user-facing warning at arm time — a user can add a phone-only contact, arm SOS, and believe that contact will be reached.
recommendation: Configure Twilio (or another SMS provider) in production, OR clearly gate phone-only contacts: at contact-add and at SOS/check-in arm time, warn that SMS delivery is currently unavailable and only email contacts will be reached. Do not let the product imply a phone contact will be alerted when the channel is a no-op.
verifyNote: Reproduced live: POST /api/safety/live-share {"contact":"+14155551212"} returned 201 with delivery {"kind":"phone","sent":false,"reason":"sms_not_configured"}. sms.ts:7-9 returns {ok:false,skipped:true} when TWILIO_* env vars are unset. notifications/index.ts:26-37 (notifyContact, used by both sos.ts:62 and check-in triggerExpiry) routes phone to sendSms; on skipped with no email it emits receipt status 'failed' detail 'sms_provider_not_configured_and_no_email' (line 36) — a phone-only trusted contact is never reached on SOS or a missed check-in. SOS hard-blocks at 0 contacts but does not warn about phone-only-no-delivery. High is appropriate for a personal-safety product.

title: Live-share / SOS "live location" link shows the recipient no coordinates — feature is a stub despite the promise
evidence: Product copy repeatedly promises live location: SosPanel says 'a live-location link, and a map pin of where you are' (safety/page.tsx:480) and LiveSharePanel is titled 'Live location share'. But the recipient view at /share/<token> renders no map and no coordinates: 'Live coordinate streaming is on the roadmap. For now, this link confirms an active Live Share session' (share/[token]/page.tsx:26-31). The API GET /api/share/<token> returns only {expiresAt, userId} — confirmed live. createLiveShare/resolveSharedView never store or serve any lat/lng (live-share.ts:31-103). The only location ever transmitted is a one-time Google Maps pin embedded in the SOS email body (sos.ts:48), not in the durable share link. So the durable 'live location' link is non-functional.
recommendation: Either implement periodic coordinate updates (writing lat/lng to the LiveShareLink and rendering a map on the share page) or rewrite the SOS/live-share copy to stop calling it a 'live location' link / 'map pin' and describe only what it actually does (a session-confirmation link). For a safety product, claiming live tracking that doesn't exist is a meaningful expectation gap.
verifyNote: Verified in code and live. share/[token]/page.tsx:26-31 renders 'Live coordinate streaming is on the roadmap... this link confirms an active Live Share session' — no map, no coords. resolveSharedView (live-share.ts:97-103) returns only {expiresAt, userId}; createLiveShare never stores lat/lng. Live GET /api/share/<token> returned {"expiresAt":...,"userId":...} only. Yet copy promises 'a live-location link, and a map pin of where you are' (safety/page.tsx:480) and 'Live location share' (page.tsx:803). The only location ever sent is a one-time Google Maps pin in the SOS email body (sos.ts:48), not the durable share link. Expectation gap confirmed; high stands for a safety product.

title: Account hard-delete / purge worker does not exist; soft-deleted user PII retained indefinitely (contradicts GDPR comments)
evidence: auth.service.ts:188 comment: 'The retention worker hard-deletes any row whose deletedAt is past the grace window.' auth.routes.ts:107: 'The row stays in the DB until the retention worker hard-deletes past the grace window.' But retention.worker.ts only runs `prisma.securityAuditLog.deleteMany({ where: { createdAt: { lt: cutoff } } })` — it NEVER touches the User table. `grep -rn user.deleteMany|purge|hardDelete apps/api/src` returns zero matches. softDeleteAccount only obfuscates the email and sets deletedAt; the row (plus its posts, comments, trusted contacts, saved places, push subscriptions) persists forever. Project memory itself lists 'open: account-purge worker'.
recommendation: Either implement a User hard-delete sweep in the retention worker (delete Users where deletedAt < now - graceDays, relying on the existing onDelete: Cascade FKs) or correct the auth.service/auth.routes comments to stop claiming a purge happens. As written it is a documented-but-unfulfilled compliance control.
verifyNote: Verified: retention.worker.ts pruneOnce only runs prisma.securityAuditLog.deleteMany; it never touches the User table. grep for user.deleteMany/purge/hardDelete returns only generated Prisma client artifacts, no app code. softDeleteAccount (auth.service.ts:189-204) only obfuscates email + nulls displayName + bumps tokenVersion + sets deletedAt; the row and its cascade-linked posts/comments/contacts/saved-places/push-subs persist. The auth.service.ts:188 and auth.routes.ts:107 comments explicitly claim 'the retention worker hard-deletes any row whose deletedAt is past the grace window' — an unfulfilled documented compliance control. High is correct (GDPR/CCPA documented-but-unfulfilled erasure control).

title: sendEmail throws on SMTP failure but notifyContact treats it as a boolean result — one failed email aborts the rest of an emergency check-in fan-out
evidence: email.ts sendEmail does `const result = await t.sendMail(...)` with no try/catch and `return { ok: true, ... }` — it can ONLY return ok:true or throw. notifyContact does `const er = await sendEmail(...); receipts.push({... status: er.ok ? 'sent' : 'failed' })` (index.ts:44) — the 'failed' branch is unreachable; a real SMTP error throws instead. That exception propagates up through notifyContact → triggerExpiry's `for (const c of confirmed) { const r = await notifyContact(...) }` loop (check-in.service.ts:79), so the first contact whose email send fails aborts notification of every remaining contact for a timer that already fired (the timer is atomically marked TRIGGERED, so it won't retry). sendSms (sms.ts:13) similarly calls fetch with no try/catch, so a network error there throws the same way.
recommendation: Wrap sendMail/fetch in try/catch and return { ok:false } on failure so notifyContact's receipt logic works as designed, and/or wrap each notifyContact call in triggerExpiry so one contact's delivery failure cannot suppress alerts to the others. This is safety-critical: it governs the 'a contact didn't check in' emergency notification.
verifyNote: Verified: email.ts sendEmail awaits t.sendMail with no try/catch and only returns {ok:true,...} or throws — index.ts:34,45 'er.ok ? sent : failed' failed-branch is unreachable. sms.ts:13 fetch also has no try/catch (network error throws). triggerExpiry (check-in.service.ts:79-82) awaits notifyContact sequentially in a for loop with no per-iteration catch; a thrown SMTP/network error aborts notification of all remaining contacts. Timer is atomically marked TRIGGERED (lines 61-65) so it will not retry. Safety-critical emergency check-in fan-out. High is correct.

title: Citywide trend week-over-week buckets anchor to wall-clock now, not data-latest — silently empty for every lagged feed
evidence: computeCitywideTrend computes anchorMs from the freshest row (line 170) and uses it for the in-window cutoff (line 171: `cutoff = anchorMs - windowDays*DAY`), but the week-over-week sub-buckets revert to wall-clock: `const recentWeek = new Date(now - 7*DAY); const priorWeek = new Date(now - 14*DAY);` (lines 201-202), then `if (t >= recentWeek) bucketed.recent... else if (t >= priorWeek) bucketed.prior...`. For any feed lagged >14 days (the code documents LAPD showing late-2024 data, KC ~54d behind, SDPD quarterly, Phoenix stalled), every in-window row is older than now-14d, so recent=0 and prior=0 for all categories, and `if (r === 0 && p === 0) continue` (line 217) drops all three trend bullets. The per-area path getTrendForArea does this correctly: `recentWeek = new Date(anchorMs - 7*DAY)` (line 351), `priorWeek = new Date(anchorMs - 14*DAY)` (line 352). So the citywide Trend panel shows no week-over-week trend for lagged cities while the neighborhood view shows real trend — an internal inconsistency and a silent feature loss.
recommendation: Replace `now` with `anchorMs` at trend-feed.ts:201-202 so the citywide week-over-week buckets use the same data-anchored window as the in-window filter (line 171) and the per-area path (lines 351-352).
verifyNote: Verified in packages/crime-data/src/trend-feed.ts. computeCitywideTrend computes anchorMs from the freshest in-window row (lines 165-170) and uses it for the window cutoff (line 171), but the week-over-week sub-buckets revert to wall-clock: recentWeek=new Date(now-7*DAY) (line 201), priorWeek=new Date(now-14*DAY) (line 202), bucketing at lines 207-211. For any feed lagged >14d, every in-window row predates now-14d so recent=prior=0 and the `if (r===0 && p===0) continue` (line 217) drops all three trend bullets. The per-area path correctly anchors on anchorMs (lines 343, 351-352). Internal inconsistency + silent feature loss confirmed. High severity stands.

title: Uptick detector uses wall-clock now-7d/now-14d window — always empty for lagged feeds, so the Awareness uptick tile never fires for most cities
evidence: computeCitywideUpticks anchors the whole detector to wall-clock: `const now = Date.now(); const recentCutoff = new Date(now - 7*DAY); const priorCutoff = new Date(now - 14*DAY);` (lines 44-46), then counts `if (t >= recentCutoff) recent; else if (t >= priorCutoff) prior;` (lines 61-62) and skips any area with `prior < 3` (line 64). There is NO data-latest anchor like safety-score (safety-score.ts:996-997) or trend-feed's in-window filter use. The same now-anchored pattern is documented as a fixed bug elsewhere — trend-feed.ts:163-164: 'Pre-v90p7 a fixed `now - 30d` cutoff returned 0 bullets whenever the adapter was 30+ days stale.' Upticks reintroduces exactly that pattern with a tighter 14-day window, so for every feed lagged >14 days (LAPD, KC, SDPD, etc.) all areas have recent=prior=0, every area is skipped by `prior < 3`, and upticks is permanently empty. No correctness damage, but the feature is dead for the majority of the 44 cities.
recommendation: Mirror the trend-feed/safety-score anchor: compute anchorMs = freshest in-window timestamp across the city (clamped to <= now), then base recentCutoff/priorCutoff on anchorMs instead of now. Keep the prior<3 noise floor.
verifyNote: Verified in packages/crime-data/src/upticks.ts:44-67. computeCitywideUpticks anchors entirely on wall-clock: now=Date.now() (44), recentCutoff=now-7*DAY (45), priorCutoff=now-14*DAY (46); bucketed at 61-62 then `if (prior<3) continue` (64). No data-latest anchor — unlike trend-feed (170-171) and safety-score. For any feed lagged >14d all areas have recent=prior=0, all skipped by prior<3, so upticks is permanently empty. trend-feed.ts:162-164 documents this exact now-anchored pattern as a previously-fixed bug; upticks reintroduces it. Dead feature for most lagged cities. High severity stands (feature dead, not data-corrupting).

title: SSL verify-full hardening regex no-ops when DATABASE_URL has no sslmode (and never adds one)
evidence: All three Prisma instantiations build the connection string with `(process.env.DATABASE_URL ?? "").replace(/sslmode=(?:require|prefer|verify-ca)/i, "sslmode=verify-full")`. I tested the exact regex: a URL with no `sslmode` param (`postgresql://u:p@host/db`) is returned UNCHANGED — no `sslmode=verify-full` is appended. `sslmode=disable` is also left untouched. So the 'SSL pinned to verify-full' guarantee (per project MEMORY) only holds if the operator already set sslmode=require/prefer/verify-ca. With node-postgres, a URL with no sslmode and no explicit ssl option connects WITHOUT TLS, and even `sslmode=require` via libpq semantics does not verify the server cert. The code's intent (force verify-full) is defeated for the common Railway-internal / bare-URL case.
recommendation: Parse the URL and unconditionally set sslmode=verify-full (or pass an explicit `ssl: { rejectUnauthorized: true, ca }` option to PrismaPg) regardless of the inbound value, instead of a presence-dependent string replace. At minimum, append `sslmode=verify-full` when no sslmode param exists and reject `sslmode=disable`. Centralize this in one helper instead of duplicating the fragile regex in three files.
verifyNote: Verified the exact regex in all three files (packages/db/src/index.ts:8, apps/api/src/lib/prisma.ts:4, packages/db/prisma/seed.ts:6). Ran the regex against test URLs: a bare URL with no sslmode is returned UNCHANGED (no verify-full appended), and sslmode=disable is left untouched; only require/prefer/verify-ca are upgraded. PrismaPg (@prisma/adapter-pg over node-postgres) only negotiates/verifies TLS when an ssl option or recognized sslmode is present, so the 'pinned to verify-full' guarantee is genuinely defeated for the bare-URL / sslmode-absent and sslmode=disable cases, and the regex never adds a param. High is appropriate as a security-hardening gap, with the caveat that actual TLS exposure also depends on whether the managed Postgres (Railway/Neon) enforces TLS server-side; the client-side cert verification promise is what is broken.

title: Canonical Methodology page publishes wrong FBI national benchmark (364/1,896) that contradicts the numbers the app actually uses (328/1,548)
evidence: Methodology page states: "FBI Crime Data Explorer 2025. The benchmark we compare per-100,000 rates against — Violent (Persons): 364, Property: 1,896." The same page declares itself canonical (lines 21-23): "This page is the canonical source — if any in-app caption conflicts with the description here, the in-app caption is wrong." But the actual code constant is `export const FBI_NATIONAL_PER_100K_2025 = { PERSONS: 328, PROPERTY: 1548 }` (safety-score.ts:31), and NationalAverageCard.tsx renders `nationalPer100k` derived from that constant. So every in-app comparison uses 328/1,548 while the legal/methodology page publishes 364/1,896. Worse, 1,896 matches no year in the code's own documented table (safety-score.ts:23-26: 2025=1548, 2024=1771, 2023=1954, 2022=1999) — it is not even a stale-but-real figure. For a product whose Terms and Methodology repeatedly promise numbers are "reproducible: every number is derivable from the cited sources," a published benchmark that doesn't match the live computation is a self-contradicting accuracy representation and the cleanest consumer-protection exposure on the site.
recommendation: Update methodology/page.tsx lines 41-42 to the live constant (Violent 328, Property 1,548) and source it from FBI_NATIONAL_PER_100K_2025 at build time rather than a hardcoded literal, so the legal page can never drift from the engine again. Add a unit/CI assertion that the methodology-page benchmark equals the safety-score constant.
verifyNote: Verified independently. methodology/page.tsx:41 publishes 'Violent (Persons): 364, Property: 1,896' and the same page is self-declared canonical (lines 21-22). The live scoring engine constant is FBI_NATIONAL_PER_100K_2025 = { PERSONS: 328, PROPERTY: 1548 } (safety-score.ts:31). NationalAverageCard.tsx reads nationalPer100k from /safezone/safety-score (route.ts:51 → getCitywideSafetyScore → @travelsafe/crime-data/safety-score, the 328/1548 engine), so the in-app comparison card uses 328/1548 while the canonical legal page publishes 364/1896. Stronger than reported: 1896 matches NO year in the engine's own YoY table (2025=1548, 2024=1771, 2023=1954, 2022=1999) and 364 is the 2024 violent figure — the page mixes a stale-year violent number with a property number that exists nowhere. Note also a SECOND divergent live constant: assistant.ts:14 hardcodes NATIONAL_PER_100K = { PERSONS: 364, PROPERTY: 1896 } (returned to the AI via fbi_national_per_100k at assistant.ts:247), so 364/1896 is actually what the AI assistant emits — meaning the app ships two different national benchmarks. Self-contradicting accuracy representation on a page that promises every number is 'reproducible.' High is correct.

title: Public methodology + all metadata claim "FBI Crime Data Explorer 2025" benchmark, but the actual baseline data is FBI CDE 2023/2024 (year does not yet exist)
evidence: data-vintage.ts hardcodes `export const FBI_DATA_YEAR = 2025;` and `FBI_DATA_LABEL = "FBI Crime Data Explorer 2025"`, which is stamped onto OG images, layout metadata, /cities, the (app) footer, and the methodology page (methodology/page.tsx:40 'FBI Crime Data Explorer 2025'). But the actual baseline table is 2023/2024: fbi-baselines.ts:40-45 every new-city row is `year: 2023`, and the comments state the data is older — line 76 'FBI CDE 2024 values (2025 SRS not yet published)', line 119 'CDE base is the older year'. As of 2026-06-02 the FBI has not published 2025 annual CDE totals (data-vintage.ts:9 itself notes '2025 figures published Sept 2026'). The product is publicly attributing a specific, more-recent FBI vintage to its crime grades than the data it actually uses — an accuracy/substantiation problem for a safety product that grades neighborhoods against that benchmark.
recommendation: Set FBI_DATA_YEAR to the true vintage actually loaded into fbi-baselines.ts (2024, or 2023 where that is the latest row), or render the per-source year from the baseline rows rather than a single forward-dated constant. NationalAverageCard already reads `score.source.publishedYear` (NationalAverageCard.tsx:53) — make the global label derive from real data the same way.
verifyNote: Verified independently. data-vintage.ts:11 hardcodes FBI_DATA_YEAR=2025 and line 12 builds the label 'FBI Crime Data Explorer 2025', consumed in opengraph-image.tsx, layout.tsx:60, (app)/layout.tsx:71, cities/page.tsx, cities/[city] + neighborhood pages, and methodology/page.tsx:40 (literal '2025'). data-vintage.ts:9 itself states 2025 CDE figures publish Sept 2026 (i.e. do not yet exist as of 2026-06-02). fbi-baselines.ts:40-45 new cities are year:2023, with comments line 76 'FBI CDE 2024 values (2025 SRS not yet published)' and line 119 'CDE base is the older year'. The product publicly attributes a forward-dated FBI vintage to grades it derives from older data. High is appropriate (public substantiation/accuracy on a safety product).

title: TimeOfDayCard always shows empty 'no incidents' state — fetches bullets=0 then recomputes histogram from the (now empty) dispatch list
evidence: The card fetches `/safezone/trend?area=...&bullets=0` (line 162) with the comment 'this card only needs the timeOfDay histogram, not the ~740KB dispatch list'. But the histogram is built client-side by iterating `data.bullets` and counting only `b.kind === 'dispatch'` (lines 175-184). Server-side, `bullets=0` makes `bulletCap = Math.min(DISPATCH_CAP, 0) = 0` and `sortedWindow.slice(0,0) = []` (trend-feed.ts:262-264), so the response contains zero dispatch bullets. The card's `total` is therefore always 0, so for every non-date-only city it renders the line: 'No dispatchable incidents in the trend feed for {area}... the upstream police feed simply hasn't published recent records for this exact area, not that nothing happened there.' The card's TrendResp interface (lines 132-135) defines only `{area, bullets}` and never reads `data.timeOfDay`, which the server *does* compute and return regardless of bulletLimit (trend-feed.ts:295). The same neighborhood page also renders TrendPanel's 'When reports happen' chart, which DOES read `trend.timeOfDay` (requested with bullets=150) and shows the correct distribution — so users see one broken empty hour-of-day card next to one working one.
recommendation: Make TimeOfDayCard consume the server-computed `timeOfDay` buckets (the same 4-bucket object TrendPanel uses), OR drop bullets=0 and request enough dispatch bullets to build the 24-bar histogram. The cleanest fix is to read `data.timeOfDay` (and render the existing 24-bar UI only when a richer histogram source is available); recomputing client-side from dispatch bullets is redundant with the server's tz-aware `timeOfDayAnalysis`.
verifyNote: Verified end-to-end. TimeOfDayCard.tsx:162 fetches with bullets=0; safezone.routes.ts:136,146 coerces bullets→bulletLimit; trend-feed.ts:262/406 set bulletCap=Math.min(DISPATCH_CAP,0)=0 and sortedWindow.slice(0,0)=[] so the response carries ZERO kind==='dispatch' bullets (only kind==='trend'). The card builds its histogram solely by iterating data.bullets and counting b.kind==='dispatch' (lines 175-184), so total is always 0 and every non-date-only city hits the total===0 empty state at lines 239-258. TrendResp interface (132-135) never reads data.timeOfDay even though the server returns it (trend-feed.ts:295). Meanwhile TrendPanel fetches bullets=150 (TREND_DISPLAY_BULLETS=150) and renders trend.timeOfDay correctly (TrendPanel.tsx:189-190), so a working chart sits next to the broken empty one. High is correct: a card that is broken on every neighborhood page for 38/44 cities.

title: Public Coverage dashboard under-reports Los Angeles by ~6x (18 vs 116 neighborhoods) due to a stale hardcoded baseline
evidence: Baseline hardcodes los-angeles at neighborhoodCount: 18. Live https://communitysafe.app/api/coverage returns {"slug":"los-angeles",..."neighborhoodCount":18,...}, yet https://communitysafe.app/api/geo/areas?city=los-angeles returns 116 real neighborhoods (Adams-Normandie ... Woodland Hills) and apps/web/public/geo/los-angeles.geojson has 114 polygons. The LAPD adapter (lapd-socrata.ts:159-184) now maps incidents through a 114-entry rpt_dist_no->neighborhood crosswalk (src/data/la-neighborhoods.ts), so the real served count is ~116. The coverage probe times out on LA's large feed (100k incidents) and falls back to this stale 18 baseline. Compare san-diego (125) / san-francisco (43) baselines, which are accurate.
recommendation: Update COVERAGE_BASELINE['los-angeles'].neighborhoodCount from 18 to ~116 to reflect the post-crosswalk neighborhood expansion. Better: regenerate the baseline programmatically from each adapter's discover() output so it cannot drift from the live data layer.
verifyNote: Reproduced every leg. baseline.ts:35 hardcodes los-angeles neighborhoodCount:18. Live /api/coverage returns LA neighborhoodCount:18 (health live). Live /api/geo/areas?city=los-angeles returns 116 areas (key 'areas', sample Adams-Normandie/Arleta/...). apps/web/public/geo/los-angeles.geojson has 114 polygon features. lapd-socrata.ts:159-184 maps rpt_dist_no via rdToNeighborhood (la-neighborhoods.js): I evaluated it = 1131 RD keys -> 114 distinct neighborhoods, matching the 114 polygons; falls back to displayLabelLA(rawArea) when RD not in crosswalk. Served count ~116 vs dashboard 18 (~6x under-report). san-diego baseline 125 and san-francisco 43 match live for contrast. baseline.ts header even documents the timeout-fallback mechanism. High severity correct.

title: Boston and Philadelphia curated populations are keyed to stale district-anchor slugs — 0% match current neighborhood slugs, so safety scores fall back to peer-share denominator
evidence: The Boston (v97) and Philadelphia adapters now geocode incidents into real neighborhoods, emitting slugs like bos-back-bay, bos-roslindale, phl-rittenhouse, phl-fishtown-lower-kensington. But the curated population table only contains the OLD police-district anchor keys: Boston has 12 like 'bos-a1-downtown-north-end-beacon-hill-chinatown', 'bos-d4-south-end-back-bay-fenway'; Philadelphia has 21 like 'phl-12th-district-southwest-philadelphia', 'phl-9th-district-center-city', 'phl-77th-district-citywide-administrative'. Programmatic check: of the 26 current Boston neighborhood slugs, 0 have a curated population; of the 159 current Philadelphia neighborhood slugs, 0 have a curated population. By contrast DC matches 51/51 and Oakland 131/131. Per safety-score.ts (lines ~1490-1527) a missing curated/generated pop drops the area to polygon-area-share, then to peer-share (cityPop/N) — the least accurate denominator — so EVERY Boston and Philadelphia neighborhood's per-100k crime rate (and thus letter grade) is computed on a fallback population, not a real one.
recommendation: Regenerate the ACS-joined population entries for Boston and Philadelphia keyed to the CURRENT neighborhood slugs (bos-<name>, phl-<name>) using the existing Census Reporter + TigerWeb spatial-join pipeline against the bundled boston-neighborhoods.ts / phl-neighborhoods.ts polygons (the same way DC's 51 and Oakland's 131 were produced). Then delete the 12 + 21 orphaned district-anchor entries so they cannot accidentally rematch a fallback path.
verifyNote: Reproduced independently. neighborhood-populations-generated.ts under "boston" holds 12 district-anchor keys (bos-a1-downtown-north-end-beacon-hill-chinatown ... bos-e5-west-roxbury-roslindale) plus a stray det-boston-edison; under "philadelphia" 21 phl-Nth-district-* keys. boston-ckan.ts:432-434 and phl-carto.ts:218 build modern slugs from polygon NAMES (bos-back-bay, bos-roslindale, phl-rittenhouse, phl-fishtown-lower-kensington). Programmatic set-intersection: Boston 26 current slugs vs 12 keys = 0; Philadelphia 158 current slugs vs 21 keys = 0. neighborhood-population.ts:85-93 looks up by GENERATED_NEIGHBORHOOD_POPS[city][slug] / POP[city][slug] with no boston/phl manual overrides, and safety-score.ts:1492-1522 confirms the curated->polygon-area-share->peer-share(cityPop/N) fallback. DC=51 and Oakland=131 generated entries confirm the contrast. Only nit: finding says 159 Philadelphia slugs; the geocoder polygon set is 158 (159 is the served-map count). Does not affect the 0-match conclusion. High severity correct.

title: Charlotte per-division population (~1.49M) nearly doubles the city population, deflating crime rates
evidence: The 14 clt- population entries sum to 1,494,010 (e.g. clt-university-city: 404197, clt-steele-creek: 266111, clt-south: 158006). The City of Charlotte population is ~875,000. CMPD patrol divisions span all of Mecklenburg County, so these denominators reflect county/division populations, not city neighborhoods. safety-score.ts divides in-window incidents by these populations to get per-100k rates; an inflated denominator (e.g. University City at 404k) systematically understates the computed crime rate for those areas and skews the citywide fresh-area denominator (safety-score.ts:1049-1088).
recommendation: Recompute Charlotte area populations against the actual division boundary polygons within the city limits, or drop these generated values and let the city fall back to peer-share so the denominator stays bounded by CITY_POPULATION. Verify University City's 404,197 figure against a real source.
verifyNote: Verified: the 14 clt- entries in neighborhood-populations-generated.ts:169-182 sum to exactly 1,494,010 (clt-university-city 404197, clt-steele-creek 266111, clt-south 158006 all present). Actual Charlotte city population is 897,720 (population.ts:52) — so the division sum is ~1.66x city pop (finding said '~875k / nearly doubles'; the multiplier is real, slightly overstated wording). Divisions span Mecklenburg County, not city limits. Confirmed these are consumed as the denominator: safety-score.ts:1492-1503 uses knownNeighborhoodPopulation() (which reads GENERATED_NEIGHBORHOOD_POPS) as the top-priority curatedPop denominator, so an inflated 404k University City denominator systematically understates that area's per-100k rate. High severity appropriate.

title: Norfolk has ZERO neighborhood population entries while all 3 peer cities have full coverage
evidence: Grep count of population keys by city prefix: buf-=35, kc-=145, sp-=17, but nor-=0. Norfolk discovers ~50-122 neighborhoods (adapter comment line 25 says "Norfolk has 122 areas"; provenance says "~50 named neighborhoods") yet not a single one has a population entry in neighborhood-populations-generated.ts. Buffalo by contrast has all 35 (e.g. buf-allentown:3987, buf-kensington-bailey:17277). With no curated or generated population, computeSafetyScore (safety-score.ts denominator hierarchy lines 1490-1527) must fall through to polygon-area-share or peer-share (cityPop/N) for every Norfolk area, which is the least accurate denominator tier and produces less reliable per-100k rates and grades for the entire city.
recommendation: Run the ACS/TigerWeb generation step for Norfolk's Civic League polygons (norfolk.geojson) to populate nor-* entries, mirroring the buf-/kc-/sp- coverage, so Norfolk neighborhood safety scores use a real population denominator instead of peer-share.
verifyNote: Independently recounted population keys in neighborhood-populations-generated.ts: nor-=0, buf-=35, kc-=145, sp-=17. No "norfolk" section exists in the generated file at all (only 29 other cities), and grep of the manual-override neighborhood-population.ts also yields 0 nor- entries. norfolk.geojson has 110 features (verified). safety-score.ts lines 1492-1531 confirm the denominator hierarchy: knownNeighborhoodPopulation() (curated) -> polygon-area-with-peer-floor -> peer-share. With zero curated pop, every Norfolk area skips tier 1. Minor accuracy caveat on the finding's wording: because Norfolk DOES have polygons, most areas land in tier 2 (polygon-area, max with peer-share floor), not the worst tier-3 peer-share — so 'least accurate denominator tier ... for every area' slightly overstates. But the substantive claim (zero curated population vs all 3 peers fully covered, degrading per-100k accuracy citywide) is fully verified. Keeping high.

title: Denver crime data is dead in production without DENVER_ARCGIS_TOKEN, but the Coverage dashboard falsely reports it 'live'
evidence: Denver's upstream ODC_CRIME_OFFENSES_P FeatureServer requires auth: '499 GWM_0003 Token Required' / 403 without a token (adapter header comment lines 12-22). fetchPage only attaches a token if process.env.DENVER_ARCGIS_TOKEN is set (denver-arcgis.ts:96-98); that env var is NOT set anywhere in the repo (no .env.example entry, no deploy config — only the adapter, env.ts:36 readOpt, and a safety-score.ts:199 comment reference it). On 499 every page throws, getRowsDenver returns []. safety-score.ts:199-200 states plainly: 'Denver needs DENVER_ARCGIS_TOKEN set in prod or the adapter returns EMPTY — that env gap, not this scale, is the real Denver risk.' Yet COVERAGE_BASELINE['denver'] = { neighborhoodCount: 77, ... } means the public /api/coverage dashboard reports Denver as live with 77 neighborhoods even when the adapter is returning zero rows. The 78-feature denver.geojson then renders all polygons at the zero-count risk level (1/green).
recommendation: Either provision DENVER_ARCGIS_TOKEN in Railway/Vercel prod (the adapter is otherwise correct), or, if the token is unobtainable, switch Denver's coverage status to honest 'warming-up'/'no-data' (the baseline fallback in status.ts:160-167 should be suppressed for token-gated cities) so the dashboard does not claim live coverage that does not exist. Add DENVER_ARCGIS_TOKEN to .env.example with a comment.
verifyNote: Verified all claims independently. denver-arcgis.ts:96-98 attaches token only if process.env.DENVER_ARCGIS_TOKEN set; on 499/embedded-error fetchPage throws and getRowsDenver catch returns []. grep across packages/apps/workers shows DENVER_ARCGIS_TOKEN ONLY in adapter, env.ts:36 (readOpt), safety-score.ts:199 comment, and compiled dist — NOT in .env.example (both /.env.example and apps/web/.env.example, grep exit 1) nor any deploy config. safety-score.ts:199-200 comment verbatim matches. COVERAGE_BASELINE['denver']={neighborhoodCount:77} at baseline.ts:42. status.ts:160-167 fallback sets health='live' from baseline when live count is 0. Live probe of https://communitysafe.app/api/coverage returns denver health=live count=77 — confirms the dashboard claims Denver live/77 even with dead upstream. High severity correct.

title: Sacramento: every incident gets the same downtown centroid, so all crime-map dots stack on one point
evidence: The Sacramento ArcGIS feed has no per-incident lat/lng; the adapter hardcodes SACRAMENTO_CENTROID = { lat: 38.5816, lng: -121.4944 } (line 78) and assigns it to every row's lat/lng (lines 241-242) and to every discovered area's centroid (line 273). CrimeMap.tsx:665-684 plots per-incident drill-down dots at r.lat/r.lng — so for Sacramento, selecting ANY of the 117 neighborhoods renders every incident dot piled on the exact same downtown coordinate, regardless of where the crime occurred. (Choropleth polygon coloring still works because it is name-keyed, not centroid-keyed.)
recommendation: Suppress per-incident CircleMarker dots for Sacramento (and any adapter whose incidents lack true coordinates) — gate the dot rendering on whether the adapter provides real per-row coords, or have the adapter leave lat/lng undefined so CrimeMap's existing finite-check skips them, rather than emitting a fake shared centroid that misleads users into thinking every crime happened downtown.
verifyNote: Verified exactly. sacramento-arcgis.ts:78 SACRAMENTO_CENTROID={lat:38.5816,lng:-121.4944}; line 241-242 every row lat/lng=centroid; line 273 every discovered-area centroid=centroid (line numbers exact). CrimeMap.tsx:665-684 renders per-incident CircleMarker at center=[r.lat,r.lng] after finite+range guards (666-668) — the shared centroid passes the guards so all Sacramento dots stack on one downtown point. Choropleth is name-keyed so unaffected. Caveat: live prod currently returns Sacramento newestIncidentAt:null (adapter returning 0 rows), so no dots render right now; the defect manifests whenever live rows flow. Code defect real, high severity correct.

title: Atlanta: every discovered neighborhood shares one hardcoded centroid, breaking geolocation snapping
evidence: buildAtlantaAreas maps every area to `centroid: ATLANTA_CENTROID` (33.7490, -84.3880) — a single citywide point — instead of averaging per-area incident lat/lng the way indianapolis-arcgis.ts:188, raleigh-arcgis.ts:127 and tucson-arcgis.ts:222 do (`centroid: { lat: e.latSum/e.count, lng: e.lngSum/e.count }`). KnownArea.centroid is consumed by nearestArea() in neighborhoods.ts:74 (haversineKm to area.centroid), which is wired to /api/geo/lookup (apps/web/src/app/api/geo/lookup/route.ts:36) and crime-data.routes.ts:37. Because all 242 Atlanta areas report identical distance from any query point, nearestArea cannot resolve the correct neighborhood — it returns whichever area happens to be first, regardless of where the user actually is.
recommendation: Compute Atlanta centroids from real per-area incident coordinates like the other three adapters (accumulate latSum/lngSum/count in buildAtlantaAreas). The adapter already keeps r.lat/r.lng per row; the only reason it can't today is the lines 105-106 fallback that overwrites missing/zero coords with ATLANTA_CENTROID — filter those rows out of the centroid average instead of folding them into city center.
verifyNote: Verified packages/crime-data/src/adapters/atlanta-arcgis.ts:135 maps EVERY area to ATLANTA_CENTROID (33.7490,-84.3880); lines 105-106 fold missing/zero coords into the same point. By contrast indianapolis-arcgis.ts:188, raleigh-arcgis.ts:127, tucson-arcgis.ts:222 all compute centroid:{lat:e.latSum/e.count,lng:e.lngSum/e.count}. The consequence is real and city-scoped: apps/api/src/services/geo/lookup.service.ts:24-36 (nearestAreaForCity) iterates city.discover() for the selected city and selects via strict `km < best.km`, so when all 242 Atlanta centroids are identical it always returns whichever area sorts first (buildAtlantaAreas sorts by label.localeCompare → 'Adair Park'), independent of the user's actual coordinates. Atlanta is wired through this adapter (cities.ts:331 atlantaAdapter). High is correct.

title: Phoenix generated population entries are orphaned (ZIP-keyed) and never match the village slugs the adapter emits — every Phoenix area loses its census denominator
recommendation: Re-key the Phoenix generated populations to village slugs by summing each village's member-ZIP populations (the ZIP→village map at phoenix-ckan.ts:144-172 plus the existing 41 ZIP populations make this a deterministic rebuild), or add a village-sum layer in knownNeighborhoodPopulation(). Until then, confirm the polygon-area-share fallback produces sane Phoenix per-100k rates and flag that Phoenix scores currently use no census population.
evidence: knownNeighborhoodPopulation() (neighborhood-population.ts:89) looks up GENERATED_NEIGHBORHOOD_POPS["phoenix"][areaSlug]. The adapter emits areaSlug like "phx-camelback-east"/"phx-maryvale" (slugForArea→villageSlug, phoenix-ckan.ts:224-227), but ALL 41 generated keys are ZIPs: "phx-85003":7712 … "phx-85086". grep for any village-slug key (phx-camelback-east, phx-maryvale, phx-estrella, etc.) returns zero matches. So generated lookup always misses → falls through to polygon-area/peer-share for every one of the 13 villages. This is a stale artifact of the v105 ZIP→village migration noted in the adapter (lines 214-218).
verifyNote: Independently verified. neighborhood-populations-generated.ts:1298-1339 "phoenix" block has exactly 41 keys, all ZIP-based (phx-85003..phx-85086); grep for any village-slug key (phx-camelback-east/phx-maryvale/phx-estrella/etc.) returns ZERO matches. phoenix-ckan.ts slugForArea (line 224-227) emits village slugs via villageSlug(label) -> 'phx-camelback-east' etc., and getDiscoveredAreasPhoenix (336-352) emits ONLY the 13 villages (UNMAPPED bucket skipped line 340). knownNeighborhoodPopulation (neighborhood-population.ts:89) does GENERATED_NEIGHBORHOOD_POPS['phoenix'][areaSlug] -> undefined for every village slug -> returns null. safety-score.ts:1492-1522 then falls to polygon-area share (1509). So every Phoenix area loses its census denominator. Note: phoenix.geojson IS keyed by village NAME (13/13 match), so the MAP renders fine; only the slug-keyed population lookup is broken, confirming the bug is real and distinct. High severity justified (systemic accuracy gap across all 13 Phoenix areas), not data-loss (polygon fallback still yields a denominator).

title: Web session tokens cannot be revoked on logout / password-change / sign-out-everywhere
evidence: Web signSession encodes only {uid,email} — no ver/tokenVersion and no typ (jwt.ts:5-8). requireSession only rejects deleted/banned accounts (auth.ts:30) and explicitly states in its comment (auth.ts:7-15) that version-based revocation is impossible without re-issuing tokens. The env comment (env.ts:18-19) claims "we have no server-side revocation table yet / no tokenVersion column" — but the column DOES exist (schema.prisma:109) and the Express side uses it. So a stolen/leaked web token stays valid for the full 24h regardless of logout or password change; the only kill-switch is deleting or banning the account.
recommendation: Add a ver claim to web tokens and check it against User.tokenVersion in isSessionRevoked (the column already exists), and increment tokenVersion on logout/password-change so web sessions get the same revocation guarantee the API already has. Correct the stale comment in env.ts.
verifyNote: Verified: web signSession encodes only {uid,email} with no ver/typ (apps/web/src/server/lib/jwt.ts:5-8,17-27). isSessionRevoked checks only deletedAt/permanentlyBanned (apps/web/src/server/lib/auth.ts:20-40). The tokenVersion column DOES exist (schema.prisma:109) and the API enforces it (refreshAccessToken/logout). The env.ts:18-19 comment claiming 'no tokenVersion column' is stale/false. Web account/delete has no tokenVersion bump in the route file. A web token stays valid for the full 24h with no logout/password-change kill switch. High is correct.

title: TOTP MFA verification has no per-account lockout — brute-forceable via distributed IPs
evidence: The account lockout (failedAttempts/lockedUntil, MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS=5) is incremented ONLY on a bad bcrypt.compare inside login() (auth.service.ts:69-78). verifyMfaAndIssueTokens does not touch failedAttempts/lockedUntil on a wrong code; it just throws mfa_invalid_code (auth.service.ts:133). The only throttle on /auth/mfa/verify is authLimiter (20/15min) keyed per-IP (rate-limit.ts:3-8). otplib verifySync runs with epochTolerance default 0 (functional.js: epochTolerance:t.epochTolerance??0), i.e. a single 30s window, ~1e6 code space. With a valid 5-min mfaPendingToken, a distributed attacker spreading across IPs can attempt many codes per window with no per-account ceiling.
recommendation: Apply the same per-account failed-attempt counter/lockout to MFA verification (and to disable-MFA), keyed on the user, so a fixed number of wrong codes locks the second factor independent of source IP.
verifyNote: Verified: verifyMfaAndIssueTokens (apps/api/src/services/auth.service.ts:119-138) never touches failedAttempts/lockedUntil on a wrong code — it just throws mfa_invalid_code (line 133). Lockout is only incremented on bad bcrypt.compare in login() (lines 69-78). /auth/mfa/verify's only throttle is authLimiter (20/15min, per-IP, rate-limit.ts:3-8). otplib verifySync uses epochTolerance??0 (confirmed in node_modules/otplib/dist/functional.js → single 30s window, ~1e6 space). Distributed-IP brute force of TOTP with a 5-min valid mfaPendingToken has no per-account ceiling. High is correct.

title: Web lookup ignores citySlug for area-snap and uses SD-tuned 20km cap, diverging from Railway's per-city 60km snap
evidence: The Railway service added nearestAreaForCity(point, citySlug) which discovers THAT city's areas and caps at 60km (lookup.service.ts:36 `best.km < 60`). The Vercel mirror never adopted this: after a correctly city-scoped Nominatim geocode it calls the bare `nearestArea(geo)` (lookup.ts:119,154,194), which reads the merged global last-known-good list and hard-caps at 20km (packages/crime-data/src/neighborhoods.ts:78 `best.km < 20`). The 20km radius is calibrated for compact San Diego; for sprawling metros (Phoenix, Jacksonville, LA, Houston) a valid geocoded address can sit >20km from every discovered neighborhood centroid and return no_match even though the city is fully supported. The two endpoints back the same UI (web /api/geo/lookup proxies to Railway first via tryProxy, but falls through to this local path on Railway error/timeout — exactly the cold-container case the proxy comment at route.ts:19-24 cites), so the same query yields different results depending on which path serves it.
recommendation: Mirror nearestAreaForCity into the web service: snap against the selected city's discovered areas with the same (or larger) distance cap, and thread citySlug through all three nearestArea call sites. At minimum raise the global nearestArea cap above 20km or make it city-aware so large metros stop spuriously returning no_match on the fallback path.
verifyNote: Reproduced. Railway apps/api/src/services/geo/lookup.service.ts:11-41 defines nearestAreaForCity(point, citySlug) which discovers the selected city's areas and caps at best.km < 60 (line 36), threaded through both geocode call sites (lines 139, 149). The Vercel mirror apps/web/src/server/services/geo/lookup.ts never adopts it: all three snap sites call bare nearestArea(geo) with no citySlug (lines 118, 153, 193). nearestArea (packages/crime-data/src/neighborhoods.ts:70-79, re-exported by the web wrapper) reads the merged global last-known-good list and hard-caps at best.km < 20 (line 78). The route (apps/web/src/app/api/geo/lookup/route.ts:25-26) proxies to Railway first but explicitly falls through to this local path on Railway error/timeout, so a city-supported address >20km from any discovered centroid in a sprawling metro returns no_match on the fallback path while Railway resolves it. Divergence and 20km cap both verified. (Minor: the proxy comment cites a 60s timeout, not 30s as the finding's prose implies — immaterial.) High severity stands.

title: Proximity alert zones silently never fire for cities whose feed has no per-incident coordinates (Phoenix, Boise, Saint Paul, Virginia Beach, and 0/missing-coord incidents in Charlotte/Dallas)
location: apps/api/src/services/safety/proximity.worker.ts:57 (incidentsNearPoint) ; packages/crime-data/src/adapters/phoenix-ckan.ts:296-305 (no lat/lng), boise-arcgis.ts:108, saint-paul-arcgis.ts:145, virginia-beach-arcgis.ts:149 (lat: undefined), charlotte-arcgis.ts:158 / dallas-socrata.ts:163 (lat undefined when source is 0/missing)
evidence: incidentsNearPoint does `if (inc.lat == null || inc.lng == null) continue;` then distance-filters on incident lat/lng. Phoenix incidents are pushed with NO lat/lng fields at all (phoenix-ckan.ts:296 builds the Incident with id/area/occurredAt/nibrsCategory/ibrOffenseDescription/beat/blockLabel only — geography is ZIP-only per the file header). Boise/Saint Paul/Virginia Beach explicitly set `lat: undefined`. Charlotte/Dallas set `lat: undefined` whenever the source coordinate is 0 or missing. For all of these, every incident is dropped by the null guard, so `incidentsNearPoint` returns [] and the worker `continue`s — a user in Phoenix who creates an Alert Zone and enables notifications will NEVER receive a proximity alert, with no UI indication. SavedPlacesPanel.tsx offers the same '🔔 Alerts on' toggle for every city.
recommendation: Detect coordless cities and either (a) fall back to area-level alerting (alert when a NEW incident lands in the place's nearest area/centroid, instead of point-radius filtering) for feeds lacking per-incident coordinates, or (b) surface an honest 'point-level alerts not available for <city> — this feed reports by neighborhood only' note in SavedPlacesPanel so users aren't told alerts will reach them when they cannot.
verifyNote: Verified end-to-end. incidentsNearPoint (proximity.worker.ts:57) drops any incident with null lat/lng before distance-filtering. phoenix-ckan.ts (~295-305) builds the Incident with only id/area/occurredAt/nibrsCategory/ibrOffenseDescription/beat/blockLabel and NO lat/lng (geography is ZIP-only). boise-arcgis.ts:108-109, saint-paul-arcgis.ts:144-145, virginia-beach-arcgis.ts:149-150 all explicitly set lat: undefined / lng: undefined. charlotte-arcgis.ts:158-159 and dallas-socrata.ts:163-164 set lat/lng to undefined whenever the source coordinate is 0 or missing. types.ts:37-38 confirms lat/lng are optional. All four coordless cities (boise=CITIES[23], saint-paul=CITIES[27], phoenix=CITIES[39], virginia-beach=CITIES[41]) are in the registry with bboxes, so users can save places there. SavedPlacesPanel.tsx universally offers the alert toggle (line 88 toggleAlerts, 212 'Alerts on') and advertises 'notify you when a new incident is reported nearby' (line ~100). For these cities every incident is dropped, incidentsNearPoint returns [], and the worker continues (line 79) — alerts can never fire, with no UI disclosure. High is correct.

title: Trusted-contact consent gate (permissionAcknowledged) is enforced on the unused Railway API but NOT on the Vercel route the client actually calls
evidence: The Express API rejects adds without affirmative consent: contacts.service.ts:28 `if (!input.permissionAcknowledged) throw new HttpError(400, "permission_acknowledgement_required", ...)` with the comment 'privacy-policy promise'. The Vercel mirror addContact() has no such check (contacts.ts:22 signature is `{label,email,phone}`, no permission field; the route schema NewContact at route.ts:7-11 omits it entirely). With API_BASE defaulting to '' (api-client.ts:7, same-origin Vercel), the client at TrustedContactsManager.tsx:47 POSTs `{label,email,phone}` with no permissionAcknowledged and never collects it. So in production the documented consent requirement is silently not enforced, and confirmation emails get sent to people the adder may not have permission to contact.
recommendation: Enforce permissionAcknowledged in apps/web/src/server/services/contacts.ts and the Vercel NewContact zod schema (z.literal(true)), and add a required acknowledgement checkbox to TrustedContactsManager before POST. Keep the two backends' validation in sync or collapse to one.
verifyNote: Verified end-to-end. Express apps/api/src/services/contacts.service.ts:28-31 enforces `if (!input.permissionAcknowledged) throw HttpError(400, permission_acknowledgement_required)` and sets permissionAcknowledged:true on create (line 42). The Vercel mirror apps/web/src/server/services/contacts.ts:22 has signature {label,email,phone} with NO permission field, NO check, and does not set the column on create (lines 28-37). The Vercel route schema apps/web/src/app/api/contacts/route.ts:7-11 (NewContact zod) omits permissionAcknowledged. api-client.ts:7 API_BASE defaults to '' (same-origin Vercel) and TrustedContactsManager.tsx:47-50 POSTs {label, email||null, phone||null} with no consent. schema.prisma:253-257 confirms permissionAcknowledged Boolean @default(false) with a comment 'Privacy policy promised we capture this'. So in production the documented consent gate is silently not enforced and rows persist with permissionAcknowledged=false. High is correct (legal/privacy-policy exposure).

title: AMBER (child-abduction) alerts can lag ~15-20 min behind issuance
evidence: AmberAlertsBanner calls useApi(`/official-alerts?city=...`) with no refreshIntervalMs override, so it uses DEFAULT_REFRESH_MS = 15*60*1000 (api-client.ts:17). The server-side AMBER adapter additionally caches for CACHE_TTL_MS = 5*60*1000 (amber.ts:16). For the single most time-critical surface in the product (an active Child Abduction Emergency), a user with the page already open can wait up to ~15 min for the next client poll, and that poll can itself hit a server cache up to 5 min stale — a combined worst case near 20 min. The banner is styled role=alert/aria-live=assertive implying immediacy the data pipeline does not deliver.
recommendation: Give the AMBER/official-alerts fetch a much shorter refreshIntervalMs (e.g. 60-90s) on the AmberAlertsBanner, and drop the AMBER server cache TTL to ~60s (NWS rate limits tolerate this for a single low-cardinality event query). Optionally fetch AMBER on a separate, faster-polling endpoint than the bundled weather/quake/traffic aggregate.
verifyNote: Reproduced. AmberAlertsBanner.tsx:35 calls useApi(`/official-alerts?city=...`) with no refreshIntervalMs, so useApi falls to DEFAULT_REFRESH_MS=15*60*1000 (api-client.ts:17,303). Server AMBER adapter caches CACHE_TTL_MS=5*60*1000 (amber.ts:16,42). On an already-open page the next client poll is up to ~15min away and may hit a server cache up to 5min stale → combined ~20min worst case. Banner is role=alert/aria-live=assertive (AmberAlertsBanner.tsx:41-42). High is defensible for the most time-critical surface, though tab-focus/city-change refetch and multi-hour AMBER lifetimes partially soften real-world impact.

title: All 8 backdrop photos fetched eagerly per city (~2MB) while only one is visible
evidence: CityBackdrop maps over photos (all 8) rendering each as a next/image fill inside `<div className='absolute inset-0 ...'>` — every image is laid out at full viewport size with only CSS opacity toggling visibility (opacity-100 vs opacity-0). next/image lazy loading (loading=lazy for non-priority) is driven by IntersectionObserver/in-viewport status; an opacity:0 fill element still occupies the full viewport and therefore intersects, so the browser fetches all 8 immediately rather than deferring. Confirmed every city has exactly 8 photos (node scan: {'8': 44}). Live optimizer serves each at WebP ~248KB for w=1920 (curl -H 'Accept: image/webp' /_next/image?...&w=1920&q=75 -> content-type image/webp, content-length 248236). So ~8 x 250KB ≈ 2MB of backdrop imagery is pulled on every city view, of which 7 photos are invisible until the 30s rotation reaches them (a full 8-photo cycle takes 4 minutes). Code comment at line 20 still says 'Each city carries 4 photos' (stale; actually 8), doubling the original footprint.
recommendation: Render only the current photo (idx) plus optionally the next one for a smooth crossfade, mounting the others lazily as the rotation advances; or move non-current images out of the viewport / use a single <Image> swapping src. Also reconsider 8 photos/city at 1920px — 3-4 is ample for a 30s-rotation backdrop. This alone removes ~1.5MB from the initial paint of every page (backdrop is mounted in the root layout, so it affects the public landing LCP too).
verifyNote: Verified: node scan of apps/web/src/lib/city-photos.ts confirms exactly 8 photos for all 44 cities ({"8":44}, 352 total wikimedia URLs). CityBackdrop.tsx:43-64 renders ALL photos as next/image fill inside `absolute inset-0` divs, only toggling CSS opacity (line 46). Non-priority images get native lazy-load, but an opacity:0 fill element still occupies the full viewport and intersects, so the browser fetches all 8. Live optimizer (/_next/image?...w=1920&q=75) returns image/webp at content-length 248236 -> ~8x250KB ≈ 2MB per city view. Comment at line 20 still says 'Each city carries 4 photos' (stale; actually 8). Backdrop is mounted in root layout via CityBackdropLazy so it hits public LCP. High is correct.

title: Dispatcher LKG caches are unbounded and invisible to the OOM memory watchdog
location: packages/crime-data/src/dispatcher.ts:62-64 (lkgIncidents/lkgAreaStats/lkgRecentReports); apps/api/src/index.ts:132-163 (watchdog only evicts registered row caches)
evidence: Three module-level Maps `lkgIncidents`, `lkgAreaStats`, `lkgRecentReports` are keyed by AREA slug (not city) and set on every successful fetch (lines 86,93,113,130) with no size cap, TTL, or eviction anywhere in the file. The v97 memory watchdog only calls evictAllRowCaches()/evictColdRowCaches() (index.ts:141,148), which iterate the `entries` Map in cache-registry.ts populated by registerRowCache() — the adapter row caches only. The LKG maps are never registered, so the watchdog cannot reclaim them. Worse, getIncidents returns `filtered.slice(...)` (sf-socrata.ts:179) — a fresh array of references to the SAME Incident objects held in the adapter row cache. So after the watchdog evicts an adapter's row cache, any area whose incidents were stored in lkgIncidents keeps those Incident objects alive via the LKG array's references, partially defeating the eviction that is the documented root-cause OOM guard. Across 44 cities with hundreds of areas each (Detroit 199, Atlanta 244) this is a monotonically-growing heap floor the watchdog's GC cannot lower.
recommendation: Register the LKG maps with the cache-registry (or cap them to an LRU of N most-recent area keys, e.g. via a bounded Map). At minimum, clear/trim them inside evictAllRowCaches's phase-2 fallback so the all-or-nothing OOM guard actually frees everything. Document that LKG arrays pin row objects and ensure eviction nulls both.
verifyNote: Verified dispatcher.ts:62-64 declares lkgIncidents/lkgAreaStats/lkgRecentReports as plain module-level Maps keyed by area slug, set on success at lines 86,93,113,130 with no cap/TTL/eviction. cache-registry.ts only iterates `entries` populated by registerRowCache; the LKG maps are never registered, and evictAllRowCaches/evictColdRowCaches (called by the watchdog at index.ts:141,148) cannot touch them. getIncidents returns the adapter rows by reference (line 112 stores `incidents` directly), so LKG arrays pin those Incident objects past adapter-cache eviction. Real unbounded heap floor invisible to the OOM watchdog. High is correct.

title: Route timeouts abandon but never cancel the heavy compose — timed-out requests keep their compute slot and row arrays
evidence: withScoreTimeout/area-brief/incident-summary all use Promise.race([compose, setTimeout(...)]). On the 45s (safety-score) / 22s (AI) timeout the route returns 503/null, but the underlying compose promise is not aborted — there is no AbortController threaded through getCitywideSafetyScore -> dispatcher -> getIncidents. Because withComputeLimit releases its city slot in the fn's `.finally` (compute-limit.ts:90), a timed-out compose holds its slot (1 of MAX=6 distinct cities) AND its in-flight per-area row arrays until it eventually finishes. Under exactly the burst scenario the gate exists to protect against (many cold cities, slow upstreams), each timed-out request keeps consuming a slot and memory for up to the undici 30s body timeout beyond the route's own deadline, so the gate stays saturated and heap stays high precisely when the client already gave up.
recommendation: Thread an AbortSignal from the route timeout through the dispatcher to the undici fetch so a timed-out request actually cancels its upstream pull and releases its compute slot + buffers immediately. Short of that, prefer the Redis L2 / LKG fast paths before entering the gate so timed-out cold composes don't pin a slot.
verifyNote: Verified withScoreTimeout (safezone.routes.ts:47-52), area-brief (ai.routes.ts:45-52) and incident-summary (ai.routes.ts:101-106) all use Promise.race([compose, setTimeout]) with no AbortController threaded into getCitywideSafetyScore/getSafetyScore/dispatcher/undici. withComputeLimit (compute-limit.ts:88-92) releases the city slot only in fn's .finally, so a timed-out compose keeps its 1-of-6 slot and in-flight row arrays until the upstream completes (up to undici's 30s body timeout, index.ts:94). Saturates the gate precisely under the burst it guards. High is correct.

title: GeoJSON map assets are oversized (full precision + dense vertices); honolulu 2.0MB, SF 1.5MB, raleigh 1.3MB for only 6 polygons
evidence: Coordinates are stored at 12-13 decimal places (nanometer precision — meaningless for neighborhood boundaries): honolulu.geojson precision histogram = {12: 56451, 13: 51244 numbers}. Per-feature bloat is extreme: raleigh.geojson is 1,328KB for only 6 features (~221KB/polygon), san-francisco 1,536KB for 41 features (~38KB each), honolulu 2,064KB for 83 features. CrimeMap.tsx:257 comment calls these 'one of the small GeoJSONs' — they are not. Trimming coordinates to 5 decimal places (~1m accuracy, finer than any neighborhood polygon needs) alone shrinks raleigh 1328KB->750KB, SF 1536KB->911KB, honolulu 2064KB->1230KB (~40%); Douglas-Peucker vertex simplification would cut far more since vertex count dominates raleigh's 221KB/polygon.
recommendation: Run a build-time pass over public/geo/*.geojson: round coordinates to 5 decimal places and apply topology-preserving simplification (mapshaper/topojson or turf simplify at ~1e-4 tolerance). Target <150KB per file. This is a static asset transform with zero runtime risk and cuts map-tab load + Leaflet parse/render time substantially on the worst cities.
verifyNote: Verified directly. File sizes match: honolulu 2,113,937 bytes (2.06MB), san-francisco 1,573,486 (1.5MB), raleigh 1,359,898 (1.33MB); /geo dir totals 19M. Feature counts confirmed: raleigh 6, SF 41, honolulu 83 (~221KB/polygon for raleigh). Coordinate precision histogram for honolulu confirms 12-13 dp dominate (12:56451, 13:51244) — nanometer precision. Re-ran 5-dp rounding myself: raleigh 1328->758KB (43%), SF 1536->921KB (41%), honolulu 2064->1242KB (40%), matching the ~40% claim. CrimeMap.tsx:257 comment 'one of the small GeoJSONs' confirmed (they are not small). High severity appropriate — these are the heaviest static assets and the worst cities ship a 2MB blob on the map tab.

title: Every device silently creates a permanent real User row (anonymous), and these are never purged
evidence: SessionBootstrap mounts at root and calls ensureAnonymousAuth() on first visit, which POSTs /api/auth/anonymous and creates a prisma.user row with email device-<rand>@travelsafe.local (verified live: returned uid cmpx4sp2m..., reused:false, status 201). The purge-accounts cron only deletes rows where deletedAt < cutoff — anonymous device rows never get deletedAt set, so they accumulate unbounded (one per device/browser/cleared-localStorage). No TTL or sweep exists for them.
recommendation: Add a sweep for stale anonymous accounts (e.g. email LIKE 'device-%@travelsafe.local' AND no activity / updatedAt older than N days AND no posts/contacts/timers) to the purge cron, or stop minting a DB row until the device first writes data.
verifyNote: Verified. SessionBootstrap.tsx calls ensureAnonymousAuth() on mount which POSTs /api/auth/anonymous (anonymous/route.ts:29-37) creating a real prisma.user row with email device-<rand>@travelsafe.local, displayName 'Anonymous device'. Live: status 201, reused:false, uid cmpx5h8ui... The purge cron (purge-accounts/route.ts:48-52) only selects where deletedAt < cutoff; anonymous rows never get deletedAt set automatically. Confirmed via grep: no sweep keyed on device-%@travelsafe.local or updatedAt-based staleness exists anywhere in apps/web/src. These rows accumulate unbounded (one per device/browser/cleared-localStorage). Medium is correct (storage/PII-minimization growth, not directly exploitable).

title: Register UI says account creation is removed, but /register endpoints still work; login UI dead-ends through a redirect and has no MFA/error nuance
evidence: register/page.tsx is a static explainer stating 'CommunitySafe doesn't require sign-up... account creation has been removed' yet POST /api/auth/register (apps/web/src/app/api/auth/register/route.ts) and Express POST /auth/register are both live and create accounts. The login page on success does router.push('/threats'), and /threats is a server redirect to /city (threats/page.tsx) — a double-hop dead-end legacy target. The login page also does not surface the 423 account_locked state distinctly (it shows raw err.message), and never handles mfaRequired.
recommendation: Either re-enable a real register UI or fully retire the register endpoints (return 410 like the deprecated community POST). Point login success straight to /city instead of the legacy /threats redirect. Surface the account_locked (423) message explicitly.
verifyNote: Verified. register/page.tsx is a static Server Component explainer ('No account needed... CommunitySafe doesn't require sign-up') yet POST /api/auth/register (register/route.ts) and Express POST /auth/register are both live and functional (register() in services/auth.ts:17-28 creates accounts). login/page.tsx:24 does router.push('/threats'); threats/page.tsx is a server redirect()->'/city' — a real double-hop legacy target. The login page shows raw err.message (line 44) and does not distinctly surface the 423 account_locked state nor handle mfaRequired. All sub-claims verified. Medium appropriate (UX dead-end + half-retired endpoint, no direct exploit). Note: the 'enable register UI or fully retire endpoints' is sound; the register endpoint still enforces min(12), so it's not insecure, just inconsistent with the UI copy.

title: Unsupported city on safety-score returns 500 internal_error instead of a clean 404
evidence: Live test: GET /api/safezone/safety-score?city=not-a-real-city returned HTTP 500 with body {"error":"internal_error"}. The recon/design intent (and the city_not_supported path) expects a 404 for unknown cities. A generic 500 indicates an unhandled throw on the unknown-city path rather than a validated 'city not supported' response, and surfaces as a hard error to clients/monitoring.
recommendation: Validate the city slug up front and return 404 city_not_supported (matching the documented contract) for unknown cities, reserving 500 for genuine internal faults.
verifyNote: Reproduced live: GET /api/safezone/safety-score?city=not-a-real-city returned HTTP 500 {"error":"internal_error"}. Root cause confirmed in packages/crime-data/src/safety-score.ts:881 — computeCitywideSafetyScore throws a plain `new Error('city_not_supported: ...')`, NOT an HttpError. errorResponse (apps/web/src/server/lib/http.ts:17-21) only maps HttpError to its status; a plain Error falls through to the 500 'internal_error' branch. The area path correctly throws HttpError(404) (safety-score.ts:1397). The code comment at line 877-878 explicitly states callers 'can catch and convert to a 404' but no caller does. Raising to medium: a 500 on a routine bad-input path is a real defect that pollutes error monitoring and breaks the documented 404 contract; it is more than cosmetic but not exploitable, so not high.

title: Crime Map per-neighborhood CTAs redirect to citywide page, dropping the area context they promise
evidence: CTAs labeled 'Safety Index for {name}', '30-day timeline for {name}', 'Awareness brief for {name}' link to /safety-score, /trends, /threats. All three are server redirect() stubs to /city (citywide): safety-score/page.tsx, trends/page.tsx, threats/page.tsx each redirect('/city'). Per-area surfaces live under /neighborhood. The code comment (1049-1055) claims Next/Link preserves useArea context, but landing on /city shows the citywide view, not the named neighborhood.
recommendation: Point these CTAs at /neighborhood (and /neighborhood?tab=personal) instead of the legacy redirect stubs.
verifyNote: Verified CrimeMap.tsx:1057-1074: three CTAs labeled 'Safety Index/30-day timeline/Awareness brief for {name}' link to /safety-score, /trends, /threats. Each of those pages (app/(app)/{safety-score,trends,threats}/page.tsx) is a bare server redirect() to /city — the citywide surface, not the per-area /neighborhood page. The 'for {name}' promise is dropped; the named-area drill-down lives at /neighborhood. Medium is correct (misleading nav, not a security issue).

title: Security-audit events mislabeled: logout logged as auth.token.refresh; block/mute logged as moderation.suspend
evidence: auth.routes.ts:97 logout writes `event: 'auth.token.refresh' ... detail: { action: 'logout' }`. moderation.routes.ts:116 the /block handler writes `event: 'moderation.suspend' ... detail: { action: 'block', targetUserId }`. The SecurityEvent enum (lib/audit.ts:8-27) has no auth.logout or moderation.block member, so a real token-refresh is indistinguishable from a logout, and a user-initiated block is indistinguishable from a moderator account suspension at the event level — you must parse free-form detail to tell them apart. This undermines the audit trail the code explicitly cites for DISA STIG AU-2/AU-3.
recommendation: Add 'auth.logout' and 'moderation.block'/'moderation.mute' to the SecurityEvent union and use them, so audit queries by event type are accurate.
verifyNote: Verified: auth.routes.ts:97 logout writes event:'auth.token.refresh', detail:{action:'logout'}. moderation.routes.ts:116 /block writes event:'moderation.suspend', detail:{action:'block',targetUserId}. SecurityEvent union (lib/audit.ts:8-27) has no auth.logout or moderation.block/mute member. Audit queries by event type conflate real token-refresh with logout and user-block with moderator-suspend; cited for DISA STIG AU-2/AU-3. Medium is correct.

title: Community react/comment and moderation block handlers write FK references from req.params/body with no existence check → 500 instead of 404 on bad id
evidence: POST /community/posts/:id/react does `postReaction.upsert({ create: { postId, userId, kind } })` with postId taken directly from req.params.id and no check that the Post exists. POST /community/posts/:id/comment does `postComment.create({ data: { postId: req.params.id, ... } })` likewise. POST /moderation/block upserts userBlock with blockedId = req.body.userId with no check the target user exists. A non-existent id triggers a Prisma foreign-key violation that falls through to errorHandler's generic `500 internal_error` (middleware/error.ts:47) rather than a clean 404/400 — and it logs the FK error as an unhandled error in Sentry, polluting error metrics.
recommendation: Validate post/user existence (or catch the Prisma P2003 FK error) and return 404/400. The codebase already does this consistently elsewhere (crime-data area-stats 404, contacts not_found), so these handlers are the outliers.
verifyNote: Verified: community.routes.ts:101-105 postReaction.upsert and :117-124 postComment.create take postId from req.params.id with no Post existence check; moderation.routes.ts:111-115 userBlock.upsert create:{blockedId:userId} with no target-user check. A nonexistent id yields a Prisma P2003 FK violation; error.ts:16-48 has no P2003 branch so it falls to console.error + captureException(Sentry) + generic 500. Confirms 500-instead-of-404 and Sentry noise. Other handlers (e.g. check-in markSafe 404, live-share 404) do validate, so these are outliers. Medium is correct.

title: Post.deletedAt soft-delete is documented and indexed but never set, and feed queries lack the deletedAt filter
evidence: schema.prisma comments the deletedAt column as 'Adapters that surface posts to users filter where deletedAt IS NULL' and adds a dedicated `@@index([deletedAt])`. But grepping the whole codebase, NO handler ever sets Post.deletedAt (take-down uses status:REJECTED via /moderation/posts/:id/review; admin cleanup uses hard `prisma.post.deleteMany`; only User.deletedAt is ever written). Consequently none of the post-surfacing queries (`GET /community/posts`, `/posts/mine`, `/neighborhood/feed`, moderation queue) include `deletedAt: null` — confirmed via grep returning zero matches. Today this is harmless because no Post row ever has deletedAt set, but it is a latent correctness/privacy gap: the moment any code path sets Post.deletedAt (the documented intent), those rows immediately leak into public feeds because the filter the schema promises does not exist. The index also currently indexes a column that is always NULL (dead index).
recommendation: Either (a) implement the soft-delete write path and add `deletedAt: null` to every Post-reading query (mirror the User client-extension approach by extending the Post model in apps/api/src/lib/prisma.ts), or (b) remove the unused Post.deletedAt column + index and update the schema comment, since take-down is actually implemented via status. Pick one so the documented contract matches reality.
verifyNote: schema.prisma:355-388 confirms Post.deletedAt + the documented 'filter where deletedAt IS NULL' comment + dedicated @@index([deletedAt]).
