Grep across apps/api/src + schema shows Post.deletedAt is NEVER written: the only deletedAt write at auth.service.ts:195 is on User; take-downs go through moderation/queue.service.ts reviewPost() which sets status=REJECTED, not deletedAt. None of the post-surfacing queries include deletedAt:null (community.routes.ts:43/62, neighborhood.routes.ts:40, queue.service.ts:9 all lack it). The User soft-delete client extension in lib/prisma.ts only covers the User model, not Post. So the documented contract is unimplemented and the index currently covers an always-NULL column — a latent correctness/privacy gap. Minor evidence inaccuracy: the finding asserts 'admin cleanup uses hard prisma.post.deleteMany' but I found no post.deleteMany in apps/api/src; this does not affect the core verdict. Medium is appropriate (latent, not currently exploitable).

title: Seed creates a known-credential demo account with no environment guard
evidence: seed.ts upserts `demo@travelsafe.local` with `bcrypt.hash("travelsafe-demo", 10)` and `update: {}`. The only protection is a code comment ('Sample demo user ... Replace before prod.'). There is no `if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production') return` guard and no randomization. prisma.config.ts wires `seed: "tsx prisma/seed.ts"`, so `prisma migrate reset`/`db seed` run against a production DATABASE_URL (which Railway/Vercel inject as a real env var) would plant a login with publicly-known credentials.
recommendation: Guard the demo-user block behind `if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production' && process.env.SEED_DEMO_USER === 'true')`, or generate a random password and log it once. Keep the Area reference-data seed unconditional.
verifyNote: seed.ts:50-66 confirmed: upserts demo@travelsafe.local with bcrypt.hash('travelsafe-demo', 10) and update:{}, guarded only by a comment ('Replace before prod.'). No NODE_ENV/production guard and no randomization. prisma.config.ts wires migrations.seed = 'tsx prisma/seed.ts' and datasource.url reads the real DATABASE_URL (Railway/Vercel inject it), so a prisma migrate reset / db seed against prod would plant a known-credential login. Exploitability is operator-gated (someone must run seed against prod), so medium is reasonable.

title: check-in cron is scheduled daily but its code comment claims 'every minute' — Vercel fallback for safety timers only fires once/day
evidence: vercel.json schedules /api/cron/check-in at "0 0 * * *" (daily midnight UTC). The handler's own comment says: "Vercel Cron hits this every minute (configured in vercel.json)". These directly contradict. The handler fires expired check-in safety timers (triggerExpiry → notifies trusted contacts when a user fails to check in). The real-time driver is the Railway in-process worker (apps/api/src/services/safety/check-in.worker.ts:49, every CHECKIN_WORKER_INTERVAL_SECONDS, min 5s, default ~30s), so this Vercel cron is the redundant/fail-safe path — but as scheduled it only provides safety-timer coverage once per 24h if Railway is down, not the 'every minute' the comment promises. Note: Vercel Hobby-plan crons are capped at once-daily, so per-minute may not even be achievable on the current plan.
recommendation: Decide the intended role. If the Vercel cron is meant to be a real fallback for a life-safety feature, it must run at least hourly (requires a Vercel Pro plan for sub-daily crons) and the comment fixed to match. If it is only a daily backstop, fix the comment to '0 0 * * * (daily)' so the next maintainer doesn't assume minute-level coverage exists.
verifyNote: vercel.json:10 schedules /api/cron/check-in at '0 0 * * *' (daily) while route.ts:8 comment says 'every minute'. Direct contradiction confirmed. Worker is the real driver (check-in.worker.ts:49, setInterval at max(5,CHECKIN_WORKER_INTERVAL_SECONDS)*1000; env.ts:52 default 30s). Vercel cron is the fail-safe but only fires daily, not the 'every minute' the comment promises. Medium severity appropriate for a misdocumented life-safety fallback.

title: PWA manifest advertises "37 US cities" and "FBI Crime Data Explorer 2025" — stale count contradicts the live 44-city fleet
evidence: manifest.json description: 'Neighborhood-level safety awareness across 37 US cities. Drawn from official police open-data feeds and the FBI Crime Data Explorer 2025 national average.' The registry actually has 44 cities (44 `slug:` entries in packages/crime-data/src/cities.ts; project memory confirms the expansion to 44). This description is user-facing in PWA install prompts and app-listing surfaces, and it is a static string not derived from CITIES.length or FBI_DATA_LABEL, so it has drifted. (docs/competitive-moat.md:32,186 and docs/wireframes.md:343 repeat the stale 37 but are internal.)
recommendation: Update manifest.json to '44 US cities' (or generate it via a Next app/manifest.ts route that reads CITIES.length and FBI_DATA_LABEL so it can never drift). Refresh the competitive-moat doc count too.
verifyNote: Verified. manifest.json:4 description literally reads '37 US cities' and 'FBI Crime Data Explorer 2025'. cities.ts contains 44 real city entries (lines 73-431); the raw 'slug:' grep count of 48 includes the interface field at line 64 plus three function params at 491/539/573 — net 44, matching project memory. Static string not derived from CITIES.length, so it has drifted. Medium fair (user-facing in PWA install prompts).

title: AreaInsightsPanel leaks raw area slug into header and AI brief (humanizeArea not applied on insights path)
evidence: getAreaInsights(area) is called with the area SLUG (route resolveArea returns q.neighborhood, the slug). It returns `{ area, ... }` verbatim (insights.ts:88 `area,`) and builds the brief with `buildBrief(area, ...)` (line 92), e.g. `${area} has been close to its baseline this week.`. AreaInsightsPanel then renders the header `12-week trend{data?.area ? ` · ${data.area}` : ""}` (AreaInsightsPanel.tsx:45) and the prose `{data.brief}` (line 61). community/page.tsx:189 passes `neighborhood=${encodeURIComponent(area.slug)}`. Result for a slugged area like `gnv-azalea-trails`: header reads "12-week trend · gnv-azalea-trails" and the brief reads "gnv-azalea-trails has had few or no recorded incidents...". humanizeArea() exists in packages/crime-data/src/cities.ts:573 and would render "Azalea Trails", but it is not called here.
recommendation: In getAreaInsights, resolve a display label (prefer the discovered KnownArea.label, fall back to humanizeArea(slug)) and use it for both the returned `area` field and buildBrief(). Apply in both apps/api/src/services/crime-data/insights.service.ts and apps/web/src/server/services/crime-data/insights.ts so Vercel and Railway agree. Alternatively, have AreaInsightsPanel display the area label it already has from useArea rather than echoing data.area.
verifyNote: Verified end-to-end. community/page.tsx:189 passes neighborhood=${encodeURIComponent(area.slug)} (the slug). insights/route.ts:30 sets area = q.neighborhood ?? q.jurisdiction (slug, no humanization). Both getAreaInsights impls (apps/web/src/server/services/crime-data/insights.ts:87-96 and apps/api/src/services/crime-data/insights.service.ts:87-96) return {area} verbatim and call buildBrief(area,...), which interpolates the raw slug into prose (e.g. insights.ts:45 '${area} has had few or no recorded incidents...'). AreaInsightsPanel.tsx:45 renders '12-week trend · {data.area}' and :61 renders {data.brief}. humanizeArea exists at packages/crime-data/src/cities.ts:573 and strips the gnv- prefix (cities.ts:556) to produce e.g. 'Azalea Trails' but is not invoked on this path. User-facing display defect, not security/data-loss; medium is appropriate.

title: SavedPlacesPanel place rows frequently render an empty grey grade dot for valid, low-incident areas
evidence: PlaceRow fetches /safezone/safety-score and renders summary.grade in the colored chip, falling back to a grey '·' when grade is null. By design the score layer returns null grade for any zero-incident / quiet window (the documented 'all-100 false-positive' guard). So a saved place snapped to a genuinely quiet neighborhood centroid shows a grey '·' with title 'Loading…' indefinitely after load resolves — indistinguishable from a still-loading or errored row (the catch branch also sets grade:null with areaLabel '—'). The user cannot tell 'no recent incidents' from 'failed to score'.
recommendation: Distinguish the three states explicitly: still-loading (skeleton), scored-quiet ('No recent incidents' / neutral badge, not the same '·' as loading), and lookup-failed ('Score unavailable'). At minimum change the resolved-but-null title from 'Loading…' to a truthful label.
verifyNote: Verified SavedPlacesPanel.tsx:181-198 and useSafeZoneData.ts:138-139 zero-count guard returning null grade. PlaceRow renders grey '·' with title 'Loading…' whenever grade is null — both on quiet-scored areas (legit null) and on the catch branch (grade:null). The grade chip and its title are identical across loading/scored-quiet/failed states. Minor nuance: the catch sets areaLabel '—' so the subtitle reads 'Near —', a slight distinguisher, but the chip itself conflates all three. Medium is appropriate.

title: AIAssistant steals keyboard focus to its launcher pill on every page load
evidence: useEffect(()=>{ if(open){...} else { /* Skip on first mount */ launcherRef.current?.focus({preventScroll:true}); } },[open]). The comment claims first-mount is skipped, but there is no first-mount guard (no useRef sentinel). On initial mount `open===false`, so the else-branch runs and calls launcherRef.current.focus() immediately. AIAssistant is rendered in the (app) layout, so on every route load/navigation focus lands on the floating 'Ask CommunitySafe' button instead of the page content. preventScroll:true hides the visual jump but keyboard/screen-reader users are dumped on the assistant pill, and Enter/Space would open the dialog unexpectedly.
recommendation: Add a first-mount ref guard so the focus-restore only fires on an open->closed transition: e.g. `const mounted = useRef(false); useEffect(()=>{ if(!mounted.current){mounted.current=true; return;} ... },[open])`.
verifyNote: Reproduced in AIAssistant.tsx:56-66. `open` initializes to useState(false), and the [open] effect runs on mount: with open===false it takes the else branch and calls launcherRef.current?.focus({preventScroll:true}). The line-60 comment 'Skip on first mount' is aspirational only — there is no mounted-ref sentinel, so nothing actually skips the first run. AIAssistant is mounted in (app)/layout.tsx:101, so every route load/navigation dumps keyboard/SR focus onto the floating launcher pill. Real medium a11y defect; severity correct.

title: Cookie consent cannot be changed where the banner says it can; consent keys absent from Privacy dashboard
evidence: CookieConsentBanner copy: "You can change this anytime in Privacy Settings." But settings/privacy/page.tsx has no cookie-consent control and its LOCAL_KEYS inventory (the documented place to clear stored prefs) does not include `cs.consent.v1` (consent choice) or `cs.age.v1` (COPPA age choice). The page comment explicitly states: "if a new localStorage-backed preference is added elsewhere, add its key here so the user can reset it." Once a user picks Accept/Reject there is no UI path to revisit it, contradicting the banner's promise and CCPA/GDPR expectation of a persistent way to withdraw consent.
recommendation: Add `cs.consent.v1` and `cs.age.v1` to LOCAL_KEYS so they appear in the inventory and are cleared by 'Clear all', and/or add an explicit advertising-consent toggle on the privacy dashboard that re-surfaces the banner.
verifyNote: CookieConsentBanner.tsx:47 copy promises 'You can change this anytime in Privacy Settings' linking /settings/privacy. The privacy page LOCAL_KEYS (page.tsx:11-22) lists only travelsafe.* keys; neither cs.consent.v1 (CookieConsentBanner.tsx:16) nor cs.age.v1 (AgeGate.tsx:17) appears, despite the page comment (lines 9-10) instructing that new localStorage keys be added so they can be reset. No consent toggle exists anywhere on the dashboard. So 'Clear all'/'Clear cached' will not clear the consent or age choice and there is no UI path to revisit consent — contradicting the banner and CCPA/GDPR withdrawal expectations. Confirmed; medium appropriate.

title: Moderation 'Take down' silently fails with no feedback if the API rejects
evidence: async function takeDown(id){ if(!confirm(...)) return; await api(`/moderation/posts/${id}/review`,{...}); await reload(); } — no try/catch. api() throws on non-2xx (api-client.ts:130-135 `if(!res.ok){... throw err}`). If the moderator's session expired or RBAC rejects (403/410), the promise rejects unhandled, the post is NOT taken down, and the moderator sees no error — the post simply remains in the list as if nothing happened.
recommendation: Wrap the call in try/catch and surface a visible error (e.g. set an error state rendered with role=alert), matching the error handling already present elsewhere in the app.
verifyNote: moderation/page.tsx:23-30 takeDown() awaits api(.../review) with no try/catch and no error state. Verified api() throws on non-2xx (api-client.ts:130-135 constructs Error with status and throws). On 403/410/session-expiry the promise rejects unhandled, reload() never runs, the post stays in the list, and the moderator gets no feedback. Confirmed medium.

title: Baton Rouge adapter ROW_LIMIT 5000 may not cover the 180-day window (per-charge rows)
evidence: ROW_LIMIT = 5_000 with windowDays:180 on a per-charge dataset (PROVENANCE notes 'per-charge rows (one incident may produce multiple rows)'). NOLA and Cincinnati adapters use 50_000. If BRPD publishes >5000 charge rows in 180 days, the oldest part of the window is silently truncated, potentially undercounting lower-activity neighborhoods and shrinking effective windowDays in safety-score annualization. Not a naming/map defect, but a coverage-completeness risk specific to BR.
recommendation: Raise BR ROW_LIMIT to match the other adapters (e.g. 50_000) and confirm the 180-day window is fully covered, or verify the feed volume is well under 5000/180d.
verifyNote: Confirmed and arguably under-rated. baton-rouge-socrata.ts:17 ROW_LIMIT=5000 with windowDays:180 (line 80) and order:'report_date DESC' (line 83). Live feed has 15,884 charge rows in the 180-day window, so the DESC pull caps at the most-recent 5000 rows, which only reach back to 2026-04-04 vs the intended start 2025-12-04 — an effective window of ~58 days, not 180 (~31% of intended rows). NOLA (nola-socrata.ts:26) and Cincinnati (cincinnati-socrata.ts:30) both use 50_000; only BR and tiny Cambridge use 5000. Critically, getIncidents (baton-rouge-socrata.ts:163) reads from the 5000-capped cache and merely slices it, so safety-score's per-area call of limit:50000 (safety-score.ts:907) CANNOT recover more than 5000 rows for all of BR combined. The codebase itself documents this exact failure mode (safety-score.ts v59 comment): a 5k cap 'silently truncated Phoenix's biggest villages... collapsed the safety-score's effective window from 365 days to ~70 days... under-stated Phoenix's Part-1 rate by ~13x vs FBI baseline' — which is why the per-area limit was raised to 50k. The BR source-level cap re-introduces that bug. Real, reproducible coverage truncation that the team treats as a significant accuracy defect elsewhere; medium, not low.

title: Charlotte areas are 14 CMPD patrol divisions, not recognizable neighborhoods
evidence: The adapter labels every incident by CMPD_PATROL_DIVISION (line 148: `const div = r.CMPD_PATROL_DIVISION?.trim()`), yielding 14 division names (Airport, Central, Eastway, Freedom, Hickory Grove, Independence, Metro, North, North Tryon, Providence, South, Steele Creek, University City, Westover). The geojson has exactly these 14 division polygons. None are recognizable Charlotte neighborhoods (NoDa, Plaza Midwood, Dilworth, Myers Park, South End, Ballantyne, Uptown). The code comment (lines 10-13) acknowledges CMPD also publishes ~460 NPAs but deems them 'too granular,' so it picks divisions instead. A resident searching 'Plaza Midwood' or 'South End' finds nothing.
recommendation: Either surface a curated set of well-known Charlotte neighborhoods (mapping CMPD NPAs or lat/lng into named neighborhood polygons, the way Dallas geocodes into named polygons) or clearly label these as 'patrol divisions' in the UI area picker (the provenance disclaimer already says 'patrol divisions' but the area picker presents them as neighborhoods).
verifyNote: Reproduced exactly. charlotte-arcgis.ts:148 sets div=CMPD_PATROL_DIVISION; comments lines 10-13 acknowledge ~460 NPAs deemed 'too granular'. charlotte.geojson has exactly 14 polygons named Airport, Central, Eastway, Freedom, Hickory Grove, Independence, Metro, North, North Tryon, Providence, South, Steele Creek, University City, Westover — all CMPD patrol divisions, none recognizable neighborhoods (no NoDa/Plaza Midwood/Dilworth/etc). Provenance disclaimer (lines 80-83) does say 'patrol divisions', but the area picker surfaces them as neighborhoods. Minor inaccuracy: coverage baseline.ts:67 declares charlotte neighborhoodCount=13 (not 14), since the adapter min-count>=3 filter / 'Unknown' bucket can drop one; this does not affect the finding's substance.

title: Baltimore: 32 substring-collision polygon name pairs can mis-bind map stats via the substring fallback
evidence: The polygon→adapter-slug join falls back to substring matching when exact normName match fails: `if (labelNorm.includes(norm) || norm.includes(labelNorm)) { slug = s; break; }`. Baltimore's 278 polygons contain 32 substring pairs, e.g. 'Downtown' ⊂ 'Downtown West', 'Arlington' ⊂ 'East Arlington'/'West Arlington', 'Forest Park' ⊂ 'Central Forest Park'/'Forest Park Golf Course', 'Fells Point' ⊂ 'Upper Fells Point'. If the live feed omits the more-specific neighborhood (e.g. emits 'Downtown' but not 'Downtown West'), the 'Downtown West' polygon's normName ('downtownwest') .includes('downtown') is true, so 'Downtown West' is bound to 'Downtown' stats — painting the wrong incident count on that polygon. Exact match wins first, so this only fires for feed-missing neighborhoods, but Baltimore is the most exposed city for this systemic risk.
recommendation: Make the substring fallback safer: require the shorter token to be a whole-word prefix/suffix (token boundary), or prefer the longest-matching candidate and skip the fallback entirely when an exact-match polygon for the candidate exists elsewhere in the set. At minimum add Baltimore-specific guard aliases like the existing NAME_ALIASES table.
verifyNote: Verified. CrimeMap.tsx:323-327 substring fallback fires only when exact normName match fails (exact wins at line 322). baltimore.geojson has 278 named polygons and exactly 32 ordered (shorter-in-longer) substring relations; cited examples all reproduce: 'Downtown'⊂'Downtown West', 'Arlington'⊂East/West Arlington, 'Forest Park'⊂Central Forest Park/Forest Park Golf Course, 'Fells Point'⊂'Upper Fells Point'. Matching is polygon-name(norm) vs adapter-label(labelNorm); Baltimore adapter labels = feed neighborhood field ≈ polygon names, so the risk is real when the feed omits the more-specific neighborhood. Conditional trigger (feed-missing) keeps it medium, correctly rated.

title: Baltimore has no curated or generated per-neighborhood population (falls back to area/peer share)
evidence: grep for 'balt-' in neighborhood-populations-generated.ts returns 0 keys and the manual POP map has 0 Baltimore entries, despite the baseline declaring 283 Baltimore neighborhoods (apps/web/src/server/services/coverage/baseline.ts:65). With no population, per-area safety scores fall back to polygon-area-share-of-city-pop or peer-share (safety-score.ts:1490-1527). This is a defined honest fallback, but it makes Baltimore's 278 per-neighborhood scores materially less accurate than Minneapolis (which has full 87/87 ACS population coverage). Tiny neighborhoods and large parks get the same peer-share treatment.
recommendation: Run the generated ACS population pipeline (Census Reporter + TigerWeb) for Baltimore's 278 neighborhood polygons, as already done for Minneapolis, so per-100k rates use real denominators rather than area-share approximations.
verifyNote: Verified: grep for 'balt-' in neighborhood-populations-generated.ts returns 0 keys; 0 'baltimore' entries in manual neighborhood-population.ts. baseline.ts:65 declares baltimore neighborhoodCount=283. With no curated pop, safety-score.ts falls through to the polygon-area-share (1508-1518) or peer-share (1519-1527) branch, a defined honest fallback but materially less accurate than Minneapolis's full ACS coverage. Medium severity appropriate.

title: Milwaukee map GeoJSON contains raw-ZIP artifact polygons and excluded-suburb orphans
evidence: The served milwaukee.geojson has 27 polygons; 6 of them are orphans the adapter can never match: three raw-ZIP labels 'Milwaukee 53110', 'Milwaukee 53154', 'Milwaukee 53295' (exactly the 'unrecognizable Milwaukee 53xxx' artifact the v106 fix at lines 190-198 claims to have removed), plus 'West Allis', 'Greenfield', 'Brown Deer' — the suburbs explicitly dropped by NON_MPD_ZIPS (53227/53228/53223) and the cross-municipality note at lines 91-96. These 6 polygons resolve to no adapter slug, so the CrimeMap v82 orphan filter (CrimeMap.tsx:335-349) drops them from render. Net effect: they are dead weight in the shipped asset (and visible artifacts if the v82 filter ever regresses), not a live map break.
recommendation: Regenerate milwaukee.geojson from the 190-neighborhood DCD source restricted to the ~21 neighborhoods the adapter actually emits, or at minimum delete the 3 'Milwaukee 53xxx' raw-ZIP features and the 3 suburb features so the asset only contains in-jurisdiction, recognizable neighborhoods.
verifyNote: Reproduced independently. milwaukee.geojson has exactly 27 features; 6 resolve to no emittable adapter label: 'Milwaukee 53110/53154/53295' (the raw-ZIP artifact the v106 drop at milwaukee-ckan.ts:190-198 removes from aggregation), plus 'West Allis','Greenfield','Brown Deer' (the NON_MPD_ZIPS 53227/53228/53223 dropped per the note at lines 91-96). The CrimeMap v82 orphan filter at CrimeMap.tsx:335-353 (finding cited 335-349) filters render polygons to those resolving to an adapter slug, so these drop in steady state. One nuance: 'Brown Deer' substring-matches the 190-set area 'Brown Deer Park' via the fuzzy match at CrimeMap.tsx:323-327, so on a COLD-cache serve (when /geo/areas returns the 190-entry STATIC_MILWAUKEE_AREAS floor) the Brown Deer polygon could render shaded as 'Brown Deer Park' rather than being dropped. The other 5 always drop. Core conclusion (dead weight in the shipped asset, not a live map break) holds. Medium is appropriate.

title: Milwaukee adapter comment claims polygon-name alignment with a 190-neighborhood dataset, but the served map uses a different 27-feature file
evidence: milwaukee-ckan.ts imports milwaukeePolygons from data/milwaukee-neighborhoods.ts (parsed: 190 entries, all valid centroids, clean DCD names like 'New Coeln') and the comment states names 'match entries in the bundled milwaukee-neighborhoods.ts polygon dataset (190 official MKE DCD neighborhoods)'. But the map the user actually sees (CrimeMap fetches /geo/milwaukee.geojson) is a separate 27-feature file with ZIP-flavored/suburb names. The two artifacts have different provenance and content; the 190-set is used only for centroids (MKE_CENTROID / STATIC_MILWAUKEE_AREAS), the 27-set drives the rendered map. 'Bayside' (ZIP 53217) is an adapter-emittable neighborhood with NO polygon in the served geojson, so it can never render a shape on the map.
recommendation: Make the served geojson the authoritative neighborhood-polygon source and derive both the map and the centroid table from it, or document the intentional split. Add a 'Bayside' polygon (or drop 53217 from ZIP_NEIGHBORHOOD) so every adapter neighborhood has a renderable shape.
verifyNote: Confirmed. milwaukee-ckan.ts:22 imports milwaukeePolygons from data/milwaukee-neighborhoods.ts (parsed: exactly 190 entries, 0 bad centroids, includes 'New Coeln'); it is used only for MKE_CENTROID (line 27-28) and STATIC_MILWAUKEE_AREAS (line 167). The rendered map is the separate 27-feature /geo/milwaukee.geojson with ZIP/suburb names. Divergent provenance/content confirmed. 'Bayside' (53217 in ZIP_NEIGHBORHOOD line 76) is adapter-emittable but has NO polygon in the served geojson — confirmed it can never render a shape. The stale CrimeMap.tsx:337 comment ('Milwaukee: 190 polys / 27 areas') further evidences the conflation. Medium is appropriate.

title: Norfolk collapses every neighborhood's discovered centroid to one hardcoded city point
evidence: Norfolk's feed publishes no per-row lat/lng (only streetno/street), so fetchNorfolk stamps EVERY incident with lat:NORFOLK_CENTROID.lat, lng:NORFOLK_CENTROID.lng, and getDiscoveredAreasNorfolk emits centroid: NORFOLK_CENTROID for all ~50+ areas. Compare buffalo-socrata.ts:133 and kansas-city-socrata.ts:354 which compute real per-area centroids by averaging incident coordinates. Consequence: neighborhoods.ts nearestArea(point) haversine snapping (used by geolocation 'find my area') maps any Norfolk GPS fix to whichever area sorts first, never the actual containing neighborhood; SavedAreasRail/any centroid-distance UI is meaningless for Norfolk. The choropleth crime MAP itself is unaffected (it uses static polygons keyed by name, not centroids).
recommendation: Derive per-area centroids from the norfolk.geojson polygon centroids (polygon name → centroid lookup) and emit those in getDiscoveredAreasNorfolk instead of the single NORFOLK_CENTROID, so geolocation snapping resolves to the correct neighborhood.
verifyNote: norfolk-socrata.ts line 115 defines NORFOLK_CENTROID={lat:36.85,lng:-76.28}. fetchNorfolk stamps lat:NORFOLK_CENTROID.lat / lng:NORFOLK_CENTROID.lng on every incident (lines ~168-169) since the feed selects only streetno/street, no coords. getDiscoveredAreasNorfolk emits centroid:NORFOLK_CENTROID for all areas (~line 220). Verified peers compute real per-area centroids by averaging incident coords: buffalo-socrata.ts:133 and kansas-city-socrata.ts:354 both use {lat:e.latSum/e.count,lng:e.lngSum/e.count}. nearestArea() in neighborhoods.ts:70-78 picks min haversine and only replaces best when km<best.km strictly; with all Norfolk centroids identical, every area ties and the first-iterated one wins regardless of the GPS fix — mechanism confirmed. Map choropleth uses static name-keyed polygons so it's unaffected, as the finding states. Medium is appropriate.

title: Kansas City surfaces ~10+ misspelled neighborhood names directly to users as area labels
location: packages/crime-data/src/data/kansas-city-neighborhoods.ts (Auto-generated from blackmad/neighborhoods); labels assigned via geocodeKansasCity point-in-polygon in kansas-city-socrata.ts:294
evidence: The 145 KC polygon `name` values ARE the user-facing area labels (each incident's area = the polygon name it falls in, kansas-city-socrata.ts:294 area = geocodeKansasCity(...)). The crowd-sourced source carries spelling errors that are shown verbatim in the wheel picker, hotspots, ThreatFeed and AI briefs: "Indipendence Plaza" (Independence), "Faireway Hills" (Fairway), "Northest Industrial District" (Northeast), "East Sqope Highlands" (Swope), "Norble And Gregory Ridge" (Noble), "Washington Weatley" (Wheatley), "Bannister Ares" (Acres), "Bleheim Square" (Blenheim), "South India Mound"/"North India Mound" (Indian Mound). These are real KC neighborhoods with corrupted spellings, not fabricated, but they read as data-quality errors to a Kansas City user.
recommendation: Add a small KC name-correction map (typo → correct spelling) applied at label emit time in the adapter / data file, or hand-fix the worst ~10 names in kansas-city-neighborhoods.ts. These are cosmetic-but-credibility-damaging in a safety product.
verifyNote: All 9 cited misspelled names exist verbatim in data/kansas-city-neighborhoods.ts (Indipendence Plaza, Faireway Hills, Northest Industrial District, East Sqope Highlands, Norble And Gregory Ridge, Washington Weatley, Bannister Ares, Bleheim Square, plus both North India Mound and South India Mound — confirmed both present). kansas-city-socrata.ts:294 sets area = geocodeKansasCity(lng,lat) ?? 'Unmapped', i.e. the polygon name is the user-facing label, so these typos surface verbatim to KC users in pickers/feeds. Confirmed; medium is fair for a credibility defect in a safety product (cosmetic, not data-corruption).

title: All four cities absent from generated ACS population table -> area safety scores use weaker fallback denominators
evidence: GENERATED_NEIGHBORHOOD_POPS contains 29 top-level city keys (baton-rouge, boise, boston, ... washington-dc) but NOT atlanta, indianapolis, raleigh, or tucson. knownNeighborhoodPopulation(citySlug, areaSlug) (neighborhood-population.ts:89) therefore returns null for every area in these four cities, and there are no manual POP overrides for them either (POP map at neighborhood-population.ts:47 only populates San Diego). safety-score.ts then falls back to polygon-area-share or peer-share (cityPop/N), which gives most areas an undifferentiated denominator and reduces per-100k accuracy versus the 29 ACS-backed cities.
recommendation: Run tools/build-neighborhood-populations.mjs for atlanta/indianapolis/raleigh/tucson (their /public/geo polygons exist, which is the spatial-join input the pipeline needs) so per-neighborhood rates use real ACS populations rather than peer-share. At minimum document that these four are explicitly on the polygon-area fallback tier.
verifyNote: Verified GENERATED_NEIGHBORHOOD_POPS has exactly 29 top-level city keys (baton-rouge..washington-dc) and does NOT include atlanta/indianapolis/raleigh/tucson. neighborhood-population.ts:85-93: manual POP map only has 'san-diego', generated lookup returns null for these 4 → knownNeighborhoodPopulation returns null. safety-score.ts:1492-1503 confirms the 3-tier estimator. One nuance the finding slightly overstates: all four cities DO have /public/geo/*.geojson polygons that loadPolygonAreas (polygon-areas.ts:75) reads, so they land on tier-2 polygon-AREA weighting (which differentiates by polygon size), not the worst-case undifferentiated tier-3 peer-share. Accuracy is genuinely weaker than the 29 ACS-backed cities; medium is appropriate.

title: Austin 'neighborhoods' are 10 APD patrol sectors, not recognizable neighborhoods — coarse grain that mislabels itself as neighborhood granularity
recommendation: This is honestly disclosed in the provenance disclaimer, so keep as-is, but consider setting PROVENANCE.granularity to a non-'neighborhood' value (e.g. 'sector') since the 10 'Sector X' areas are city-quadrant patrol zones (e.g. 'Central / Downtown (Sector George)'), not neighborhoods like Hyde Park, Mueller, Zilker, East Austin. Real famous Austin neighborhoods are absent because APD strips lat/lng (verified in adapter comments).
evidence: austin.geojson has exactly 10 features all named 'X (Sector Y)'. Adapter comment (lines 16-18): 'Austin strips coordinates, so TRUE per-incident neighborhood assignment is impossible. We bucket by APD SECTOR.' granularity is nonetheless set to 'neighborhood' (line 182). No Austin neighborhood names (Hyde Park, Mueller, Zilker, Clarksville) exist anywhere in the area set.
verifyNote: Verified. austin-socrata.ts:113-124 SECTOR_NAMES are 10 APD patrol sectors all labeled 'X (Sector Y)'; PROVENANCE.granularity:'neighborhood' at line 182 despite adapter comments (lines 14-18) stating Austin strips coordinates and bucketing is by APD SECTOR. austin.geojson has exactly 10 features, all 'Sector' labels (verbatim match), and grep for Hyde Park/Mueller/Zilker/Clarksville returns zero. The granularity field mislabels patrol sectors as neighborhoods; honestly disclosed in the disclaimer. Medium appropriate.

title: Phoenix crime data is an annual snapshot frozen at 2025-12-31 — ~5 months stale as of 2026-06-02
recommendation: Acceptable since the recency string and disclaimer surface 'Data through Dec 2025 — annual snapshot' verbatim in the UI. Verify the Coverage dashboard does not mark Phoenix 'live' (status.ts baseline fallback can read live off stale data). Re-pull when phoenixopendata publishes the next annual archive.
evidence: PROVENANCE.recency: 'Data through Dec 2025 — annual snapshot (frozen at 2025-12-31, updated about once a year). ZIP-level only.' The adapter keeps only the most-recent full year and the snapshot does not roll. As of today (2026-06-02) the newest possible Phoenix incident is >5 months old — disclosed but materially staler than the live-feed cities.
verifyNote: Verified. WINDOW_START_MS = Date.UTC(2025,0,1) (line 70); filter at line 293 keeps only rows >= 2025-01-01, an annual snapshot that does not roll. PROVENANCE.recency (line 244-245) discloses 'Data through Dec 2025 - annual snapshot (frozen at 2025-12-31...)'. As of 2026-06-02 the newest incident is ~5 months old. Honestly disclosed but materially staler than live-feed cities. Medium is defensible; could be argued low given disclosure, but the staleness is real and worth flagging.

title: Virginia Beach: all 333 areas share one citywide centroid (feed has no coordinates)
evidence: getDiscoveredAreasVirginiaBeach maps every discovered subdivision to `centroid: { ...VB_CENTROID }` where VB_CENTROID = {lat:36.8529, lng:-76.0339} (line 73). The comment at lines 171-173 confirms: 'VB rows carry no coordinates (geometryType None), so we cannot derive per-area centroids... every area centroid falls back to the citywide centroid.' The crime-map polygons render correctly from the static GeoJSON, but every centroid-dependent feature (nearestArea haversine snapping in neighborhoods.ts, HotspotCard 'jump to neighborhood', route/proximity exposure scoring) treats all 333 VB subdivisions as located at a single point downtown.
recommendation: Derive per-area centroids from the GeoJSON polygons (compute polygon centroid once at load) and attach them in the discover function instead of VB_CENTROID, so map-jump/nearest-area/route features position VB neighborhoods correctly. The polygon geometry already exists in apps/web/public/geo/virginia-beach.geojson.
verifyNote: Verified VB_CENTROID={lat:36.8529,lng:-76.0339} at virginia-beach-arcgis.ts:73, assigned to every discovered area as centroid:{...VB_CENTROID} at line 188, with the admitting comment at 171-173 (feed geometryType None, no per-row coords). Independently confirmed the centroid is consumed by real per-area features that thus collapse to one point for all 333 VB areas: route waypoint/from-to construction (apps/web/src/app/(app)/route/page.tsx:161,182-183), proximity haversine in api/safety/by-coordinates/route.ts:107 and api/crime-data/alerts/route.ts:70, and safe-route.ts:239-350. The finding's one secondary example ('HotspotCard jump to neighborhood') is imprecise — HotspotCard.tsx does not use centroid for map-jump — but the load-bearing claim (route/proximity/nearest scoring mislocates every VB area) is correct. Medium severity appropriate (degraded feature accuracy, not data loss).

title: All four cities absent from generated per-neighborhood population table (degrades per-100k area rates)
location: packages/crime-data/src/neighborhood-populations-generated.ts (no jax-/vb-/gnv-/tpa- keys); manual POP map neighborhood-population.ts also has none
evidence: GENERATED_NEIGHBORHOOD_POPS contains 0 entries for prefixes jax-, vb-, gnv-, tpa- (verified by key scan). The manual POP map in neighborhood-population.ts also has no entries for these prefixes (only San Diego is populated). These are the 4 newest cities (registry comments call them the 41st-44th); the population build script (tools/build-neighborhood-populations.mjs) was never re-run after they were added. Per safety-score.ts population hierarchy (lines 1490-1527), area per-100k rates therefore fall back to polygon-area-share or peer-share denominators rather than real ACS population — less accurate per-neighborhood grading for all of Jacksonville (208), VB (333), Gainesville (114), Tampa (148).
recommendation: Re-run tools/build-neighborhood-populations.mjs (Census Reporter B01003 + TigerWeb spatial join) for the four city GeoJSONs to populate GENERATED_NEIGHBORHOOD_POPS, restoring ACS-based per-100k area rates. Citywide FBI baselines exist and are unaffected.
verifyNote: GENERATED_NEIGHBORHOOD_POPS top-level keys = 29 cities (baton-rouge..washington-dc); jacksonville/virginia-beach/gainesville/tampa all absent (0 entries each by citySlug). Manual POP table (neighborhood-population.ts) holds only San Diego-style slugs, 0 for the four cities. knownNeighborhoodPopulation (neighborhood-population.ts:79-94) reads manual then GENERATED; safety-score.ts:1492-1527 confirms the documented hierarchy (Tier1 curated → Tier2 polygon-area-share → Tier3 peer-share). With no curated pop, all four cities fall to Tier 2/3, degrading ACS-based per-100k area rates. tools/build-neighborhood-populations.mjs exists. Severity correct.

title: Session tokens stored in localStorage (XSS-exfiltratable); no HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite cookie option
evidence: Auth tokens are kept in localStorage and attached as a Bearer header; there are no Set-Cookie / HttpOnly / SameSite paths anywhere (grep for httpOnly/Set-Cookie in apps/web/src returned only doc comments). The env.ts comment itself acknowledges "tokens live in localStorage and a leak via XSS ... would otherwise stay valid for a full week" (env.ts:16-18). Combined with finding pentest-authn-2 (no revocation), any XSS yields a 24h-valid, non-revocable bearer token.
recommendation: Either move the session token to an HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite=Strict cookie (and re-enable the CSRF guard accordingly), or keep localStorage but pair it with short access tokens + working tokenVersion revocation so an exfiltrated token has a tightly bounded blast radius.
verifyNote: Verified: tokens read from localStorage.getItem('travelsafe.token') (api-client.ts:19-22, attached as Bearer at line 122) and directly in safety/page.tsx:189. No Set-Cookie/HttpOnly/SameSite path exists in apps/web/src. env.ts:16-18 comment itself acknowledges the XSS-exfiltration risk. Combined with finding-2 (no revocation) the blast radius is a 24h-valid non-revocable bearer token. Medium is correct.

title: Web bcrypt cost (12) and token TTL (24h) diverge from the hardened API values (13 / 15m)
evidence: API: BCRYPT_ROUNDS default 13 (api/src/env.ts:27), access token 15m with 30d refresh + rotation-ready revocation (jwt.ts:17-18). Web: BCRYPT_ROUNDS default 12 (web/env.ts:24), single 24h token, no refresh, no ver. Since registration/login can occur on either surface against the same User table sharing the same JWT_SECRET, password hashes and session strength depend on which backend served the request. The API code comment (api/env.ts:21-26) cites OWASP 2025 guidance for raising to 13; the web path quietly stays at 12.
recommendation: Align BCRYPT_ROUNDS and token lifetime/refresh model across both backends (prefer the stronger API values), or collapse to one backend so there is a single source of truth.
verifyNote: Verified: API BCRYPT_ROUNDS default 13 (apps/api/src/env.ts:27) and 15m access TTL (apps/api/src/lib/jwt.ts:17). Web BCRYPT_ROUNDS default 12 (apps/web/src/server/lib/env.ts:24) and single 24h token (env.ts:23), no refresh, no ver. Both backends write the same User table and share JWT_SECRET, so hash/session strength depends on which backend served the request. Medium is correct.

title: No password-reset / forgot-password or password-change flow exists
location: apps/api/src/routes/auth.routes.ts (no reset/change route); apps/web/src/app/api/auth (only anonymous/register/login/me); grep for forgot/reset/password-change across apps/* found only comments
evidence: There is no endpoint to reset a forgotten password or change an existing one. auth.ts:23 references "/auth/password-change" in a comment but no such route is implemented (grep confirms only comment hits). Account lockout (5 fails / 15 min) with no self-service unlock and no reset path means a user who forgets their password — or is locked out by an attacker spamming wrong passwords against their email — has no recovery other than admin/DB intervention. The lockout is also a targeted DoS primitive: knowing a victim's email, an attacker can keep their account locked indefinitely with 5 bad attempts per 15 minutes.
recommendation: Add an authenticated password-change route (verify current password, then bump tokenVersion) and an email-token-based password reset that also resets failedAttempts/lockedUntil. Consider an unlock-on-reset path so lockout cannot be weaponized into a persistent account-DoS.
verifyNote: Verified: no password reset/change/forgot route on either surface — every grep hit for password-change/reset/forgot is a comment (apps/api/src/middleware/auth.ts:23, auth.routes.ts:104) or generated Prisma code; no route file implements it. Lockout (5 fails/15min, auth.service.ts:47-48 and web auth.ts:37-38) with no self-service unlock or reset is a targeted account-DoS primitive. Minor location nit: the finding cites 'auth.ts:23' for the password-change comment but it is actually apps/api/src/middleware/auth.ts:23; substance holds. Medium is correct.

title: POST /community/posts/:id/comment and /:id/react accept any postId with no post-visibility/existence check (interact with hidden, rejected, or soft-deleted posts)
evidence: Both handlers take postId = req.params.id and write directly: react does prisma.postReaction.upsert({ where:{postId_userId_kind:{postId,userId,kind}}, create:{postId,userId,kind} }) and comment does prisma.postComment.create({ data:{ postId: req.params.id, authorId: req.session!.uid, ... } }). Neither first loads the Post nor checks status === VERIFIED or deletedAt IS NULL. The feed (GET /community/posts) only returns status=VERIFIED, and the schema has Post.status (PENDING/NEEDS_EDITS/REJECTED/VERIFIED) and Post.deletedAt (schema.prisma:347,359). So an authenticated user who knows or guesses a cuid can react to and comment on posts that moderation has hidden/rejected/soft-deleted — content they are never authorized to see — and those reactions/comments increment _count.reactions/_count.comments that the feed renders. A non-existent postId additionally produces an unhandled Prisma P2003 FK error surfaced as a 500 rather than a clean 404.
recommendation: Before the upsert/create, load the post (findFirst where { id: postId, status: VERIFIED, deletedAt: null }) and return 404 if not found, mirroring the feed's own visibility filter. Add a deletedAt IS NULL filter everywhere posts are surfaced. For comments, also consider that approved comments inherit the parent's visibility.
verifyNote: Verified in apps/api/src/routes/community.routes.ts:93-129. react handler (101-105) does prisma.postReaction.upsert and comment handler (117-124) does prisma.postComment.create with postId=req.params.id, neither loads the Post nor filters status===VERIFIED or deletedAt:null. The feed at lines 40-42 returns only status:VERIFIED. Crucially, the prisma.ts soft-delete client extension covers ONLY the User model (query.user.*), NOT Post — so no global filter rescues this. Schema confirms Post.status (PENDING/NEEDS_EDITS/REJECTED/VERIFIED) and Post.deletedAt (schema.prisma:347,359). An authed user with a known/guessed cuid can react/comment on hidden/rejected/soft-deleted posts and inflate _count.reactions/_count.comments rendered by the feed. Medium is correct: requires auth + cuid knowledge, impact is interaction with hidden content + count inflation, not data exfiltration.

title: CSP allows script-src 'unsafe-inline' (no nonce/hash), weakening XSS mitigation
evidence: Observed header: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://pagead2.googlesyndication.com https://googleads.g.doubleclick.net https://www.googletagservices.com https://ep1.adtrafficquality.google https://www.google.com . The presence of 'unsafe-inline' (with no 'nonce-...' or 'strict-dynamic') means any injected inline <script> would execute, so the CSP provides little protection against reflected/stored XSS. The rest of the policy is excellent (default-src 'self', object-src 'none', frame-ancestors 'none', base-uri 'self', form-action 'self', upgrade-insecure-requests).
recommendation: Move to a nonce-based CSP (Next.js supports per-request nonces via middleware) or hash-based allowlisting and drop 'unsafe-inline' from script-src. If the Google Ads/AdSense tags require inline bootstrapping, scope them with hashes/nonces rather than a blanket 'unsafe-inline'. style-src 'unsafe-inline' is lower-risk but ideally also tightened.
verifyNote: Live CSP response header on https://communitysafe.app/ contains: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://pagead2.googlesyndication.com https://googleads.g.doubleclick.net https://www.googletagservices.com https://ep1.adtrafficquality.google https://www.google.com — with NO nonce-, hash, or strict-dynamic. style-src 'unsafe-inline' also present. The rest is strong (default-src 'self', object-src 'none', frame-ancestors 'none', base-uri 'self', form-action 'self', upgrade-insecure-requests). 'unsafe-inline' negates script-src XSS mitigation; medium severity is correct.

title: User-supplied lat/lng accepted unbounded (no range/finite validation) and stored + embedded verbatim into map links sent to trusted contacts
evidence: Both the check-in and SOS bodies validate coordinates only as `lat: z.number().optional()` / `lng: z.number().optional()` — no .min(-90)/.max(90), .min(-180)/.max(180), or finiteness guard. These flow straight into Prisma (lastLat/lastLng) and into outbound notifications: check-in.service.ts:73 builds `https://www.google.com/maps?q=${timer.lastLat},${timer.lastLng}` and sos.ts:49 builds `https://maps.google.com/?q=${opts.lat},${opts.lng}`. sos.ts:48 guards only with `typeof opts.lat === 'number'`, which is true for NaN and Infinity. A client (or a tampered request) can submit garbage or out-of-earth coordinates that are then persisted and mailed to a victim's trusted contacts as a 'where they were' pin, undermining the one signal those contacts rely on in an emergency.
recommendation: Constrain coordinates at the schema boundary: `lat: z.number().finite().min(-90).max(90).optional()`, `lng: z.number().finite().min(-180).max(180).optional()`, in both safety.routes.ts and the Vercel sos/route.ts (and the live-share path if it ever carries coords). Reject or drop coordinates that fail, rather than persisting/forwarding them.
verifyNote: Core defect confirmed, but one piece of the evidence is WRONG. Schemas are exactly lat: z.number().optional()/lng: z.number().optional() with no range/finite guards (apps/api/src/routes/safety.routes.ts:15-16; apps/web/src/app/api/safety/sos/route.ts:8-9), and sinks persist to Prisma lastLat/lastLng and build google maps q= links (check-in.service.ts:23-24,72-73; sos.ts:48-49). HOWEVER the claim that NaN/Infinity pass is incorrect: zod 4.4.3 (verified by running it) rejects NaN/Infinity for z.number(), and JSON bodies cannot even transmit NaN/Infinity (they serialize to null, which fails the type check). The real, reproducible gap is FINITE out-of-range values: lat=999, lng=-200, 1e9 are all ACCEPTED, persisted, and embedded into the map link mailed to trusted contacts. So the substantive vulnerability (out-of-earth coordinates stored + forwarded) holds; the NaN/Infinity rationale does not. Medium severity is appropriate.

title: Citywide disclaimer describes adapter-vs-baseline grading, but the grade is now a static national-anchored constant that ignores the live feed
evidence: rawGrade = fbiBaseline ? gradeCitywideAbsolute(fbiBaseline) : gradeFromNationalDeltas(rows). gradeCitywideAbsolute computes danger = (baseline.violent/national.PERSONS)*0.7 + (baseline.property/national.PROPERTY)*0.3 using ONLY the static CITY_FBI_BASELINES values — it never reads `rows` (the live adapter sample). Yet the disclaimer emitted for the same response (when fbiBaseline is truthy) says: "The grade compares this rate against [city]'s OWN FBI-published rate ... A grade of A means the current adapter sample is >=30% below that baseline; C means within +-20% of it; E means >=60% above (a real spike or adapter over-count)." None of those thresholds (0.7/0.9/1.2/1.6) are what runs; the actual thresholds are danger<=1.15->A, 1.50->B, 2.20->C, 3.05->D else E, and the adapter sample is irrelevant to the letter (it only affects suppression-to-N/A via the divergence guard).
recommendation: Rewrite the fbiBaseline branch of the citywide disclaimer to match gradeCitywideAbsolute: state that the grade compares the city's FBI-published rate to the U.S. national average (violent-weighted 70/30), and remove the false 'current adapter sample is >=30% below that baseline' language. Mirror the BlockScoreWidget's 'How the letter grade works (updated)' note which already describes the correct national-anchored scheme.
verifyNote: Reproduced. safety-score.ts:1150-1152 sets rawGrade = gradeCitywideAbsolute(fbiBaseline) when fbiBaseline is truthy. gradeCitywideAbsolute (:739-748) computes danger = (violent/328)*0.7 + (property/1548)*0.3 from ONLY the static CITY_FBI_BASELINES values (never touches rows), with thresholds A<=1.15, B<=1.50, C<=2.20, D<=3.05, else E. Yet the disclaimer emitted in the same fbiBaseline branch (:1249-1252) literally says 'A grade of A means the current adapter sample is >=30% below that baseline; C means within +-20% of it; E means >=60% above'. Those thresholds (0.7/0.9/1.2/1.6) and the 'adapter sample' framing are false: the letter is national-anchored and ignores rows (rows only affect suppression-to-N/A via the divergence guard at :1168-1228). User-facing accuracy claim is wrong; medium is correct.

title: Per-area Safety Index chip labels say 'national average' but the index is computed relative to the city, contradicting the legend and benchmark line in the same card
evidence: BAND_STYLE labels render on the prominent chip as: safe='Fewer reports than national average', moderate='About the national average', elevated='More reports than national average'. But for per-area mode useSafeZoneData computes the ratio as `r.cityPer100k > 0 ? r.localPer100k / r.cityPer100k : 1` (line 173) — i.e. neighborhood-vs-CITY, not national. The BandLegend directly below correctly says 'Below the city\'s typical baseline', and the benchmark line resolves to the city's own rate for cities with a baseline. So the headline chip text is the only element claiming a national comparison and it is wrong for the neighborhood score (which is always city-relative per the updated-grade note on lines 154-161).
recommendation: Change the per-area BAND_STYLE labels to reference the city baseline (e.g. 'Fewer reports than this city\'s average' / 'About this city\'s average' / 'More reports than this city\'s average'), or pass the anchor through so the chip can say 'national' only in the citywide-no-baseline case. The legend and benchmark text already use the correct framing.
verifyNote: Reproduced. BlockScoreWidget.tsx:26-28 BAND_STYLE.label = 'Fewer/About/More reports than national average' and that label is rendered on the prominent chip (:124) and aria-label (:89). But per-area mode computes the ratio as r.cityPer100k>0 ? r.localPer100k/r.cityPer100k : 1 (useSafeZoneData.ts:173) = neighborhood-vs-CITY, and the per-area headline anchor is `${api.city.label} citywide` (:188). The BandLegend directly below (BlockScoreWidget.tsx:233-235) correctly says 'the city's typical baseline', and the v102 note (:159-160) says 'The Safety Index dial above is always neighborhood-vs-city'. The headline chip is the sole element claiming a national comparison and it contradicts the same card. Medium correct (user-facing accuracy mislabel).

title: Per-area scoring still silently falls back to San Diego for unrecognized area slugs (residual of the v96 citywide-only fix), risking a wrong-city score with HTTP 200
evidence: getSafetyScore() resolves the city via `const city = cityForArea(areaSlug)`. cityForArea matches a hardcoded prefix table and ends with `return CITIES[0];` (San Diego) for ANY slug that matches no prefix (cities.ts:536). The citywide path was hardened in v96 to throw `city_not_supported` via cityBySlug (returns null) -> 404, but the per-area path has no equivalent: a prefix-less or mistyped slug routes to San Diego, and the only protection is the 404 guard at :1396 which fires solely when `areaIncidents.length === 0 && idx < 0`. A bare slug that collides with a San Diego discovered area that has data (e.g. 'downtown') therefore returns a San Diego score with HTTP 200 for what the caller may have meant as another city — the same data-bleed class the v96 note called out for citywide. The front-end always sends prefixed slugs so it is shielded, but direct /safezone/safety-score?area= hits are exposed.
recommendation: In getSafetyScore, detect the fallback (e.g. when cityForArea returns CITIES[0] but the slug carries no SD override and no recognized prefix) and throw a 404 like the citywide path, OR validate that the resolved slug actually belongs to a city whose adapter discovers it before scoring. Reuse the existing 'Unknown area slug' 404 mapping in safezone.routes.ts:122.
verifyNote: Mechanism reproduced. cityForArea (cities.ts:491-537) matches a hardcoded prefix table and ends with `return CITIES[0];` (:536); CITIES[0] is San Diego (confirmed by v99 comment :474 'San Diego is the default city (CITIES[0])'). getSafetyScore resolves city = cityForArea(areaSlug) (safety-score.ts:1272) with no validation that the slug truly belongs to that city. The only protection is the 404 at :1396 which fires solely when areaIncidents.length===0 && idx<0 (:1396). A bare/mistyped slug that collides with a San Diego discovered area carrying data (idx>=0) bypasses the guard and returns an SD score with HTTP 200 — the same data-bleed class the v96 citywide fix (safezone.routes.ts:113-117) was built to stop, but the per-area path has no equivalent throw. Front-end is shielded (always sends prefixed slugs), so this is a direct-API exposure only; medium is defensible though borderline low given limited reach.

title: Incidents arriving during the 6h cooldown are permanently suppressed (baseline advances before the cooldown check)
evidence: The tick advances the dedup baseline unconditionally — `await prisma.savedPlace.update({ ... data: { lastSeenIncidentAt: new Date(newestMs) } });` (line 93) — and ONLY AFTER that checks `if (p.lastAlertAt && now - +new Date(p.lastAlertAt) < COOLDOWN_MS) continue;` (line 96). So any new incident that lands while a place is in its 6h cooldown is marked as 'seen' (baseline moves past it) but never delivered. After the cooldown expires the worker only considers incidents newer than the advanced baseline, so the suppressed incidents are lost forever rather than batched into the next allowed push.
recommendation: Only advance lastSeenIncidentAt for incidents you actually decide to (eventually) alert on. When in cooldown, do NOT move the baseline past un-notified fresh incidents — leave them so the next post-cooldown tick can report them. Alternatively track lastSeenIncidentAt separately from lastNotifiedIncidentAt.
verifyNote: Verified in proximity.worker.ts. fresh is computed at line 89; line 93 advances lastSeenIncidentAt to newestMs UNCONDITIONALLY (whenever fresh.length>0), and only AFTER that line 96 applies the 6h cooldown continue. So incidents arriving during cooldown are marked seen (baseline moved past them) but never pushed; after cooldown expires only incidents newer than the advanced baseline are considered (line 88-89), so the suppressed ones are lost. The code comment at line 95 even acknowledges 'hold the push (but keep the advanced baseline)'. Real defect. Medium is appropriate.

title: New incidents from lagged feeds are missed because dedup keys on occurredAt (crime time), not ingestion time
evidence: Baseline = newest occurredAt; fresh = incidents where `+new Date(i.occurredAt) > baselineMs`. Most adapters lag days-to-weeks (recon: SF recency window 180d, several cities backfill), so a genuinely-new record that the feed publishes today but whose occurredAt is older than the place's current newest occurredAt is never classified as 'fresh' and never alerted. The feature is sold as 'notify you when a new incident is reported nearby' (SavedPlacesPanel.tsx:101) — 'reported' implies ingestion time, but the code keys on event time.
recommendation: Key dedup on a stable per-incident identity not yet seen (e.g. track a set/high-water of incident IDs, or use feed publish/ingest timestamp where the adapter exposes one) rather than max(occurredAt), so a backfilled-but-newly-published incident still triggers.
verifyNote: Verified. Baseline is max(occurredAt) (line 80, 88) and fresh is occurredAt > baselineMs (line 89). The Incident type (types.ts:29-39) has only occurredAt — no ingest/report/publish timestamp exists to key on instead. A record the feed publishes today but whose occurredAt predates the place's current newest occurredAt is never classified fresh, so backfilled-but-newly-published incidents never alert. The UI sells it as 'new incident reported nearby' (reported implies ingest time). Real design limitation; impact depends on backfill frequency. Medium/medium-confidence is fair.

title: Public share endpoint returns the sharer's internal userId (cuid) to any token holder
evidence: resolveSharedView returns `{ expiresAt, userId: link.userId }`. The /share/:token endpoint is fully public (no auth). Anyone with the link (which is also emailed/SMS'd, and may leak via referrer or shared screenshots) obtains a stable internal user identifier. The page only displays expiresAt, so userId serves no UI purpose and is gratuitous data exposure that can be used to correlate/track a user across other endpoints that key on uid.
recommendation: Drop userId from the resolveSharedView response. Return only what the share page renders (expiresAt and, eventually, the shared coordinates). If the sharer's display name is desired, look it up server-side and return only the name.
verifyNote: resolveSharedView returns { expiresAt, userId: link.userId } at apps/web/src/server/services/safety/live-share.ts:102 and identically apps/api/src/services/safety/live-share.service.ts:53. The /api/share/[token] route (apps/web/src/app/api/share/[token]/route.ts:5-7) is fully public (wrap with no requireSession) and returns the raw object. The share page apps/web/src/app/share/[token]/page.tsx only renders expiresAt (line 25); userId is in the ShareView interface (line 6) but never displayed — gratuitous internal cuid exposure to any token holder. Medium is appropriate.

title: Live Share ships no location — the share link only confirms a session exists; feature is non-functional vs its name and emailed promise
evidence: createLiveShare stores only `{userId, token, expiresAt}` — no coordinates are ever captured or updated for a live-share link (CheckInTimer stores lastLat/lng, LiveShareLink does not, schema.prisma:646-657). The share page itself states 'Live coordinate streaming is on the roadmap. For now, this link confirms an active Live Share session.' Yet the email/SMS sent to the recipient says 'your contact is sharing their live location with you' (live-share.ts:59-60). A recipient opening the emergency/share link in a real situation gets no location and an empty page — a meaningful safety-product gap and a misleading message.
recommendation: Either implement coordinate capture/streaming (add lat/lng + updatedAt to LiveShareLink and a POST update endpoint) or change the recipient message and UI to accurately state no location is shared yet. Do not promise 'live location' in emails for a feature that conveys none.
verifyNote: Confirmed. schema.prisma:646-657 LiveShareLink stores only id/userId/token/expiresAt/revokedAt/createdAt — no lat/lng/updatedAt (contrast CheckInTimer:635-636 which has lastLat/lastLng). createLiveShare (live-share.ts:40-42) persists only {userId, token, expiresAt}; no coordinate capture. The recipient email/SMS body (live-share.ts:59-60) says 'your contact is sharing their live location with you', yet the share page (page.tsx:26-30) states 'Live coordinate streaming is on the roadmap. For now, this link confirms an active Live Share session.' Misleading promise + non-functional core feature. Medium is appropriate.

title: getCitywide() recency window uses wall-clock Date.now() with no data-anchoring; undercounts lagged/frozen feeds while safety-score does not
evidence: dispatcher.ts: windowCutoffMs = windowDays!=null ? Date.now()-windowDays*MS_PER_DAY : null, then filters with inWindow(inc.occurredAt). No anchor to newest data row. safety-score.ts:996-997 fixes this for scores: anchorMs = (nowMs-dataLatestMs>14d) ? dataLatestMs : nowMs, comment names Phoenix (no 2026 data) and KC (~54d behind). Today 2026-06-02; phoenix-ckan.ts frozen at 2025-12-31 (~153d lag), so getCitywide(phoenix,{windowDays:30}) returns totalIncidents=0. incident-summary.service.ts:111,117,143 papers over it with windowDays->x2->365 cascade and comments (119-141) that recent windows come back empty for KC/Boston/LA with AI trend 'falling -100%'. Citywide counts/charts/AI text wrong for lagged feeds on sub-365d windows though the grade is correct.
recommendation: Port the v99 anchor into _getCitywide: when nowMs-dataLatest>14d, cut from dataLatest-windowDays instead of Date.now()-windowDays. Removes the brittle 3-step fallback in incident-summary.service.ts.
verifyNote: Reproduced. dispatcher.ts:184 sets windowCutoffMs = Date.now() - windowDays*MS_PER_DAY with no data anchor; inWindow (185-189) filters per-area occurredAt against wall-clock. safety-score.ts:996-997 does the opposite: dataLatestMs = newest row, anchorMs = (nowMs-dataLatestMs>14d)?dataLatestMs:nowMs. phoenix-ckan.ts:15-16 documents the feed FROZEN at 2025-12-31 (today 2026-06-02 = ~153d lag), so a sub-365d citywide window over Phoenix returns 0 while the grade is correct. incident-summary.service.ts (apps/api/.../ai/incident-summary.service.ts) lines 111/117/143 confirm the exact windowDays -> *2 -> 365 fallback cascade, with comments 119-141 noting recent windows come back empty for KC/Boston/LA and trend reads 'falling -100%'. Asymmetry and undercount are real; medium is appropriate (grade unaffected, only citywide counts/charts/AI text on sub-365d windows).

title: DC ArcGIS adapter silently caches partial data on page failure (the exact bug Detroit fixed in v99)
evidence: fetchDC() fires all 15 pages x 2 layers via Promise.all with per-page .catch(()=>[]) (line162), then getRowsDC() caches whenever rows.length>0 (line215). No completeness flag, no per-page retry, fixed PAGE_SIZE=2000 with no exceededTransferLimit check. Transient page errors are swallowed and the deflated result cached for 5min. detroit-arcgis.ts:97-137 documents the identical failure ('12k-instead-of-30k pull deflated Detroit citywide rate ~2x, mis-grading as A') and fixes it with bounded concurrency(4), per-page retry(4), and a complete flag preventing caching partial pulls. DC never got the fix.
recommendation: Apply the Detroit pattern: per-page retry+backoff, bounded concurrency, completeness flag so partial pulls return-but-don't-cache. Optionally honor exceededTransferLimit.
verifyNote: Reproduced. dc-arcgis.ts fetchDC (160-164) fires all PAGES(15) x layers via one Promise.all with per-page .catch(()=>[]) and fixed PAGE_SIZE=2000, no exceededTransferLimit / per-page retry. getRowsDC (210-220) caches whenever rows.length>0 (215) with no completeness flag and serves stale on error (219). detroit-arcgis.ts (97-166,198-222) documents the identical pre-v99 bug ('12k-instead-of-30k pull deflated Detroit citywide rate ~2x, mis-grading as A') and fixes it with per-page retry x4 backoff (105-119), bounded concurrency 4 (129), and a complete flag that only overwrites cache when complete||!cache (210). DC never received this fix. Medium correct.

title: NWS, AMBER, and USGS web adapters have no per-request fetch timeout
location: apps/web/src/server/services/official-alerts/nws.ts:55, amber.ts:47, usgs.ts:68 (vs state-traffic.ts:175/238 and chp.ts:173 which use AbortSignal.timeout)
evidence: The traffic adapters guard every fetch with signal: AbortSignal.timeout(FETCH_TIMEOUT_MS) / 8_000, but getNwsAlerts, getAmberAlerts, and getUsgsEarthquakes call fetch(...) with no signal. The aggregator route awaits Promise.all([nws, usgs, amber, traffic]) (route.ts:31-36) and wrap() (server/lib/http.ts:25) adds no outer timeout. A slow/hung api.weather.gov or earthquake.usgs.gov connection therefore stalls the entire official-alerts response (and the Weather + AMBER + Traffic cards that all share it) until Node's default socket timeout, instead of degrading to the documented per-source silent fallback.
recommendation: Add signal: AbortSignal.timeout(8_000) to the fetch calls in nws.ts, amber.ts, and usgs.ts, matching the traffic adapters, so a hung upstream falls into the existing catch -> stale/[] path quickly.
verifyNote: Reproduced. grep confirms NO signal/AbortSignal in web nws.ts, amber.ts, usgs.ts fetch calls, while state-traffic.ts (175,238,874,930) and chp.ts (173) all use AbortSignal.timeout(FETCH_TIMEOUT_MS=8_000). Route awaits Promise.all([nws,usgs,amber,traffic]) (route.ts:31-36) and wrap() (http.ts:25-33) adds no outer timeout, so a hung api.weather.gov / earthquake.usgs.gov stalls the whole aggregate response. Medium is correct.

title: NWS alert-to-city matching uses fragile city-name substring against county/zone areaDesc
evidence: Filtering keeps an alert if area.includes(cityNeedle) || area.split(";").length > 5, where cityNeedle is the city LABEL (e.g. 'cambridge', 'new york city', 'fort worth') and areaDesc is NWS's semicolon-joined county/forecast-zone list. NWS areaDesc is county/zone-based, not city-based, so many cities never substring-match their county (Cambridge MA lives in 'Middlesex'; Fort Worth in 'Tarrant'; Oakland in 'Alameda'), and the '.split(";").length > 5' branch is a crude proxy for 'statewide' that will both miss small multi-county alerts (<=5 zones) covering the city and admit large alerts that don't actually touch the city. Net effect: the Weather card can under-report real local warnings or show irrelevant far-away ones, with no county/zone mapping behind the label.
recommendation: Map each city to its NWS county/forecast zone(s) (or use the NWS points->zone API once per centroid) and filter by zone/UGC code instead of substring-matching the city label against areaDesc. At minimum, match on county name rather than city name.
verifyNote: Reproduced. nws.ts:65-72 filters area.includes(cityNeedle) || area.split(';').length>5 where cityNeedle = city LABEL. Confirmed via city-meta.ts that labels are city names (Cambridge, Fort Worth, Oakland), which do not substring-match their county-based NWS areaDesc (Middlesex/Tarrant/Alameda). The >5 branch is a crude statewide proxy. Under/over-reporting risk is real. Medium correct.

title: Fallback message falsely claims 4 states have no public feed when they have feeds gated behind undocumented env keys
evidence: GA/VA/OH/CO are registered only when GA_511_KEY / VDOT_API_KEY / OHGO_API_KEY / COTRIP_API_KEY are set (keyedEntry returns null otherwise, so trafficAgencyForState→null). None of these four env vars appear in apps/web/.env.example or /.env.example (grep for '511|ohgo|cotrip|vdot|dot' returns nothing), so an operator has no signal they exist and they are almost certainly unset in production. With them unset, Atlanta (GA), Virginia Beach + Norfolk (VA), Cincinnati + Cleveland (OH), and Colorado Springs + Denver (CO) — 7 served cities — render the panel's honest-fallback copy: "<city>'s state highway patrol doesn't publish a free public road-conditions feed yet, so live traffic incidents aren't available here." That statement is factually wrong: GA (511GA), VA (511 Virginia), OH (OHGO), and CO (CoTrip WZDx) all publish feeds; they just require a key. The code's own comments (lines 1213-1216) acknowledge these are 'env-key-gated', contradicting the user-facing 'doesn't publish a free public feed' claim.
recommendation: Either (a) document the four keys in .env.example and obtain them so the states go live, or (b) soften the fallback copy to 'live traffic incidents aren't available here yet' without asserting the state publishes no feed. Given the project's repeated honesty posture in MEMORY, the false 'no feed exists' claim should be corrected regardless.
verifyNote: Verified independently. keyedEntry() (state-traffic.ts:1161-1169) returns null when the env var is absent, so GA/VA/OH/CO are dropped from REGISTRY (loop 1197-1208) and trafficAgencyForState returns null. Grep of both .env.example files (root and apps/web) for 511/ohgo/cotrip/vdot/GA_511_KEY/VDOT_API_KEY/OHGO_API_KEY/COTRIP_API_KEY returns ZERO matches, so the keys are undocumented and almost certainly unset in prod. With them unset, the 7 served cities (atlanta=GA; virginia-beach,norfolk=VA; cincinnati,cleveland=OH; colorado-springs,denver=CO per city-meta.ts) hit the fallback copy at TrafficAlertsPanel.tsx:82-88 asserting the state 'doesn't publish a free public road-conditions feed yet.' The code's own comments (lines 893-907, 947-953, 1071-1076, 1213-1216) document that 511GA/511 Virginia/OHGO/CoTrip DO publish feeds and are merely key-gated, directly contradicting the user-facing claim. Honesty defect, medium is correctly rated.

title: Traffic alerts with missing/unparseable timestamps default to now() and float to the top of the merged official-alerts list
evidence: toIso() returns `new Date().toISOString()` (i.e. now) whenever a feed timestamp is null, empty, or unparseable. WZDx alerts derive `effective` from `core.update_date` (line 781) and several ArcGIS configs from optional fields; any row missing that field gets stamped 'now'. The aggregator route then sorts the whole merged list (NWS + USGS + AMBER + traffic) strictly by `+new Date(b.effective) - +new Date(a.effective)` (route.ts:42). Result: a work-zone or closure with no real timestamp is treated as the freshest event and outranks genuinely recent weather/earthquake alerts in the combined feed, and the panel prints a misleading 'reported <current time>' (TrafficAlertsPanel.tsx:75). This silently misrepresents data freshness — the same class of bug MEMORY notes were fixed elsewhere (date-only cities).
recommendation: When a feed timestamp is absent/unparseable, emit a null/sentinel `effective` and either omit the 'reported' line or label it 'time unknown', and sort such rows to the bottom rather than treating them as now. At minimum, don't let synthetic now() timestamps win the cross-source freshness sort.
verifyNote: Verified. toIso() (state-traffic.ts:106,115) returns new Date().toISOString() for null/empty/unparseable input. WZDx alerts derive effective from toIso(core.update_date) (line 780); many other configs feed optional fields (e.g. MD toIso(props.Updated ?? props.Created), etc.). route.ts:41-42 sorts the whole merged NWS+USGS+AMBER+traffic list strictly by +new Date(b.effective)-+new Date(a.effective), so any synthetic now()-stamped row floats to the top and outranks genuinely recent weather/quake alerts. TrafficAlertsPanel.tsx:75 then prints 'reported <now>'. Real code defect misrepresenting freshness; medium appropriate.

title: Florida traffic uses only layer 7 ('Other Incidents') for all 4 FL cities; planned closures on sibling layers are not queried
evidence: FL_CONFIG targets FeatureServer/7 only ('Other Incidents'), and FL serves four cities (Jacksonville, Gainesville, Tampa, Miami). The Road_Closures FeatureServer is a multi-layer service (the DC adapter, by contrast, deliberately queries+merges three sibling layers via multiArcgisEntry to avoid missing closures). If FL's road-closure rows live on a different layer index than 7, FL cities will under-report planned/full closures relative to DC's modeled-correct multi-layer approach. The comment only documents layer 7's fields, not whether other layers carry closures.
recommendation: Verify whether FL closures live on a separate layer of the Road_Closures service; if so, use multiArcgisEntry to merge them as DC does. If layer 7 genuinely carries all incident+closure rows, add a comment stating the other layers were checked and are redundant.
verifyNote: Confirmed and STRONGER than the auditor's low-confidence guess. FL_CONFIG (state-traffic.ts:371-373) queries only FeatureServer/7. I queried the live service metadata: Road_Closures/FeatureServer has 8 layers (0 FHP-Closures, 1 FHP-Crashes, 2 FHP-Brush Fires, 3 FHP-All Other Incidents, 4 FL511-Crashes, 5 FL511-Congestion, 6 FL511-Planned Construction, 7 FL511-Other Incidents). Live counts right now: layer1=50, layer4=4, layer6=59, layer7(queried)=25. So all 4 FL cities (jacksonville, gainesville, tampa, miami) are currently MISSING ~50 FHP crashes and ~59 planned-construction/closure rows that live on dedicated sibling layers, while DC by contrast deliberately merges its 3 sibling layers via multiArcgisEntry (731-744). Crashes are the most safety-relevant content; under-reporting them across 4 cities is a real defect. Raising severity from low to medium.

title: Large static GeoJSON polygon files served with no cache TTL (max-age=0), re-downloaded per map view
location: apps/web/public/geo/*.geojson (honolulu 2.06MB, san-francisco 1.5MB, indianapolis 1.4MB, raleigh 1.3MB...); fetched in apps/web/src/app/(app)/map/CrimeMap.tsx:274; cache claim in comment at CrimeMap.tsx:257-261
evidence: CrimeMap fetches `/geo/${slug}.geojson` into a per-session module POLYGON_CACHE, with a comment stating 'Vercel serves these as immutable CDN assets, so a memory cache is purely an arrived-once-stays-fast win.' Live headers contradict this: `curl -D - https://communitysafe.app/geo/honolulu.geojson` returns `cache-control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate` (vs `_next/static` chunks which correctly return `public,max-age=31536000,immutable`). Compressed transfer for honolulu is 711,797 bytes (raw 2,113,937). The module cache only lives for one page session; a new session, hard reload, or CDN-cold edge re-pays the full download. City polygons never change, so they are safe to cache long-term.
recommendation: Add a headers() rule in next.config.ts for `source: '/geo/:path*'` setting `Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` (the polygons are versioned with the deploy and never mutate). Optionally pre-simplify the heaviest GeoJSON rings (Honolulu/SF/Indianapolis) with mapshaper/topojson and trim coordinate precision to ~5 decimals to cut raw size 50-80%. Also fix the inaccurate 'immutable CDN assets' comment.
verifyNote: Verified live: curl -I https://communitysafe.app/geo/honolulu.geojson returns 'cache-control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate', content-length 2113937 raw (711797 with br). By contrast a real _next/static chunk (webpack-a3cb1e282785df23.js) returns 'public,max-age=31536000,immutable'. CrimeMap.tsx:257-261 comment claims 'Vercel serves these as immutable CDN assets' which is false. next.config.ts headers() only sets a /:path* security-header block — no /geo cache rule. Polygons are deploy-versioned and immutable, so long-term caching is safe. Medium is correct.

title: Single-area getSafetyScore is not deduped and runs a full city-wide fan-out per request
evidence: getCitywideSafetyScore wraps its body in dedupe(...) + withComputeLimit(...) (line 866). getSafetyScore (single area, line 1271) has neither wrapper at the top — it calls city.discover() then Promise.all over EVERY area with limit:50000 (lines 1292-1300) to compute the citywide peer-average denominator. The per-area getIncidents calls transitively acquire the city's gate slot (so it is throttled), but with no dedupe, N concurrent per-area /safety-score?area= requests for the same city each independently re-run the entire city-wide aggregation (parse + filter all areas' rows, the tsPerArea allocation at 1320-1327, the city totals loop at 1355-1370) rather than collapsing onto one promise. The per-area path is the documented hottest surface (BlockScoreWidget on /city and /neighborhood). The 50000 per-area limit is also the largest of any composer (citywide uses MAX_SAFE_INTEGER, trend/insights 5000, getCitywideAreaStats 1000) — inconsistent and the heaviest allocation per fan-out.
recommendation: Wrap getSafetyScore in dedupe keyed by area slug (the city-wide aggregate it computes is identical for every area in the city within the TTL, so consider deduping on city slug and reusing the shared aggregate). Reconcile the per-area incident limit with the other composers (5000 is already 'far more than 30 days for any neighborhood' per the trend-feed comment).
verifyNote: Verified getSafetyScore (safety-score.ts:1271) has no dedupe/withComputeLimit at the top, unlike getCitywideSafetyScore (line 866: dedupe+withComputeLimit). It runs city.discover() + Promise.all over every area at limit:50000 (1298-1300) plus the tsPerArea allocation (1320-1327) and city-totals loop (1355-1370); the per-area getIncidents transitively acquire the city gate but with no dedupe, N concurrent same-city per-area requests each re-run the full aggregation. One inaccuracy: the finding says 'citywide uses MAX_SAFE_INTEGER' vs per-area 50000 being 'largest of any composer' — getCitywideSafetyScore actually also uses 50000 (line 909) and it is getCitywideAreaStats (dispatcher.ts:205) that uses MAX_SAFE_INTEGER, so 50000 is NOT the largest. The substantive defect (missing dedupe, full per-area fan-out, inconsistent limits) is real. Medium is correct.

title: Trend payload 742KB->22KB fix is opt-in only; default and primary UI callers still serve the full ~760KB list
evidence: The bullets/bulletLimit parameter caps the dispatch list, but when omitted it defaults to DISPATCH_CAP=5000 (trend-feed.ts:262 `opts?.bulletLimit ?? DISPATCH_CAP`), and the route comment states 'Omitted = full list (back-compat)' (route.ts:23-24). So the 742KB->22KB win only materializes for callers that explicitly pass bullets=0 (the DataFreshnessBanner). The full ThreatFeed/TrendPanel renders still request the uncapped list, so the large payload is mitigated on the wire only by gzip/compression (index.ts:202) — the server still materializes and serializes up to 5000 bullet objects in memory per request. For a 30-day citywide window on a busy city this is the dominant per-request allocation on the trend path.
recommendation: Lower the default bulletLimit to the UI's actual visible cap (the ThreatFeed budget, ~12-50) and have the few callers that truly need the full list opt IN, inverting the back-compat default. This bounds both payload and server-side memory by default rather than relying on every caller to remember bullets=0.
verifyNote: Verified trend-feed.ts:15 DISPATCH_CAP=5000 and line 262 `Math.min(DISPATCH_CAP, opts?.bulletLimit ?? DISPATCH_CAP)` so omitted bulletLimit defaults to 5000. Route safezone.routes.ts:145-146 passes `bulletLimit: bullets` (undefined when the bullets query param is omitted), and the TrendQuery comment (134-136) confirms bullets is optional. So the trim only materializes when a caller explicitly sends bullets=0; default/full callers still materialize+serialize up to 5000 bullet objects, mitigated on the wire only by compression (index.ts:202). Per-area fetch is limit:5000 (line 159). Medium is correct.
