title: Web coverage probe fans Promise.all across all 44 cities, throttled to 6 by the shared gate, with stacked 10s+8s+8s per-city timeouts on a 60s function
location: apps/web/src/server/services/coverage/status.ts:89-120 (Promise.all over CITIES, sequential 10s discover + 8s getAreaStats + 8s getIncidents)
evidence: getCoverage() does Promise.all(CITIES.map(...)) — 44 concurrent city probes. Each probe: withTimeout(discover, 10s) then, sequentially, withTimeout(getAreaStats, 8s) and withTimeout(getIncidents limit:50, 8s) — up to 26s wall per city. The getAreaStats/getIncidents calls go through the SAME @travelsafe/crime-data/dispatcher gate (web index.ts:13), default MAX=6 distinct cities, so 38 cities queue. On a cold Vercel Lambda the worst-case is 6 cities draining their ~26s chains while 38 wait, which can approach the route's maxDuration. The v106 caps (comment lines 59-64) target the discover+sample sum, but the design still relies on the LKG/static-baseline fallbacks (lines 136-167) to keep the dashboard 'live' rather than on the probe completing — i.e. cold coverage is effectively served from baseline, not live data, by design. The withTimeout resolves a fallback but (like perf-compute-2) does not cancel the underlying gated compose, so abandoned probes keep their gate slot.
recommendation: Run the coverage probe with explicit bounded concurrency (e.g. p-limit 6, matching the gate) instead of an unbounded Promise.all so the timeout math is deterministic, and skip the getIncidents freshness sample on cold probes (it is the heaviest call and its result is discarded on timeout). Consider a dedicated lightweight discover-only probe for the health view.
verifyNote: Verified status.ts:89 Promise.all(CITIES.map(...)) — unbounded over all 44 cities. Each probe: withTimeout(discover,10s) (PER_CITY_TIMEOUT_MS=65) then sequentially withTimeout(getAreaStats,8s) and withTimeout(getIncidents limit:50,8s) (SAMPLE_TIMEOUT_MS=68, lines 110/112). getAreaStats/getIncidents resolve through `../crime-data` which re-exports @travelsafe/crime-data/dispatcher (web crime-data/index.ts:13), the SAME per-city gate (MAX default 6), so 38 cities queue. withTimeout (77-85) resolves a fallback without cancelling the gated compose, so abandoned probes hold their slot (same root cause as perf-compute-2). LKG/static-baseline fallbacks (136-167) mean cold coverage is served from baseline by design. Medium is appropriate for a health-view (non-user-facing-critical) path.

title: Static /geo/*.geojson served with no long-lived Cache-Control — large files re-downloaded on every cold map view
evidence: next.config.ts headers() returns a single rule { source: '/:path*', headers: SECURITY_HEADERS } with no Cache-Control entry for /geo. Next.js serves files under public/ with default Cache-Control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate — they are NOT treated as immutable hashed build assets. CrimeMap.tsx:260 explicitly assumes the opposite ('Vercel serves these as immutable CDN assets, so a memory cache is purely an arrived-once-stays-fast win'). The module-level POLYGON_CACHE (CrimeMap.tsx:17) only helps within a single page session; a fresh visit, hard reload, or new device pays the full multi-MB download (up to 2MB for honolulu) and the CDN cannot retain it as immutable.
recommendation: Add a headers() rule for source '/geo/:slug.geojson' with Cache-Control: public, max-age=86400, s-maxage=604800, stale-while-revalidate, immutable (or version the filenames). Pairs with perf-geo-1 to make the map tab cheap on repeat/cold loads.
verifyNote: Verified. next.config.ts:122-126 headers() returns only { source: '/:path*', headers: SECURITY_HEADERS } with no Cache-Control. SECURITY_HEADERS (line 92) contains no Cache-Control. Live curl of https://communitysafe.app/geo/honolulu.geojson returns 'cache-control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate' on a 2,113,937-byte body — exactly the default Next.js public/ behavior, not immutable. CrimeMap.tsx:260 comment claims 'Vercel serves these as immutable CDN assets' which is false. One nuance: the live response shows x-vercel-cache: HIT (age 676), so Vercel's edge does retain it server-side, softening the cost vs the finding's wording; but the browser-side max-age=0/must-revalidate means every cold browser/new-device load pays a revalidation round trip (304 best case, full 2MB on miss). Core claim holds; medium is fair.

title: SavedPlace has no index on alertsEnabled; proximity worker full-scans the table every 5 minutes
evidence: proximity.worker.ts runs every TICK_MS = 5*60*1000 (proximity.worker.ts:18,131) and issues prisma.savedPlace.findMany({ where: { alertsEnabled: true }, take: 500 }). The SavedPlace model defines only @@index([userId]) — there is no index on alertsEnabled, so Postgres does a sequential scan of the whole SavedPlace table on every tick. Harmless today at low row counts but degrades linearly with adoption and runs unconditionally forever.
recommendation: Add @@index([alertsEnabled]) (or a partial index WHERE alertsEnabled) to the SavedPlace model and migrate. Low effort, removes a recurring full-table scan.
verifyNote: Verified. schema.prisma:171-185 SavedPlace defines only @@index([userId]) (line 184); no index on alertsEnabled. proximity.worker.ts:18 TICK_MS = 5*60*1000, tick() at :70-74 runs prisma.savedPlace.findMany({ where: { alertsEnabled: true }, ..., take: MAX_PLACES_PER_TICK=500 }) every 5 minutes. Without an index Postgres seq-scans to find alertsEnabled=true rows on every tick. The take:500 bounds the returned/processed set but not the scan. Benign at low row counts, degrades linearly. Medium appropriate.

title: Sacramento coverage baseline (10) is an order of magnitude below real coverage (~100+ of 117 polygons)
evidence: COVERAGE_BASELINE['sacramento'] = { neighborhoodCount: 10 }. That figure predates the v99 adapter fix documented in sacramento-arcgis.ts:204-222, which explicitly notes the old bug surfaced 'only ~10 neighborhoods' and the fix now lands each incident on its first real association. sacramento.geojson contains 117 named neighborhood-association polygons and discovery now admits areas at count>=1 (sacramento-arcgis.ts:268), so live discovery yields ~100+. baseline.ts:23-24 says the number 'just needs to be in the right order of magnitude'; 10 vs ~100+ is a full order of magnitude off, so a cold-lambda /api/coverage answer under-reports Sacramento by ~10x.
recommendation: Refresh COVERAGE_BASELINE['sacramento'] to the post-v99 live count (run /api/coverage?cb=$(date +%s) and capture the real discovered count, ~100-117) so the cold-start fallback is in the correct order of magnitude.
verifyNote: Partially verifiable. Confirmed: baseline.ts:38 sacramento=10; v99 fix comment (sacramento-arcgis.ts:204-222) attributes the ~10-neighborhood symptom to the old split(';')[0] bug and now keeps the first surviving cleaned association per incident; getDiscoveredAreasSacramento filters count>=1 (line 268); sacramento.geojson has 117 polygons. Code reasoning that live discovery SHOULD exceed 10 is plausible. BUT I could not reproduce the '~100+' magnitude: live /api/coverage returns sacramento count=10 with newestIncidentAt:null (static-baseline fallback — prod is NOT running live discovery), and /api/geo/areas returned only 7 areas total (cold). The specific ~100-117 figure is unverified, and since prod serves the baseline anyway the practical user-facing count is 10 regardless. Staleness plausible but unproven — uncertain. Medium severity acceptable if confirmed.

title: Inconsistent password-length policy across surfaces (login min 8 vs register/Express min 12)
evidence: Web login Zod schema is z.string().min(8).max(200); web register and Express both enforce min(12) citing 'DISA STIG IA-5 / FedRAMP baseline'. The min(8) on login is harmless for length validation per se but signals the two stacks were maintained independently and the documented 12-char policy isn't uniformly applied.
recommendation: Align the login validator to min(12) (or just min(1) since login validates against the stored hash anyway) to remove the inconsistency and the false impression that an 8-char password is acceptable.
verifyNote: Verified. Web login schema apps/web/src/app/api/auth/login/route.ts:8 is z.string().min(8).max(200). Web register route.ts:9 and Express auth.routes.ts:15 both enforce min(12) citing 'DISA STIG IA-5 / FedRAMP baseline'. The min(8) on login is harmless functionally (login compares against the stored hash) but reflects independent maintenance of the two stacks and an inconsistent documented policy. Low is correct.

title: Express verifySession only enforces token type when typ is present (permissive); web verifySession enforces no typ at all
location: apps/api/src/lib/jwt.ts:76-77 (if (opts?.expectType && p.typ && p.typ !== ...)); apps/web/src/server/lib/jwt.ts:30-40 (no typ field)
evidence: Express verifySession passes the type check if p.typ is absent: `if (opts?.expectType && p.typ && p.typ !== opts.expectType)`. Today all minted tokens carry typ so this isn't exploitable, but it is a fail-open guard — a future token-minting path that omits typ would be accepted on any expectType route. The web JWT has no typ concept at all (single 24h session token, no access/refresh split), so there's no separation between token classes on the canonical surface.
recommendation: Change the Express guard to fail closed when expectType is set (require p.typ === opts.expectType, reject missing typ). Acceptable as low given current minting always sets typ.
verifyNote: Verified. apps/api/src/lib/jwt.ts:76 reads `if (opts?.expectType && p.typ && p.typ !== opts.expectType)` — a fail-open guard that passes when p.typ is absent. apps/web/src/server/lib/jwt.ts has no typ concept at all (single 24h session token). Not currently exploitable since all Express minting paths (signAccessToken/signRefreshToken/signMfaPendingToken at jwt.ts:25-41) set typ, but a future typ-less minting path would be accepted on any expectType route. Low is correct (latent fail-open, no current exploit).

title: Public share endpoint leaks the sharer's internal user id
evidence: Live test: GET /api/share/<token> (unauthenticated, public) returned {"expiresAt":"...","userId":"cmpx4sq3s000504l78ayugvcm"}. resolveSharedView returns { expiresAt, userId }. Anyone holding a share link (or a leaked one) learns the sharer's internal CUID user identifier, which is not needed by the public share page (it only renders expiresAt).
recommendation: Drop userId from the public payload; return only what the share page renders (expiresAt, and later the coordinates if implemented). Keep userId server-side only.
verifyNote: Reproduced live: unauthenticated GET /api/share/<token> returned {"expiresAt":...,"userId":"cmpx5i4x3000105jg62i8c1ak"} which matches my throwaway session's uid. resolveSharedView (live-share.ts:102) returns userId; the share page (share/[token]/page.tsx) only renders expiresAt, so userId is unnecessary in the public payload. Internal CUID leak to anyone holding the link. Low is correct (CUIDs are not directly enumerable/abusable, but should not be exposed).

title: Check-in timer can be armed with zero confirmed contacts and will notify no one on expiry
evidence: armCheckIn explicitly allows zero confirmed contacts ('Per spec: warn but allow') and relies on 'The UI must surface no confirmed contacts loudly before this point.' Live test armed a timer for a contactless anon user: POST /api/safety/check-in returned 201 with confirmedContactCount:0. On expiry, triggerExpiry iterates an empty contact list and notifies no one — the timer is a silent no-op. Combined with the daily-cron issue, a user could rely on a 'Check on me' timer that protects nobody. The SOS path correctly hard-blocks at 400 no_confirmed_contacts; check-in does not.
recommendation: When confirmedContactCount is 0, either block the arm (like SOS does) or have the UI show a prominent, non-dismissable warning that no one will be notified. The returned confirmedContactCount:0 should drive a loud UI state, not a normal armed state.
verifyNote: Reproduced live: POST /api/safety/check-in {"durationMinutes":30} on a contactless throwaway user returned 201 with confirmedContactCount:0. check-in.ts:12-16 explicitly allows zero confirmed contacts ('Per spec: warn but allow'), and triggerExpiry iterates an empty contact list (no-op). SOS by contrast hard-blocks with 400 no_confirmed_contacts (sos.ts:34-40). Real inconsistency; low is appropriate (it relies on the UI surfacing the count, and confirmedContactCount:0 is returned for the UI to act on). Note the original finding combined this with the 'daily cron' issue (F1), but F1's premise is refuted, so the compounded risk is overstated.

title: AIAssistant steals focus to the floating launcher pill on initial page load
evidence: useEffect with deps [open]: when open is false it calls launcherRef.current?.focus({preventScroll:true}). Comment says 'Skip on first mount' but there is no first-mount guard, so on initial render (open=false) focus moves to the bottom-right launcher button as soon as the shell-mounted component hydrates on every tab.
recommendation: Add a firstRun useRef guard and return early on the first effect run before focusing the launcher.
verifyNote: Verified AIAssistant.tsx:56-66. Effect deps are [open]; React runs effects on mount, and open is false initially, so the else branch runs launcherRef.current?.focus({preventScroll:true}) on first mount. The launcher button (line 188) holds the ref, so it is populated post-commit and focus does move. The inline comment 'Skip on first mount' is contradicted by code — there is no firstRun/didMount guard anywhere (grep confirms). Real defect, but it focuses a visible bottom-right launcher control (not a trap or hidden element) and preventScroll avoids a jump, so impact is minor a11y/UX. Downgraded medium→low.

title: Landing CTA and in-app links route through legacy redirect stubs
evidence: Home hero CTA links to /now (redirect to /city); login does router.push('/threats') and a Skip link to /threats; error boundary 'Back to Awareness' to /threats; cities footer to /threats,/safety-score,/trends. All are redirect-only stubs to /city. Each click pays an extra server redirect and breaks if a stub is removed.
recommendation: Link directly to canonical routes (/city, /neighborhood, /overwatch).
verifyNote: Verified all cited sites: page.tsx:27 hero CTA href='/now'; (app)/error.tsx:31 'Back to Awareness' to /threats; (auth)/login/page.tsx:24 router.push('/threats') and :53 Skip link href='/threats'; cities/[city]/page.tsx:181-184 footer to /threats,/map,/safety-score,/trends. /now, /threats, /safety-score, /trends are all confirmed bare redirect() stubs to /city. Each click incurs an extra server redirect. Low is correct.

title: HotspotCard click uses window.location.href (full reload) instead of client-side routing
evidence: selectNeighborhood sets the area then does window.location.href='/neighborhood' — a hard navigation (full reload, JS re-download, store/anon-auth re-bootstrap). Area is already persisted to the useArea store, so router.push would work. CrimeMap.tsx already migrated this same pattern to Next/Link.
recommendation: Replace window.location.href with useRouter().push('/neighborhood').
verifyNote: Verified city/page.tsx:64-67: selectNeighborhood calls setArea(...) then window.location.href='/neighborhood', a hard full-page navigation. Area is persisted to the useArea store immediately before, so router.push would preserve it without a reload. CrimeMap uses Next/Link for the analogous case. Low is correct.

title: useDocumentTitle cleanup can restore a stale previous-page title
evidence: Captures original=document.title at effect time and restores it on cleanup; on client transitions the unmount restores the prior page's title rather than a neutral default, self-correcting only when the next page's hook overwrites it.
recommendation: Set the title without capturing/restoring an 'original', or restore to a fixed brand default.
verifyNote: Verified use-document-title.ts:13-18: captures const original = document.title at effect time and restores it in cleanup. On client route transitions the unmounting page's cleanup restores the prior title rather than a neutral brand default; it only self-corrects once the next page's hook overwrites it. Real but cosmetic/transient. Low is correct.

title: Server proxy forwards Authorization upstream but emits shared s-maxage Cache-Control without Vary
evidence: tryProxy forwards the inbound Authorization header to Railway yet returns Cache-Control 'public, s-maxage=300, stale-while-revalidate=900' with no Vary header. Safe today (crime/safezone endpoints are not user-specific) but the generic helper would shared-cache user data on the CDN if reused for an auth-scoped upstream.
recommendation: Strip Authorization for public routes, or set Cache-Control: private / add Vary: Authorization.
verifyNote: Verified proxy-to-api.ts:46-47 forwards the inbound Authorization header upstream, and :75-80 emits Cache-Control 'public, s-maxage=300, stale-while-revalidate=900' (defaulted when upstream omits it) with no Vary header. Latent risk only: current consumers (crime/safezone) are not user-specific, so no live data leak. Adjusted from medium to low — it is a defensive/footgun note, not an active defect.

title: verifySession token-type guard is bypassable for any token without a typ claim (`&& p.typ`)
evidence: `if (opts?.expectType && p.typ && p.typ !== opts.expectType) throw ...` — the `&& p.typ` short-circuits the type check entirely when a token has no typ claim, so a typ-less token is accepted as BOTH an access and a refresh token. The code comments (jwt.ts:69-72) acknowledge legacy pre-v93p2 tokens have no typ; all current minters set typ, and the 30-day refresh TTL means any legacy token is now expired, so live exposure is minimal — but the guard as written is not robust against any future code path that mints a typ-less token.
recommendation: Treat a missing typ as a failure when expectType is specified: `if (opts?.expectType && p.typ !== opts.expectType) throw ...` (drop the `&& p.typ`). Since legacy tokens are long expired this is safe to tighten now.
verifyNote: Verified jwt.ts:76: `if (opts?.expectType && p.typ && p.typ !== opts.expectType) throw` — the `&& p.typ` makes a typ-less token pass as both access and refresh. All current minters (signAccessToken/signRefreshToken) set typ and the 30-day refresh TTL means legacy typ-less tokens are expired, so live exposure is minimal but the guard is non-robust. Low is correct.

title: Promise.race timeout helpers never clear their setTimeout (timer leak under load)
evidence: withCitywideTimeout / withAreaBriefTimeout / the inline incident-summary race create `new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, MS))` and Promise.race against the real work, but never capture or clearTimeout the handle. When the real promise wins, the 45s (citywide) or 22s (AI) timer keeps a live handle in the event loop until it fires. Each is short-lived and harmless individually, but under sustained traffic these accumulate thousands of pending timers and keep references to the resolve closure alive, adding avoidable heap/timer pressure on a box already fighting OOM (see the v97 memory watchdog).
recommendation: Capture the timeout id and clearTimeout it in a .finally() (or use AbortSignal.timeout). Low impact but trivially fixable and relevant given the documented memory constraints.
verifyNote: Verified: crime-data.routes.ts:52-59 withCitywideTimeout, ai.routes.ts:45-52 withAreaBriefTimeout, and ai.routes.ts:101-106 inline incident-summary race all create new Promise(resolve=>setTimeout(resolve,MS)) inside Promise.race and never capture/clearTimeout the handle. When the real work wins, the 45s/22s timer stays pending in the event loop holding the resolve closure. Trivial individually; relevant given documented OOM/memory-watchdog constraints. Low is correct.

title: Push digest deep-links to /threats, a route that only exists as a redirect stub
evidence: runDailyDigest sends `data: { url: '/threats' }`. Per the web recon map, /threats is a legacy server redirect() stub to /city (login also still redirects there). The notification click works via the redirect but the deep-link is stale and forces an extra client-side hop; if the redirect stub is ever removed the digest CTA breaks.
recommendation: Point the digest url at the canonical /city route directly to avoid relying on the redirect shim.
verifyNote: Verified: digest.service.ts:40 sends data:{url:'/threats'}. apps/web/src/app/(app)/threats/page.tsx is a server redirect() stub to /city. The notification click works today via the redirect (extra hop) but the deep-link is stale and breaks if the stub is removed. Low/info boundary; low is acceptable since it currently functions only via the shim.

title: Leaf dispatcher methods route any unrecognized area slug to San Diego's adapter (cityForArea fallback) without a cityBySlug guard
evidence: crimeData.getAreaStats/getIncidents/getRecentReports all call `cityForArea(area)` to pick the adapter. cityForArea returns `CITIES[0]` (San Diego) for any slug that matches none of its hardcoded prefixes (cities.ts:536 `return CITIES[0];`). The citywide composers were hardened to reject unknown cities via cityBySlug (dispatcher.ts:173-176, mix.ts:95-96, upticks.ts:42-43), but the leaf getIncidents path has no such guard. Impact is limited because the SD adapter filters by area label, so an unknown slug returns [] rather than leaking SD incidents — but a slug that happens to collide with a real SD neighborhood label would silently serve SD data for a foreign request. The same class of 'silent SD fallback' bug is what dispatcher.ts:170-176 and upticks.ts:40-43 were explicitly written to stop at the composer layer.
recommendation: Have cityForArea optionally signal 'no match' (or add a guarded variant) and have the leaf dispatcher methods return [] / null for slugs whose city can't be positively identified, rather than defaulting to CITIES[0].
verifyNote: Verified. dispatcher.ts getAreaStats (line 77), getIncidents (line 109), getRecentReports (line 127) all call cityForArea(area) with no cityBySlug guard. cities.ts:536 `return CITIES[0]` (San Diego) is the fallback for any unmatched slug. The composers ARE guarded: _getCitywide rejects unknown slugs (dispatcher.ts:173-176), upticks.ts:42-43, and mix uses cityBySlug. The leaf path's SD adapter filters by area label so an unknown slug returns [] in practice, but a slug colliding with a real SD neighborhood label would serve SD data for a foreign request. Real unguarded gap; impact limited by label filtering. Low severity appropriate.

title: lookupAreaKm2 substring fallback matches the first polygon when the normalized area label is empty
evidence: In the substring fallback `if (n.includes(target) || target.includes(n)) return area;` — if `target = normName(areaLabel)` is the empty string (a label consisting only of non-alphanumerics, which normName strips to ''), then `n.includes('')` is always true and the function returns the area of whatever polygon iterates first, producing an arbitrary/wrong km² used as a population proxy in safety-score's denominator. Exact-match loop (lines 114-116) wouldn't catch it unless a polygon name also normalizes to ''. Low likelihood given real labels, but unguarded.
recommendation: Early-return null when `target.length === 0` (and skip candidates whose normalized name is empty) before the substring fallback.
verifyNote: Verified in polygon-areas.ts:103-122. normName strips non-alphanumerics via /[^a-z0-9]+/g so a label of only punctuation normalizes to ''. In the substring fallback (118-120) `n.includes(target)` with target==='' is always true, returning the first iterated polygon's area — an arbitrary km2 used as a population proxy in safety-score's denominator. The exact-match loop (114-116) would only catch this if a polygon name also normalizes to ''. Unguarded edge case, low real-world likelihood given real labels. Low severity correct.

title: User soft-delete client extension does not cover findUniqueOrThrow / findFirstOrThrow / aggregate / groupBy
location: apps/api/src/lib/prisma.ts:38-59; bypassed at apps/api/src/services/auth.service.ts:171 (me() uses user.findUniqueOrThrow)
evidence: The `$extends` soft-delete filter only intercepts user `findUnique`, `findFirst`, `findMany`, `count`. `auth.service.ts:171` `me()` calls `prisma.user.findUniqueOrThrow({ where: { id: userId } })` with no deletedAt filter, so it would return a soft-deleted user's row. In practice the auth middleware (apps/api/src/middleware/auth.ts:46 selects deletedAt and rejects the session) short-circuits before me() runs, so this is defense-in-depth only — but the extension's own comment claims to make the filter 'unforgettable', which is not true for the *OrThrow / aggregate / groupBy variants.
recommendation: Add findUniqueOrThrow, findFirstOrThrow, aggregate, and groupBy to the user query extension (or switch me() to findUnique + explicit null check), so the 'unforgettable' guarantee actually holds across all read shapes.
verifyNote: lib/prisma.ts:38-59 confirms the user $extends only intercepts findUnique/findFirst/findMany/count — not findUniqueOrThrow/findFirstOrThrow/aggregate/groupBy, despite the comment claiming the filter is 'unforgettable'. auth.service.ts:171 me() uses findUniqueOrThrow with no deletedAt filter, so it would return a soft-deleted row. Confirmed the mitigating short-circuit: auth.ts:44-49 selects deletedAt and treats a soft-deleted user as token-invalid before any handler runs. So this is defense-in-depth only. Low is correct.

title: GET /community/posts/mine is an unbounded findMany (no take)
evidence: `prisma.post.findMany({ where: { authorId: req.session!.uid }, orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' }, include: { flags: true, area: true } })` has no `take`. Every other Post list query in the codebase caps at 20-1000 (see neighborhood.routes.ts:43 take:20, queue.service.ts take:50, community.routes.ts:43 take:50, neighborhood.routes.ts:22 NEIGHBORHOOD_LIST_CAP=1000). A prolific or abusive author's full post history (plus flags + area joins) is fetched and serialized on every call.
recommendation: Add `take: 100` (or a cursor) consistent with the other post lists; the @@index([authorId, status]) / authorId path already supports an ordered scan.
verifyNote: community.routes.ts:62-66 confirmed: findMany on authorId with orderBy createdAt desc, include {flags, area}, and NO take. Every comparable list does cap: community.routes.ts:43 take:50, neighborhood.routes.ts:43 take:20, neighborhood NEIGHBORHOOD_LIST_CAP=1000, queue.service.ts:8 take=50. Authenticated to own posts only, so blast radius is one user's history; low is correct.

title: CI typechecks only apps/api and apps/web; packages/db and packages/crime-data are not independently typecheck-gated
evidence: CI runs `typecheck --workspace apps/api` and `--workspace apps/web` only. packages/crime-data is built (`npm run build --workspace packages/crime-data`, ci.yml:42) so its `tsc -p tsconfig.json` does emit-typecheck it; but packages/db has a `typecheck` script (packages/db/package.json) that CI never invokes, and the root `npm run typecheck --workspaces --if-present` is also not used. A type error in packages/db (e.g. a hand-edited generated-client consumer or seed.ts) would not be caught until a downstream app build pulls it in.
recommendation: Replace the two per-app typecheck steps with `npm run typecheck` (root, which fans out `--workspaces --if-present`), or add explicit `typecheck --workspace packages/db` and `--workspace packages/crime-data` steps so every workspace with a typecheck script is gated.
verifyNote: ci.yml:44-48 typechecks only apps/api and apps/web. packages/db has a 'typecheck' script (package.json:18 'tsc --noEmit') that CI never invokes. crime-data IS emit-typechecked via its build step (ci.yml:42 -> build script runs 'tsc -p tsconfig.json'). Root 'npm run typecheck --workspaces --if-present' exists (root package.json:26) but is unused. db gap is real; low is correct.

title: No automated tests for the web app or crime-data package; CI only runs apps/api vitest
location: .github/workflows/ci.yml:56-57; apps/web/package.json (no test script); packages/crime-data/src (0 test files)
evidence: CI's only test step is `npm test --workspace apps/api`. apps/web has no `test` script at all, and packages/crime-data has zero *.test.ts/*.spec.ts files. The most defect-prone, hand-calibrated logic in the repo lives in packages/crime-data (safety-score.ts ~1634 lines, 44 per-city adapters, polygon-area math, population denominators) and has no unit-test gate — regressions there (e.g. a denominator or NIBRS-classification change) ship straight to prod once they typecheck.
recommendation: Add at least smoke/unit tests for the highest-leverage pure functions in packages/crime-data (computeSafetyScore against fixture rows, polygon-area shoelace, normName matching) and wire a test step into ci.yml. These are pure and deterministic, so they are cheap to cover and high-value.
verifyNote: ci.yml:57 only runs 'npm test --workspace apps/api'. apps/web/package.json has no test script (verified undefined). packages/crime-data/src has 0 *.test.ts/*.spec.ts files (verified via find). Low is correct.

title: Vercel installCommand uses `npm install` rather than `npm ci`, so prod web builds don't strictly honor the lockfile
evidence: `"installCommand": "npm install"`. Unlike the CI workflow (npm ci, ci.yml:36) and nixpacks (npm ci, nixpacks.toml:28), the Vercel web build runs `npm install`, which may mutate the lockfile / resolve newer compatible versions and means the deployed web bundle can drift from what CI tested. The whole point of the documented rollup-optionalDependency lockfile hack (package.json:43-46) is reproducible installs from the lock — undercut on the Vercel path by not using `npm ci`.
recommendation: Set `"installCommand": "npm ci"` in vercel.json for reproducible, lockfile-pinned prod web builds matching CI and Railway. (Vercel caches node_modules across builds regardless, so the cost is negligible.)
verifyNote: vercel.json:4 installCommand='npm install'; ci.yml:36 uses 'npm ci'; nixpacks.toml:28 uses 'npm ci --include=dev --include-workspace-root'. The Vercel web path is the only one not lockfile-strict. Low is correct.

title: City count inconsistent across legal pages: About and Methodology say/list 38 cities, Terms says 44, registry has 44
evidence: About page: "an independent neighborhood-safety information site for 38 US cities." Methodology page's data-sources list enumerates 38 city feeds (counted) and omits the 6 cities project memory records as added 2026-06-01 (Austin, Phoenix, Jacksonville, Virginia Beach, Gainesville, Tampa). Terms page (line 24): "44 supported US cities." The crime-data registry and project MEMORY both confirm 44 live cities. So Terms is current while About/Methodology are stale post-expansion, producing a public, citable inconsistency about basic product scope.
recommendation: Replace the hardcoded counts/lists with a value derived from the CITIES registry (or a shared SUPPORTED_CITY_COUNT constant) so all three pages stay in sync; at minimum bump About to 44 and add the 6 missing cities to the Methodology source list.
verifyNote: Verified. about/page.tsx:25 says '38 US cities.' methodology/page.tsx:31 prose says '44 supported cities' but the enumerated data-sources list (lines 32-36) contains exactly 38 city names and omits the 6 added 2026-06-01 (Austin, Phoenix, Jacksonville, Virginia Beach, Gainesville, Tampa). terms/page.tsx:24 says '44 supported US cities.' cities.ts registry has 44 real city entries (slugs san-diego..tampa, lines 73-431). So About is stale at 38, Methodology is internally inconsistent (44 prose vs 38 enumerated, missing the 6 new cities), Terms is current at 44. Public citable scope inconsistency. Low is correct.

title: Age gate is a soft client-side acknowledgement only; 'verifiable' COPPA framing slightly overstated, and it does not block account creation
evidence: AgeGate stores the answer in localStorage `cs.age.v1` and, on "under 13", merely redirects to /privacy#children — it does not prevent the user from clicking back or registering, and the code comment itself concedes it is "deliberately a soft gate (not server-side verification)." The component header characterizes it as satisfying COPPA §312.5's "verifiable" age check for the affirmative defense; a self-attested, bypassable localStorage flag is the common general-audience pattern but is not 'verifiable parental consent' and the FTC has not formally blessed it as such. Registration (auth.routes.ts POST /register) performs no age check. Low risk because the service genuinely does not collect data from children and the policy disclaims child-direction, but the in-code 'affirmative defense'/'verifiable' wording is stronger than what the mechanism provides.
recommendation: Soften the AgeGate internal/legal framing to 'age acknowledgement' rather than implying a verifiable COPPA check, and confirm with counsel that a self-attestation gate is acceptable for the audience; if higher assurance is wanted, gate account creation (not just browsing) behind the acknowledgement.
verifyNote: Verified. AgeGate.tsx stores choice in localStorage cs.age.v1 (line 17,64,68); 'under 13' redirects to /privacy#children (line 69) but does not block back-navigation or registration. Code header (lines 4-16) explicitly characterizes it as COPPA §312.5 'verifiable' check providing the 'affirmative-defense paper trail the FTC has accepted' while the comment itself concedes it is 'deliberately a soft gate (not server-side verification).' Register route (auth.routes.ts:19-23) performs no age check — only email/password/displayName. AgeGate mounted in layout.tsx:142. The 'verifiable'/'affirmative defense' framing is stronger than a bypassable self-attested localStorage flag supports. Low is correct given the service genuinely collects no child data and disclaims child-direction (privacy:175).

title: Brand/storage-key split (travelsafe.* keys, repo + github URL named TravelSafe) surfaced to users in the Privacy policy itself
evidence: The Privacy policy lists the on-device storage keys verbatim to users — `travelsafe.token`, `travelsafe.city.v1`, `travelsafe.area.v1`, etc. — and states the anonymous device email is `device-*@travelsafe.local` (privacy:170), while the product is branded CommunitySafe throughout and operated by CyberWave Technologies LLC. Contact/repo links point to github.com/damienmcdade/TravelSafe (privacy:185, about:64-72). This is transparent (the keys really are named that way) so it is not a misrepresentation, but the public TravelSafe vs CommunitySafe divergence on legal pages looks unpolished and could prompt user confusion about which entity holds their data.
recommendation: Either rename the storage keys/repo to the CommunitySafe brand over time, or add a one-line note in Privacy explaining 'travelsafe.*' is the legacy internal namespace for CommunitySafe operated by CyberWave Technologies LLC, so the cited keys aren't mistaken for a third party.
verifyNote: Verified. privacy/page.tsx:43-51 lists on-device keys verbatim (travelsafe.token, travelsafe.city.v1, travelsafe.area.v1, travelsafe.saved-areas.v1, travelsafe.swr.v1.*, travelsafe.safety.disclaimer.ack, travelsafe.assistant.*, plus cs.age.v1 / cs.consent.v1). privacy:170 cites synthetic device-*@travelsafe.local email. GitHub links point to github.com/damienmcdade/TravelSafe (privacy:185, about:65,149). Product branded CommunitySafe, operated by CyberWave Technologies LLC throughout. Transparent (keys really are named that way) so not a misrepresentation, but the public TravelSafe-vs-CommunitySafe divergence on legal pages is unpolished and could confuse users about which entity holds their data. Low is appropriate.

title: THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES.md still says photo attribution is template-style "work in progress" / "before public launch", but per-photo enumeration already shipped
evidence: NOTICES.md:49-55 'CC-BY-SA imagery (work in progress) ... credits/page.tsx currently uses a template-style attribution; per-photo enumeration is a tracked improvement before public launch.' The actual credits/page.tsx now renders a per-photo list (lines 63-93: iterates PHOTOS, one <li> per image linking to its Wikimedia Commons file page) and explicitly cites CC-BY-SA 4.0 §3(a)(2). The notice file describes a prior state and understates current compliance.
recommendation: Update the THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES 'CC-BY-SA imagery' section to reflect that per-photo enumeration is live in credits/page.tsx and remove the 'work in progress / before public launch' language.
verifyNote: Verified. THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES.md:49-55 says credits/page.tsx 'currently uses a template-style attribution; per-photo enumeration is a tracked improvement before public launch.' credits/page.tsx:71-90 actually iterates Object.entries(PHOTOS) rendering one <li> per image with a Commons file-page link, and lines 43-46 cite CC-BY-SA 4.0 §3(a)(2). Notice describes a superseded state. Low fair.

title: CC-BY-SA photo attribution relies solely on a link to the Commons file page; author name/license version are not shown on-page
evidence: credits/page.tsx renders only the filename as a link to commons.wikimedia.org and states attribution is 'by URI to that resource' under CC-BY-SA 4.0 §3(a)(2). CC-BY-SA 4.0 §3(a)(1) requires retaining the creator's name, copyright notice, and license identifier; §3(a)(2) permits satisfying these by a URI/hyperlink only 'to the extent reasonably practicable.' Showing just a filename (no photographer name, no per-photo license version) is a defensible-but-thin reading, especially since some PHOTOS are non-SA licenses (CC-BY 2.0/3.0, PD) lumped under one SA-framed explanation (credits/page.tsx:53-55).
recommendation: Either surface the photographer name + specific license per photo (parse/store it alongside the URL) or keep the URI approach but add the standard CC attribution string template. Low priority given links resolve to canonical pages, but worth tightening before relying on it as the sole compliance basis.
verifyNote: Verified as an accurate legal-form observation. credits/page.tsx renders only fileNameFromUrl(url) as a link to commons.wikimedia.org (lines 75-89), with attribution justified solely 'by URI to that resource' under §3(a)(2) (lines 43-46); no photographer name or per-photo license version on-page, and the license summary lumps CC-BY 2.0/3.0 and PD under one SA-framed block (lines 53-55). This is a defensible-but-thin reading, correctly characterized. Low/medium-confidence rating fair.

title: User-facing data-export filenames use the internal "travelsafe" brand, not CommunitySafe
evidence: GDPR/account data export downloads as `travelsafe-account-<date>.json` (safety/page.tsx:196) and the server route sets `Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="travelsafe-account-<uid>.json"` (export/route.ts:26); CSV export is `travelsafe-<slug>-<date>.csv` (TrendPanel.tsx:374). These filenames are seen by end users and contradict the CommunitySafe brand everywhere else in user-facing copy. (localStorage keys like `travelsafe.token` are internal and acceptable, but downloaded filenames are not.)
recommendation: Rename user-downloaded export filenames to `communitysafe-...` to match the product brand. Internal storage keys can stay for back-compat.
verifyNote: Verified. export/route.ts:26 sets Content-Disposition filename='travelsafe-account-${session.uid}.json'; safety/page.tsx:196 a.download='travelsafe-account-<date>.json'; TrendPanel.tsx:374 'travelsafe-${slug}-<date>.csv'. These are user-downloaded filenames carrying the internal brand, contradicting CommunitySafe in user-facing copy. Low fair.

title: Category enum rendered lowercase-raw in AreaInsightsPanel ("persons"/"property"/"society") — inconsistent with CategoryBreakdown's proper labels
evidence: AreaInsightsPanel shows the trend category as `{t.category.toLowerCase()}` → renders "persons" / "property" / "society" (raw NIBRS family, lowercased). CategoryBreakdown maps the same enums to user-facing labels via COLORS: PERSONS→"Persons", PROPERTY→"Property", SOCIETY→"Society". HotspotCard.tsx:79 similarly shows lowercased raw (`dominantRaw.toLowerCase()` → "persons" etc.). Same conceptual data reads two different ways across cards on the same drill-down.
recommendation: Use a shared category label map (e.g. PERSONS→"Violent (persons)" or "Persons", PROPERTY→"Property", SOCIETY→"Society/other") in AreaInsightsPanel and HotspotCard so category naming is consistent app-wide.
verifyNote: AreaInsightsPanel.tsx:66 renders {t.category.toLowerCase()} -> 'persons'/'property'/'society'. HotspotCard.tsx:79 sets dominant = dominantRaw.toLowerCase(). CategoryBreakdown.tsx:6-8 maps the same enums to proper labels 'Persons'/'Property'/'Society'. Genuine inconsistency across cards on the same drill-down. Cosmetic; low is correct.

title: IncidentSummaryCard fallback prose lowercases proper-noun context label
evidence: Fallback (LLM offline) renders `{data.recentCount.toLocaleString()} incidents reported in {contextLabel.toLowerCase()} over the last {data.windowDays} days`. When contextLabel is "San Diego (citywide)" or "Pacific Beach" this produces "...reported in san diego (citywide) over the last 30 days", lowercasing a city/neighborhood proper noun in user-facing prose.
recommendation: Drop the .toLowerCase() and phrase so the label keeps its casing, e.g. `...incidents reported in {contextLabel} over the last {windowDays} days`.
verifyNote: IncidentSummaryCard.tsx:115 fallback prose uses {contextLabel.toLowerCase()}, while the same component's header (:98) renders {contextLabel} in original casing. A proper-noun label like 'San Diego (citywide)' or 'Pacific Beach' is lowercased only in the fallback prose. Real defect, cosmetic; low correct.

title: Two near-duplicate hour-of-day cards on the neighborhood page with confusingly similar titles
location: apps/web/src/app/(app)/neighborhood/page.tsx:233 (TimeOfDayCard 'When incidents happen') and 240 (TrendPanel → TimeOfDayChart 'When reports happen')
evidence: neighborhood/page.tsx renders `<TimeOfDayCard .../>` (h3 'When incidents happen', 24-bar histogram) and immediately below `<TrendPanel headingLevel={3} />`, whose TrendReport renders TimeOfDayChart (h3/h4 'When reports happen', 4-bucket bars). Both describe hour-of-day incident distribution for the same area from the same /safezone/trend feed. Beyond the ui-tod-1 bug, this is redundant surface area and the two titles ('incidents' vs 'reports') are easy to mistake for the same card duplicated.
recommendation: Pick one hour-of-day visualization for the neighborhood page. If TimeOfDayCard is fixed, remove TrendPanel's embedded TimeOfDayChart (or vice-versa) so the page has a single, authoritative 'when incidents happen' card.
verifyNote: neighborhood/page.tsx:233 renders <TimeOfDayCard> ('When incidents happen', 24-bar) and :240 renders <TrendPanel headingLevel={3}/>, which renders TimeOfDayChart ('When reports happen', 4-bucket) at TrendPanel.tsx:189-190/407. Both visualize hour-of-day from the same /safezone/trend feed for the same area. Redundant surface with confusingly similar titles. Low is appropriate.

title: TimeOfDayChart subheader claims a fixed '{windowDays}-day window' but the buckets are anchored to the freshest data row, not now
evidence: TrendPanel's TimeOfDayChart header prints `{areaLabel} · {windowDays}-day window` (TrendPanel.tsx:408) and the body says 'Hourly distribution of every report in the window'. Server-side, `inWindow` is anchored on `anchorMs = maxT` (the freshest valid row) minus windowDays (trend-feed.ts:170-171), so for lagged feeds (the code itself names LA/NYC/KC/Phoenix) the actual calendar dates can be weeks old even though the label says '30-day window'. TimeOfDayCard's own copy handles this honestly with a computed `spanDays ... ending {newestStr}` line (TimeOfDayCard.tsx:199-269) — TrendPanel's chart does not.
recommendation: Either surface the actual data span (like TimeOfDayCard does) in TrendPanel's TimeOfDayChart, or soften the label to 'most recent ~{windowDays} days of published reports' so a lagged feed doesn't read as a literal trailing-30-day window.
verifyNote: TrendPanel.tsx:408 prints '{areaLabel} · {windowDays}-day window' and :411 says 'every report in the window', while trend-feed.ts:170-171/343-344 anchor the window on anchorMs=freshest row (maxT/freshestMs) minus windowDays, not now — so for lagged feeds the real span can be weeks stale yet labeled '30-day window'. TimeOfDayCard handles this honestly with a computed span line (TimeOfDayCard.tsx:199-269). Wording/accuracy nit; low is fine.

title: TrustedContactsManager: Remove and Resend buttons give no feedback on failure (silent dead toggle)
evidence: async function remove(id) { await api(`/contacts/${id}`, { method: "DELETE" }); await reload(); } and resend() similarly call api() with no try/catch and never touch the `error` state (unlike add(), which wraps in try/catch and calls setError). If the DELETE/resend request rejects (network blip, 401 after token expiry, server 5xx), the promise rejects unhandled, reload() never runs, and the row stays put with zero visual indication the action failed — the button feels dead. Note /contacts DELETE also has no writeLimiter on the API side per the API recon, but that is not the UI issue here.
recommendation: Wrap remove() and resend() in try/catch and surface the error via the existing setError state (and ideally a transient per-row 'busy' state mirroring add()'s pattern), so a failed action is visible rather than silently no-op.
verifyNote: Verified TrustedContactsManager.tsx:60-68. remove() and resend() call api() with no try/catch and never set the existing `error` state, unlike add() (try/catch at 46-57 with setError). A rejected DELETE/resend leaves reload() unrun and the row unchanged with zero feedback. Low is accurate.

title: LiveActivityBadge shows a permanently green 'Live' dot regardless of SSE connection health
evidence: The badge always renders a solid green sage dot and the literal text 'Live'. useCommunityStream's es.onerror handler is an empty body relying on EventSource auto-reconnect; there is no error/disconnected signal propagated to the component. If the SSE endpoint (/api/community/stream) is down or the connection is dropped and not yet re-established, the user still sees a green 'Live' indicator. The '· last update Ns ago' suffix only ever appears after a post.verified/post.reverted event, so on a quiet-but-healthy stream it also shows bare 'Live' — making 'connected' and 'down' visually indistinguishable.
recommendation: Track EventSource readyState / onerror in the hook and expose a connection status; render a muted/amber dot (e.g. 'Reconnecting…') when the stream is not OPEN so the 'Live' claim reflects reality.
verifyNote: Verified LiveActivityBadge.tsx:30-39 — dot is always bg-sage-500 (only the ping ring animates on pulse), and sse.ts:14-16 has an empty es.onerror body relying on auto-reconnect with no status exposed. 'Live' green dot renders regardless of SSE connection health. Low is accurate.

title: Community post react/report buttons swallow failures with no user feedback
evidence: Both react() and report() use try/finally with NO catch. api() throws on non-ok responses. On failure (e.g. rate-limited, auth lapse) the button resets to its idle label via finally, but never flips to the success 'Reported ✓'/done state and shows no error — the user cannot tell whether the action registered. The optimistic `setConfirmed(...)` line never runs on failure, so the only signal is the button quietly returning to default.
recommendation: Add a catch branch that surfaces a brief inline error or toast so a failed reaction/report is distinguishable from a successful one.
verifyNote: community/page.tsx:222-244: report() and react() both use try{...setConfirmed}finally{setBusy(null)} with no catch. api() throws on non-ok; on failure setConfirmed never runs and the button silently resets to idle with no error surfaced, indistinguishable from idle. Confirmed; low is right (no data loss, only ambiguous feedback).

title: AdSlot's no-AdSense guard is dead code due to hardcoded publisher-ID fallback
evidence: const ADSENSE_CLIENT_ID = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_ADSENSE_CLIENT_ID ?? "ca-pub-8731629548430880"; then `if (!ADSENSE_CLIENT_ID) return null;`. Because of the hardcoded fallback the constant is never falsy, so the documented behavior ('Renders NOTHING when NEXT_PUBLIC_ADSENSE_CLIENT_ID isn't set') is impossible — the guard can never return null and the component would always emit an <ins> with the production publisher ID. By contrast layout.tsx:42 reads the env var with NO fallback to correctly gate the auto-ads script and the CookieConsentBanner (layout.tsx:119,140). The two diverge. AdSlot is currently unused (no callsites found), so impact is latent, but if ever mounted it would serve ads (and warrant the consent banner) even on environments that intend to be ad-free.
recommendation: Drop the hardcoded fallback so AdSlot matches layout.tsx: `const ADSENSE_CLIENT_ID = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_ADSENSE_CLIENT_ID;` — then the existing null-guard works and ad-free deploys stay ad-free.
verifyNote: AdSlot.tsx:7 hardcodes `?? "ca-pub-8731629548430880"`, so ADSENSE_CLIENT_ID is never falsy and the guards at lines 48 and 59 (`if(!ADSENSE_CLIENT_ID) return null`) are dead — the documented 'renders NOTHING when env unset' is impossible. By contrast layout.tsx:42 reads the env var with no fallback (per the v92 comment removing the hardcoded default) and gates the auto-ads script + CookieConsentBanner on it, so the two diverge. Grep found zero AdSlot callsites, so impact is latent — low is correct.

title: Pricing CTA and onboarding 'Skip' link to legacy redirect stubs
evidence: Pricing Free CTA href is `/now` (a server redirect()->/city stub) and the trusted-contacts onboarding 'Skip and continue' href is `/threats` (a server redirect()->/city stub per the route map). Both resolve via an extra redirect hop rather than pointing at the canonical destinations (/city). Functional but fragile and adds a redirect round-trip; if a stub is ever removed these become 404s.
recommendation: Point the links directly at the canonical routes (e.g. /city) instead of the legacy redirect stubs.
verifyNote: pricing/page.tsx:49 Free CTA href='/now' and onboarding/trusted-contacts/page.tsx:23 'Skip and continue' href='/threats'. Confirmed both routes are server redirect stubs: (app)/now/page.tsx and (app)/threats/page.tsx each call redirect('/city'). So both links incur an extra redirect hop instead of pointing at /city directly. Functional but fragile; low correct.

title: cityForArea uses hardcoded CITIES[n] indices that must stay in lockstep with array order
location: packages/crime-data/src/cities.ts:491-537 (return CITIES[1..43]) vs CITIES array order (lines 71-437)
evidence: cityForArea maps each prefix to a positional index, e.g. `if (slug.startsWith("den-")) return CITIES[30]`. The Denver comment at lines 299-310 explicitly notes Denver was appended at the END specifically because inserting mid-list 'would shift every subsequent index' and silently misroute areas (the exact failure mode that zeroes counts per project memory). All indices are currently correct (verified live for all 8 cities), but this design is brittle: any future mid-list insert/reorder, or any divergence between the cityForArea chain and the parallel AREA_SLUG_PREFIX map (lines 546-558), would silently misroute areas to the wrong city/adapter with no compile-time guard.
recommendation: Replace positional returns with slug-keyed lookups: build a single source-of-truth Record<prefix, citySlug> (or add a `prefix` field to CityEntry) and resolve via cityBySlug(prefixMap[matchedPrefix]). This removes the index-order coupling, lets AREA_SLUG_PREFIX and cityForArea derive from one table, and eliminates the documented 'shifts every subsequent index' hazard. Optionally add a unit test asserting each prefix routes to the city whose slug carries that prefix.
verifyNote: Verified independently: extracted CITIES array slug order programmatically and it matches cityForArea's positional returns exactly (CITIES[1]=los-angeles for 'la-' through CITIES[43]=tampa for 'tpa-'). Denver comment at cities.ts:299-310 explicitly documents Denver was appended at END because 'inserting mid-list would shift every subsequent index' — confirming the hazard is real and known. A parallel AREA_SLUG_PREFIX map (lines 546-558) is a genuine second source of truth that could silently diverge from the cityForArea chain with no compile-time guard. Currently all indices correct (verified live), but the maintainability/correctness hazard is real and latent. 'low' is the appropriate severity (no current defect, but a documented silent-misroute trap). Recommendation to use slug-keyed lookup is sound.

title: Coverage dashboard shows null 'newest incident' freshness for the large-feed cities (LA, SF, Chicago) despite live data
evidence: /api/coverage returns newestIncidentAt:null for chicago, san-francisco, and los-angeles, but the underlying feeds are live and large: /api/crime-data/citywide?city=chicago total 50000, san-francisco 41318, los-angeles 99997. Only san-diego (small cached CSV) returns a real newestIncidentAt (2026-05-31). The 8s freshness sub-probe blows its budget on the big feeds and leaves the freshness field empty, so the public status page cannot show 'Latest data: <date>' for three flagship cities.
recommendation: Source the coverage freshness timestamp from the already-warmed adapter row cache / last-known-good rather than a fresh per-request sample, or raise/separate the freshness probe budget for large feeds, so LA/SF/Chicago display an honest last-updated date.
verifyNote: Live /api/coverage returns newestIncidentAt:null for chicago, san-francisco, los-angeles but a real 2026-05-31 date for san-diego (small cached CSV). status.ts:110-112 samples freshness via getAreaStats/getIncidents on areas[0] wrapped in withTimeout(SAMPLE_TIMEOUT_MS) where SAMPLE_TIMEOUT_MS=8_000 (line 68); on timeout it leaves newestIncidentAt null (line 109 comment 'on timeout we simply skip the asOf'). Live null/non-null split matches the small-vs-large-feed mechanism. Did not directly time the probe but code path + live evidence are conclusive. Low severity appropriate (cosmetic freshness gap).

title: Seattle neighborhood labels carry lowercase-mid-name and bad-acronym title-casing artifacts
evidence: titleCase() only uppercases the first char after WHITESPACE (split(/\s+/)), not after '/'. SPD prints ALL-CAPS neighborhoods, so slash- and abbreviation-containing names render with lowercase mid-name letters and mis-cased acronyms. Live label set includes: 'Brighton/dunlap', 'Central Area/squire Park', 'Roosevelt/ravenna', 'Slu/cascade', 'Madrona/leschi', 'Judkins Park/north Beacon Hill', 'Claremont/rainier Vista', 'Lakewood/seward Park', 'Roxhill/westwood/arbor Heights', plus 'Fauntleroy Sw' (should be 'SW') and 'Slu/cascade' (SLU = South Lake Union). These labels surface verbatim in the picker wheel, cards, AI briefs and the map legend. (Map matching is unaffected because the polygon file was built from the same casing and normName() collapses '/'.)
recommendation: In titleCase(), split on /[\s/]+/ (or post-process each '/'-segment) so letters after a slash are capitalized, and add an acronym pass for SW/SLU/etc. Regenerate/normalize the seattle.geojson property names to match so the map legend reads cleanly. This is purely cosmetic copy quality; no data is lost.
verifyNote: Verified independently. seattle-socrata.ts:55-57 titleCase() does s.toLowerCase().split(/\s+/) — it only re-capitalizes after whitespace, so it leaves a lowercase letter after every '/'. The seattle.geojson (parsed, 58 features) contains exactly the artifacts listed: 'Brighton/dunlap', 'Central Area/squire Park', 'Claremont/rainier Vista', 'Lakewood/seward Park', 'Roosevelt/ravenna', 'Roxhill/westwood/arbor Heights', 'Slu/cascade', 'Madrona/leschi', 'Judkins Park/north Beacon Hill', plus 'Fauntleroy Sw' (should be SW) and 'Slu/cascade' (SLU). Map matching genuinely unaffected: CrimeMap.tsx normName() (line 91-94) lowercases and replaces '/' with space on both sides. Purely cosmetic; low is correct.

title: NYC: Tottenville (precinct 123) has a neighborhood label but no polygon; precinct map has a 78-vs-77 duplicate
evidence: PRECINCT_TO_NEIGHBORHOOD has 78 entries but only 77 distinct values and NYC has 77 real precincts — i.e. one duplicate value (two precinct numbers resolve to the same neighborhood name). Polygon comparison: every served polygon matches a map value, but the map value 'Tottenville' has NO matching polygon (fuzzy-normalized). So a user who selects Tottenville on /neighborhood gets feed data but the crime map cannot shade/locate that area. All other 77 precinct neighborhoods match a polygon.
recommendation: Add a Tottenville (Staten Island far-south) polygon to new-york.geojson, and de-duplicate the precinct map so the 78th entry is intentional. Low impact (single low-volume SI precinct).
verifyNote: Core claim (Tottenville orphan) CONFIRMED: nypd-socrata.ts:198 maps precinct 123 -> 'Tottenville'; new-york.geojson parsed = 77 features and contains NO Tottenville polygon. Of the 78 precinct labels, exactly one ('Tottenville') has no polygon; all 77 polygons match a label. HOWEVER the finding's supporting evidence is partly WRONG: it claims '78 entries but only 77 distinct values ... one duplicate value'. I counted 78 distinct keys AND 78 distinct values — there is NO duplicate label. NYC's '77 precincts' count excludes the 78th here (the map legitimately has 78 distinct precinct entries). So the orphan is real but the de-duplication recommendation is based on a non-existent duplicate. Severity low is appropriate (single low-volume Staten Island precinct).

title: Philadelphia: 'Blue Bell Hill' polygon is in the served map but absent from the adapter's geocoder polygon set — a permanently empty map area
evidence: The served crime-map GeoJSON has 159 neighborhood polygons; the adapter's bundled geocoding polygon set (phl-neighborhoods.ts, header comment '158 OpenDataPhilly neighborhoods') has 158. Set-diff: the only polygon present in the map but missing from the adapter is 'Blue Bell Hill'. Because no incident can ever be point-in-polygon-assigned to a polygon the geocoder doesn't know about, the 'Blue Bell Hill' polygon will always render with 0 incidents (always risk level 1 / lowest), and it produces no pickable discovered area. Conversely the geocoder will never emit a Blue Bell Hill area. This is an honest empty cell, not a crash, hence low severity.
recommendation: Add the Blue Bell Hill polygon to phl-neighborhoods.ts (so incidents inside it geocode correctly and it can become a selectable area), or remove the Blue Bell Hill feature from philadelphia.geojson so the map and the feed have an identical 158-polygon surface.
verifyNote: Reproduced: apps/web/public/geo/philadelphia.geojson has 159 named features; phl-neighborhoods.ts has 158 polygons (header comment '158 OpenDataPhilly neighborhoods'). comm set-diff yields exactly one served-only name: 'Blue Bell Hill', zero adapter-only. Since geocoding is point-in-polygon over the bundled set, no incident can ever be assigned to Blue Bell Hill, so it renders as a permanent empty cell. Low severity correct.

title: Washington DC: several neighborhood labels carry raw source-formatting artifacts (slash/lowercase fragments) shown verbatim to users
evidence: DC's 51 names are real and recognizable (Georgetown, Adams Morgan, Columbia Heights, Capitol Hill, Historic Anacostia, etc.), but several retain unpolished source formatting that surfaces directly as the displayed label and AI-brief text: 'U St/pleasant', 'Logan Circle/shaw', 'Sw/waterfront', 'Fort Lincoln/gateway', 'Congress Heights/shipley', 'Naylor/hillcrest', 'Kent/palisades', 'Naval Station & Air Force', 'Gwu', '16th ST Heights'. These mix a slash with a lowercase fragment ('/pleasant', '/shaw', '/waterfront') and inconsistent casing ('Gwu', 'ST'), which read as data artifacts rather than neighborhood names. Slug round-tripping is unaffected (no slug collisions; labelForDCSlug reverses correctly), so this is cosmetic only.
recommendation: Normalize the DC display labels to canonical neighborhood names (e.g. 'U Street/Pleasant Plains', 'Logan Circle/Shaw', 'Southwest/Waterfront', 'GWU', '16th Street Heights') in dc-neighborhoods.json and the served GeoJSON, keeping the slugs stable so existing saved areas and curated populations still match.
verifyNote: All 10 cited labels exist verbatim in packages/crime-data/src/data/dc-neighborhoods.json (51 polygons) and are mirrored exactly in apps/web/public/geo/washington-dc.geojson (51 features): 'U St/pleasant','Logan Circle/shaw','Sw/waterfront','Fort Lincoln/gateway','Congress Heights/shipley','Naylor/hillcrest','Kent/palisades','Naval Station & Air Force','Gwu','16th ST Heights'. Verified no slug collisions (all 51 slugify uniquely), so round-tripping is unaffected — cosmetic only. Low severity correct.

title: New Orleans: 3 district-fallback neighborhood labels don't match any polygon
evidence: The coord-less fallback emits 'Treme', 'Algiers', and 'New Orleans East', none of which match a polygon in new-orleans.geojson (the polygon file has 'Treme - Lafitte', 'Algiers Point', and no 'New Orleans East'). If a row lacks coords or falls outside all 73 polygons, the area would not render on the map and would not join. Impact is small because the feed query is where: "location IS NOT NULL" so geocodeNola() handles the typical row; the other 5 fallbacks (Uptown, Mid-City, Bywater, Central City, French Quarter) do match polygons.
recommendation: Map the 3 fallback labels to real polygon names ('Treme'->'Treme - Lafitte', 'Algiers'->'Algiers Point', 'New Orleans East'->one of the East polygons like 'Little Woods' or 'Read Blvd East'), or add a NAME_ALIASES entry for each, so a coord-less/out-of-polygon row still joins to a renderable area.
verifyNote: Verified nola-socrata.ts:148-159 NOPD_DISTRICT_TO_NEIGHBORHOOD emits 'Treme','Algiers','New Orleans East'. new-orleans.geojson (73 features, parity-verified against nolaPolygons which also has 73) contains 'Treme - Lafitte' and 'Algiers Point' but no 'Treme','Algiers', or any 'New Orleans East' polygon. The other 5 fallbacks (Uptown, Mid-City, Bywater, Central City, French Quarter) all match polygons exactly. NAME_ALIASES (CrimeMap.tsx:59-89) does NOT cover these three (only covers NOLA 'florida dev'->'florida development'). Mechanism confirmed: the query is where 'location IS NOT NULL' so coord-less rows are excluded, but districtToNeighborhood is still invoked at nola-socrata.ts:189 when geocodeNola() returns null for a row that HAS coords (inside bbox/outside rings, or outside all polygons); such a row sets area='Treme'/'Algiers'/'New Orleans East' AND r.lat/r.lng, so getDiscoveredAreasNola aggregates it (passes the lat!=null guard) and it appears in lists/scores but matches no polygon on the map. Low severity correct.

title: Cincinnati: 'O'Bryonville' neighborhood in feed has no polygon
location: /Users/damiengantt-mcdade/TravelSafe/apps/web/public/geo/cincinnati.geojson (51 features) vs live feed (52 named neighborhoods)
evidence: Live CPD STARS feed (data.cincinnati-oh.gov/resource/7aqy-xrv9) returns 52 named neighborhoods after the adapter's numeric-artifact filter (cincinnati-socrata.ts:158 drops '1','15','256',...). 51 of 52 match polygons with zero orphans; only "O'Bryonville" (a small district CPD merges/splits with Evanston/Walnut Hills) has no polygon. It will appear in lists but not render on the map.
recommendation: Either add an O'Bryonville polygon, alias it to an adjacent polygon (Evanston/East Walnut Hills), or accept as a documented honest-sparse omission. Low impact.
verifyNote: Verified against live CPD feed data.cincinnati-oh.gov/resource/7aqy-xrv9 (the resource the adapter uses, cincinnati-socrata.ts:29). After applying the adapter's numeric-artifact filter (line 158 drops /^\d+$/ labels), the feed yields exactly 52 named neighborhoods; cincinnati.geojson has 51; the single feed-not-in-geo mismatch is O'BRYONVILLE with ZERO orphan polygons. O'Bryonville has 29 incidents with valid coords, so it passes count>=1 and the lat/lng-present guard and will surface in lists/scores but render nothing on the map. Exactly as described. Low severity correct.

title: Dallas uses only 24 coarse compass-sector + inner-city labels, not its ~200 named neighborhoods
evidence: Dallas geocodes each incident's lat/lng into one of 24 internal polygons (geocodeDallas), which area-wise cover ~853 km2 of the ~881 km2 city (near-complete area coverage), but the labels are mostly compass sectors: Far North/North/Northeast/East/Northwest/West/Southwest/South/Southeast Dallas, North/South Oak Cliff, plus a handful of real inner-city neighborhoods (Lakewood, Knox, Uptown, Deep Ellum, Oak Lawn, Downtown, Design District, Fair Park, Lower/Upper/Lowest Greenville, Henderson Avenue). Recognizable areas like Pleasant Grove, Vickery Meadow, Bishop Arts, Preston Hollow, Lake Highlands are absent — folded into sectors. Incidents outside all 24 polygons collapse to 'Unmapped' (line 153). Labels are real (no slugs/artifacts), so this is a granularity limitation, not a data-integrity bug.
recommendation: Optional: expand the bundled Dallas polygon set to include the well-known named neighborhoods currently folded into the compass sectors (Pleasant Grove, Bishop Arts, Lake Highlands, etc.). Acceptable as-is given honest 'Unmapped' bucketing.
verifyNote: Verified. dallas.geojson has exactly 24 named areas: Far North/North/Northeast/East/Old East/Southeast/South/Southwest/West Dallas, North/South Oak Cliff, Lakewood, Knox, Uptown, Oak Lawn, Downtown, Deep Ellum, Design District, Fair Park, Lower/Upper/Lowest Greenville, Henderson Avenue — mostly compass sectors plus inner-city names. Pleasant Grove, Vickery Meadow, Bishop Arts, Preston Hollow, Lake Highlands all absent. dallas-socrata.ts:88-95 geocodeDallas (point-in-polygon) with off-polygon incidents collapsed to 'Unmapped' (the v102 fallback line). Labels are real, granularity limitation not data bug. Low severity correct.

title: Dallas registry bbox is narrower than its own polygon data (west edge clipped)
location: packages/crime-data/src/cities.ts:185-189 (bbox west:-96.99) vs packages/crime-data/src/data/dallas-neighborhoods.ts (Southwest Dallas reaches lng -97.005)
evidence: The Dallas registry bbox is west:-96.99/east:-96.55, but the Southwest Dallas polygon extends to -97.005173 and the geojson bbox is lng -97.005 to -96.542. Points near the far-west Dallas edge fall outside the registry bbox used for geo bounds, so an incident at the city's western fringe could be excluded by bbox-gating before point-in-polygon geocoding.
recommendation: Widen the Dallas bbox west to ~-97.01 and east to ~-96.54 to fully contain the polygon set.
verifyNote: Bbox clip is real: cities.ts:187 Dallas bbox west=-96.99, east=-96.55, but dallas.geojson lng range is -97.005173 to -96.542274 (Southwest Dallas reaches -97.005). So the registry bbox is narrower than the polygon set. CAVEAT on stated mechanism: the registry bbox is NOT used to gate the crime-data adapter pipeline (dallas-socrata.ts geocodeDallas uses each polygon's OWN bbox, which includes -97.005). The registry bbox is consumed by cityFromLatLng() (cities.ts:441) and geo viewbox hints — so a far-west-fringe point fails location→city resolution / reverse geocode rather than being clipped before incident geocoding. The finding's bbox-width claim is accurate; its 'excluded before point-in-polygon geocoding' mechanism is slightly mis-attributed. Net low severity stands.

title: Milwaukee coverage is coarse: ~22 ZIP-collapsed neighborhoods (21 on the map) for a 190-neighborhood, ~590k-population city
evidence: ZIP_NEIGHBORHOOD maps 25 in-city ZIPs to only 22 distinct labels because multiple ZIPs collapse to one name (53206+53216 both -> 'Sherman Park'; 53214+53226 -> 'Story Hill'; 53218+53222 -> 'Capitol Heights'). After dropping the 3 NON_MPD suburbs, the map renders 21 neighborhoods. Major recognizable Milwaukee neighborhoods present in the 190-set (e.g. Harambee, Brewers Hill, Walker Square, Lindsay Heights, Layton Park, Murray Hill, Historic Mitchell Street) are absent because the crime feed is only ZIP-resolved and unmapped ZIPs are dropped (lines 197-198). This is an honest-sparse limitation of ZIP-level data, not a bug, but it materially understates per-neighborhood granularity vs the lat/lng cities.
recommendation: If MPD ever exposes lat/lng or a finer geographic field, geocode via PIP (as Las Vegas does) to recover real neighborhood granularity. Otherwise surface a clear 'ZIP-level grouping' label in the UI so users do not read these as true neighborhood boundaries.
verifyNote: Core confirmed: ZIP_NEIGHBORHOOD has 28 ZIPs; minus 3 NON_MPD = 25 in-city ZIPs collapsing to 22 distinct labels; the 3 named collapses match exactly (Sherman Park 53206+53216, Story Hill 53214+53226, Capitol Heights 53218+53222); 21 render after suburb drop. Unmapped-ZIP drop is at lines 197-198. One factual inaccuracy in the evidence: of the 7 'major neighborhoods present in the 190-set' listed, only 4 (Harambee, Layton Park, Murray Hill, Historic Mitchell Street) are actually in milwaukee-neighborhoods.ts; Brewers Hill, Walker Square, and Lindsay Heights are NOT in the 190-set. This does not change the conclusion (those neighborhoods are still absent from the rendered map and unemittable) and the honest-sparse ZIP-coarseness characterization is correct. Low is appropriate.

title: Boise discover() assigns the same hardcoded city centroid to every neighborhood
evidence: getDiscoveredAreasBoise maps every area to centroid { lat: 43.62, lng: -116.21 } with the inline comment '// city centroid placeholder'. The Boise feed carries no per-row lat/lng, so all ~35 neighborhood centroids collapse to a single point. This is benign for the crime map (which renders by polygon-name join, and the 35 polygon names match the geojson exactly) but breaks any centroid-dependent feature for Boise: nearestArea() haversine snapping (neighborhoods.ts) and any map pin/marker would place every Boise neighborhood at the city center.
recommendation: Populate each Boise area centroid from the matching polygon in boise-neighborhoods.ts (compute bbox-midpoint centroids as cleveland-arcgis.ts already does at lines 14-28) so nearest-area geocoding and pins are correct.
verifyNote: Confirmed exactly. boise-arcgis.ts:145 assigns centroid { lat: 43.62, lng: -116.21 } with comment '// city centroid placeholder' to every discovered area. nearestArea() in neighborhoods.ts:70-74 uses haversine over area.centroid, so it would be broken for Boise (all areas collapse to one point). The crime map joins by polygon name (35 names match the geojson exactly), so the choropleth is unaffected. cleveland-arcgis.ts:14-28 indeed computes bbox-midpoint centroids, so the recommendation is sound. Low is appropriate.

title: Las Vegas and Boise discover() return empty on a cold/failed cache (no static seed), unlike Cleveland and Milwaukee
evidence: Cleveland (cleveland-arcgis.ts:297-307 STATIC_CLEVELAND_AREAS) and Milwaukee (milwaukee-ckan.ts:238-245 STATIC_MILWAUKEE_AREAS) return a bundled static area list when the row cache is cold so /geo/areas?city= never returns [] during the ~30s cold window. Las Vegas and Boise have no such floor: getDiscoveredAreasLasVegas/Boise build solely from getRows*, which returns cache?.rows ?? [] on fetch failure. On a cold container or upstream outage, the picker and per-area lookups for these two cities can briefly return an empty area list (the documented blank-map / 404 cold-window the static seeds were added to fix elsewhere).
recommendation: Add a STATIC_* seed for Las Vegas (from lasVegasPolygons) and Boise (from boiseNeighborhoods) mirroring the Cleveland/Milwaukee pattern so cold-window discover() is never empty.
verifyNote: Confirmed. getRowsLasVegas (las-vegas-arcgis.ts:232) and getRowsBoise (boise-arcgis.ts:124) both return `cache?.rows ?? []` on fetch failure; getDiscoveredAreasLasVegas (236-255) and Boise (128-148) build solely from those rows with a count>=3 filter and have no static seed export. By contrast getDiscoveredAreasCleveland returns STATIC_CLEVELAND_AREAS on cold cache (cleveland-arcgis.ts:297-307) and Milwaukee returns STATIC_MILWAUKEE_AREAS (milwaukee-ckan.ts:244). So LV/Boise can return [] during a cold/failed window. LV does import lasVegasPolygons but only for PIP geocoding (POLY_INDEX line 103), not a cold floor. Low severity is appropriate; the finding's own medium-confidence is reasonable since the empty window is brief.

title: Norfolk exposes organization/admin labels as if they were neighborhoods
evidence: Among Norfolk's 110 polygon/area names several are civic-organization or null labels rather than neighborhood names: "No Civic League", "Downtown Norfolk Civic League", "Freemason Street Area Association", "Ghent Neighborhood League", "Ghent Square Community Assoc.". "No Civic League" in particular is a catch-all bucket for incidents with no assigned neighborhood and will appear as a selectable 'neighborhood' in the picker and as a hotspot. Also note minor source typos carried verbatim: "Miltary Circle" (Military), "Diggs Town" (Diggstown), "Greenwood/elmhurst/norview Heights" (mixed-case un-title-cased segment).
recommendation: Filter/relabel "No Civic League" (treat as Unmapped, like KC's Unmapped bucket) and either accept the civic-league org names or strip the "Civic League/Association/Neighborhood League" suffixes for display; title-case-fix the "Greenwood/elmhurst" and "Miltary Circle" entries.
verifyNote: All cited names present in apps/web/public/geo/norfolk.geojson: 'No Civic League', 'Downtown Norfolk Civic League', 'Freemason Street Area Association', 'Ghent Neighborhood League', 'Ghent Square Community Assoc.', plus typos 'Miltary Circle', 'Diggs Town', and 'Greenwood/elmhurst/...'. norfolk-socrata.ts derives area from feed neighborhd (title-cased) and only filters out UNKNOWN — not 'No Civic League' — so these org/null labels can appear as selectable neighborhoods and hotspots. Low severity confirmed.

title: Fort Worth: 6 'FWPD ... Division' patrol-zone polygons are selectable as if they were neighborhoods
evidence: fort-worth.geojson has 390 named features = 384 neighborhood associations + 6 fallback polygons named 'FWPD Central Division', 'FWPD East Division', 'FWPD South Division', 'FWPD North Division', 'FWPD Northwest Division', 'FWPD West Division'. geocodeFortWorth (line 113-115) assigns incidents outside any registered association to the division polygon, and getDiscoveredAreasFortWorth (line 198-217) emits those division names as KnownAreas. A user can therefore select 'FWPD East Division' as an 'area' — a patrol-zone label, not a neighborhood. This is intentional (header comment lines 11-18) and the names are honest, but they read as system/jargon labels rather than places.
recommendation: Either relabel the division fallbacks to a user-facing form (e.g. 'East Fort Worth (patrol area)') or visually distinguish them in the area picker so users understand a 'FWPD ... Division' entry is a coarse catch-all, not a named neighborhood.
verifyNote: Verified. fort-worth.geojson = 390 features = 384 associations + exactly 6 division polygons named 'FWPD Central/East/South/North/Northwest/West Division' (enumerated from the file). geocodeFortWorth = inPolys(...,NBHD_INDEX) ?? inPolys(...,DIV_INDEX) so incidents outside associations fall to division polygons; getDiscoveredAreasFortWorth (198-217) emits any area with count>=3 as a KnownArea, making division names selectable. Honest patrol-zone labels but read as jargon. Low severity correct.

title: Indianapolis and Tucson leak a synthetic "Unmapped" area into the area catalog
evidence: Both adapters bucket off-polygon / no-neighborhood incidents into the literal string "Unmapped" (indy line 139 `const area = nbhd ?? "Unmapped"`; tucson line 175 `(r.NHA_NAME && r.NHA_NAME.trim()) || "Unmapped"`). getDiscoveredAreasIndianapolis filters only `r.area === "Unknown"` and `"Excluded"` (lines 171, 176) and getDiscoveredAreasTucson filters only "Unknown" and purely-numeric labels (lines 205, 210) — neither excludes "Unmapped". So a non-place bucket named "Unmapped" (slug indy-unmapped / tuc-unmapped) surfaces as a selectable area in the neighborhood wheel and per-area stats. It is harmless on the choropleth map (no matching polygon -> dropped by the orphan filter) but is a user-visible fake neighborhood in the picker/stats.
recommendation: Add `if (r.area === "Unmapped") continue;` to both discovery loops (mirror the existing "Excluded"/numeric filters). The collapse-into-Unmapped behavior for the underlying rows is fine; it just should not appear as a discoverable area.
verifyNote: Verified indianapolis-arcgis.ts:139 `const area = nbhd ?? "Unmapped"` and tucson-arcgis.ts:175 `(r.NHA_NAME && r.NHA_NAME.trim()) || "Unmapped"`. getDiscoveredAreasIndianapolis filters r.area==="Unknown" (171) and "Excluded" (176) but not "Unmapped"; getDiscoveredAreasTucson filters "Unknown" (205) and purely-numeric (210) but not "Unmapped". Both require lat/lng != null (indy:177, tuc:211), and an Unmapped row has coords (it failed polygon containment, not geometry) so it passes the count filter and surfaces as slug indy-unmapped / tuc-unmapped in the catalog/picker. Low is correct.

title: Raleigh coverage is only 6 RPD districts (no real neighborhoods), capped by feed granularity
evidence: The Raleigh adapter groups incidents by the RPD `district` field (raleigh-arcgis.ts:87 `area: r.district`), and raleigh.geojson contains exactly 6 cardinal-district polygons: Downtown, North, Northeast, Northwest, Southeast, Southwest. There are no real recognizable Raleigh neighborhoods (e.g. Five Points, Cameron Village, Oakwood, North Hills) — only police districts. This is consistent with the project-memory note that Raleigh is permanently capped at 6 districts due to ~36% null-island coordinates in the feed, but it is a stark coverage gap relative to Atlanta (242), Tucson (154) and Indianapolis (99). The 6 polygons themselves are valid (no bad coords, proper bbox).
recommendation: Accept as an honest feed limitation but surface it to users (the district labels read like compass directions, not neighborhoods). If a higher-resolution source exists (Raleigh open-data has a Police Beats / CAC neighborhood layer), consider point-in-polygon geocoding like Indianapolis to lift Raleigh above district level; otherwise label these clearly as "police districts" in the UI rather than "neighborhoods".
verifyNote: Verified raleigh-arcgis.ts groups by `area: r.district!` (line ~88, header comment lines 10-11 confirm 'group by RPD district (~6)'). Parsed apps/web/public/geo/raleigh.geojson: exactly 6 features, names Downtown/North/Northeast/Northwest/Southeast/Southwest (compass directions, not neighborhoods), 36956 coords, 0 bad. Stark coverage gap vs Atlanta 242 / Tucson 154 / Indy 99 confirmed. Consistent with the project-memory cap note. Low is correct.

title: Honolulu polygon coverage ~76%: 29 of 121 discovered neighborhoods have no boundary polygon
