recommendation: No action required for honesty — unmatched areas are small/edge places (Hickam AFB, Red Hill, apartment complexes, Kalaeloa) and their incidents still count citywide per adapter design. Optionally add polygons for the larger real ones (Iwilei, Saint Louis Heights, Sunset Beach, Villages of Kapolei) to lift map coverage.
evidence: normName exact+substring match (mirroring CrimeMap.tsx join) yields 92/121 = 76%. Unmatched include real but small/odd areas: Hickam, Red Hill, Iwilei, Saint Louis Heights, Sunset Beach, Villages of Kapolei, Iroquois Point, plus apartment-complex labels (Kulana Apartments, Hahaione High Rise) and duplicate diacritic spellings (Waipiʻo Acres vs Waipio Acres, Kapaeloa vs Kamoʻoloa). Discovered names themselves are real recognizable Honolulu neighborhoods (Waikīkī, Kalihi, Chinatown, Mānoa, Kaimukī).
verifyNote: Qualitative claim confirmed but the specific numbers do not reproduce. honolulu.geojson has 83 polygons (matches). The discovery JSON neighborhoodCount is 122, not 121. Replicating a normName match yields 88 matched / 34 unmatched = 72%, not 92/29/76% as claimed. The finding's methodology also does not mirror the ACTUAL CrimeMap join, which is polygon->adapter-area (CrimeMap.tsx:318-331, exact then substring, plus a NAME_ALIASES table at line 114) over adapter-emitted areas, NOT discovered-JSON-names->polygons. However the substantive point holds: Honolulu polygon coverage is incomplete (~72-76%), several real neighborhoods (Iwilei, Saint Louis Heights, Sunset Beach, Villages of Kapolei, Hickam, Red Hill) lack polygons, and unmapped addresses still count citywide (honolulu-socrata.ts:91 folds them into the 'Honolulu' bucket). Low severity correct; numbers imprecise.

evidence: grep for "hnl-", "lb-", "atx-" keys in neighborhood-populations-generated.ts returns 0/0/0; only "phoenix" (and other cities) present. title: Virginia Beach areas are 333 micro-subdivisions, missing recognizable district names users search for
location: apps/web/public/geo/virginia-beach.geojson (333 features); adapter discover at virginia-beach-arcgis.ts:183 admits areas at count>=1
evidence: VB's areas are platted subdivisions (Morgan`s Walke, Brigand`s Quay, Brinkley Estates; ~37% carry subdivision suffixes Estates/Quay/Walke/Pines/Woods/Manor/Court). Major recognizable VB districts are MISSING as searchable areas: no 'Oceanfront', 'Town Center', 'Bayside', or 'Chesapeake Beach'. Recognizable districts that do appear are split into sub-subdivisions (e.g. 'Kempsville Manor'/'Kempsville Gardens' but no district-level 'Kempsville'; 'Lynnhaven Shores'/'Lynnhaven Woods' but no 'Lynnhaven'). The discover filter is count>=1 (vs Jacksonville count>=10, Gainesville count>=3), so single-incident micro-subdivisions flood the browsable catalog. Names are real (not artifacts), so this is a recognizability/usability gap, not a data-integrity bug.
recommendation: Consider raising VB's discover min-count above 1 to suppress single-incident micro-areas, and/or adding a district-rollup layer (Oceanfront/Town Center/Bayside/Kempsville/Lynnhaven) so users can browse by recognizable VB districts rather than only platted subdivisions.
verifyNote: Discover filter is count>=1 at virginia-beach-arcgis.ts:183, vs jacksonville MIN_AREA_INCIDENTS=10 (line 36/206) and gainesville count>=3 (line 231) — threshold comparison accurate. geojson has 333 features that are platted subdivisions (Morgan`s Walke, Brigand`s Quay, Brinkley Estates). Verified recognizable district names are absent: 'Oceanfront','Town Center','Bayside','Chesapeake Beach','Kempsville','Lynnhaven' all 0 matches, while sub-subdivisions exist (Kempsville Manor/Gardens/Lakes/Crossing, Lynnhaven Shores/Woods/Colony/Forest). Names are real (usability/recognizability gap, not data-integrity). The '~37% carry subdivision suffixes' figure is imprecise (exact suffix-ending match is 54/333≈16%; broader 'contains' would be higher) but is non-load-bearing color. Low severity correct.

Gainesville comment (line 419) says 'point-in-polygon to 12 GPD patrol zones' but the adapter PIPs against 114 named neighborhoods (gainesville-neighborhoods.ts header: 'replacing the old GPD patrol call-signs', geojson has 114). Implementations are modern/correct; comments are stale. Low severity correct.

title: TOTP shared secret (mfaSecret) stored in plaintext at rest
evidence: verifyAndEnableMfa persists the raw base32 secret directly (mfa.service.ts:38-39: data:{ mfaSecret: secret }) and disable/verify read it back verbatim. Anyone with read access to the User table (DB dump, SQL injection elsewhere, backup leak) obtains every user's TOTP seed and can generate valid codes, neutralizing the second factor.
recommendation: Encrypt mfaSecret at rest with an app-managed key (e.g. AES-GCM with a KMS/env key separate from JWT_SECRET) and decrypt only in-memory for verification.
verifyNote: Verified: mfaSecret is String? (schema.prisma:119) and verifyAndEnableMfa persists the raw base32 secret (apps/api/src/services/mfa.service.ts:37-40); disable/verify read it verbatim. Anyone with User-table read access obtains every TOTP seed. No encryption-at-rest. Low is reasonable (requires existing DB read access). Confirmed.

title: Single JWT_SECRET signs access, refresh, and mfa-pending tokens (type-tag disambiguation only)
evidence: signAccessToken/signRefreshToken/signMfaPendingToken all call jwt.sign(..., env.JWT_SECRET) and differ only by a typ claim (jwt.ts:27,32,40). Type confusion is currently prevented by the typ check (verifySession expectType, verifyMfaPendingToken typ===mfa_pending), but a single secret means one leak compromises all token classes, and any future handler that forgets to pass expectType accepts a refresh or mfa-pending token where it should not. Note also the web verifySession (web/jwt.ts:30-40) does not enforce typ at all, so a web route would accept an Express refresh/mfa-pending token as a session.
recommendation: Use separate signing secrets per token purpose (or at minimum keep enforcing expectType on every protected route and have the web verifier reject non-access typ tokens). The JWT_EXPIRES_IN env on the API (api/env.ts:19) is also declared-but-unused — remove to avoid the false impression it controls TTL.
verifyNote: Verified: signAccessToken/signRefreshToken/signMfaPendingToken all sign with env.JWT_SECRET, differing only by typ (apps/api/src/lib/jwt.ts:27,32,40). The web verifySession (apps/web/src/server/lib/jwt.ts:30-40) does NOT enforce typ at all, and both backends share JWT_SECRET — so a web protected route would accept an API-issued refresh or mfa-pending token as a valid session. JWT_EXPIRES_IN is declared on the API (env.ts:19) but never read by jwt.ts (which hardcodes ACCESS_TTL/REFRESH_TTL) — confirmed dead/misleading. Low is correct (type-confusion currently mitigated on the API side by expectType, but the cross-backend web gap and single-secret blast radius are real).

title: Public share-token view leaks the sharer's internal user id (userId cuid) to any token holder
evidence: resolveSharedView returns { expiresAt, userId } and GET /share/:token res.json()s it directly with no auth. The web type confirms it is consumed verbatim: interface ShareView { expiresAt: string; userId: string }. Anyone holding a share link receives the sharer's stable internal User.id. It is not location/PII directly, but it is an internal object identifier that should not cross the trust boundary; it enables correlating multiple share links to the same user and is unnecessary for rendering the shared view.
recommendation: Do not return userId from the public token endpoint. Resolve any needed display data server-side (e.g., a non-correlating display name or nothing) and return only what the share UI must render (expiry, and the live position once that is wired in).
verifyNote: Verified. live-share.service.ts:53 returns { expiresAt, userId: link.userId }; share.routes.ts:18 res.json()s it with no auth; web share/[token]/page.tsx:6 type ShareView includes userId. Internal User.id cuid crosses the public token boundary, enabling cross-link correlation. The web page (lines 18-34) only renders expiresAt and never uses userId, confirming it is unnecessary to return. Low is appropriate — internal identifier leak, not direct PII/location.

title: User-supplied area string reaches SANDAG SoQL $where via raw string interpolation (escaped, but the only raw-interpolation site)
location: packages/crime-data/src/adapters/sandag-socrata.ts:137 (getAreaStats), reachable from apps/api/src/routes/crime-data.routes.ts /area-stats via resolveArea() -> dispatcher.ts:90-95 SANDAG fallback
evidence: sandag-socrata.ts:132-138: `async getAreaStats(area){ const known=findArea(area); const jurisdiction = known?.jurisdiction ?? area; const rows = await sodaGet({ $where: `jurisdiction='${jurisdiction.replace(/'/g,"''")}'` ... })`. When findArea(area) misses, the RAW user value from ?neighborhood= (validated only as z.string()) becomes `jurisdiction` and is interpolated into the SoQL string. dispatcher.getAreaStats falls back to sandagSocrataAdapter for any area routed to San Diego (cityForArea default prefix is ""), so an unmatched ?neighborhood= reaches this. The `.replace(/'/g,"''")` doubles single quotes, which IS the correct SoQL string-literal escape, so quote-breakout is blocked and this is not currently exploitable. It is flagged because it is the only adapter doing raw query interpolation (every other adapter interpolates only a server-computed date cutoff, e.g. nypd-socrata.ts:225), the escape is a single fragile inline regex with no test, and the value is fully attacker-controlled.
recommendation: Do not interpolate user input into SoQL at all: when findArea(area) misses, return null instead of falling back to the raw `area` as `jurisdiction` (an unknown area has no SANDAG jurisdiction row anyway). If interpolation must stay, centralize SoQL string-literal escaping in lib/http.ts alongside socrataDate() and add a unit test for the quote-doubling.
verifyNote: Verified end-to-end. sandag-socrata.ts:135-138 interpolates `jurisdiction` into a SoQL $where: `jurisdiction='${jurisdiction.replace(/'/g,"''")}'`, and when findArea(area) misses, jurisdiction = the raw `area`. Reachability confirmed: crime-data.routes.ts areaQuery declares `neighborhood: z.string().optional()` (NO max length), resolveArea returns it verbatim, dispatcher.getAreaStats calls cityForArea(area) which (cities.ts:491-540) returns CITIES[0]=San Diego (sdpdNibrsAdapter) for ANY unmatched slug; SDPD returns null for an unknown area, then dispatcher.ts:90-95 falls back to sandagSocrataAdapter with the raw area. The `.replace(/'/g,"''")` IS the correct SoQL string-literal escape so quote-breakout is blocked — not currently exploitable, hence low. One imprecision in the finding's wording: it says the 'default prefix is empty', but the actual mechanism is a `return CITIES[0]` default at the end of cityForArea, not a prefix match; conclusion unaffected. It is genuinely the only adapter doing raw user-value interpolation (others interpolate only server-computed date cutoffs). Severity low is correct.

title: GET /ai/area-brief does not length-validate the `area` query param
evidence: `const area = typeof req.query.area === "string" ? req.query.area : ""; if (!area) return 400;` — there is no max-length cap, unlike the sibling /ai/incident-summary (summaryQuery: z.object({ area: z.string().min(1).max(120) ...})) at ai.routes.ts:86-90 and crime-data areaQuery (neighborhood capped). The unbounded string flows into generateAreaBrief(area) -> getCrimeMix(area) and into a Redis cache key (area-brief.service.ts cache-key prefix) and the LLM grounding lookup. Express json/query limits cap the absolute size, but an oversized/odd area string can bloat the Redis key namespace and is an inconsistency with every other text endpoint.
recommendation: Validate with z.string().min(1).max(120) the same way incident-summary and crime-data do, to keep the cache-key and downstream lookup bounded.
verifyNote: Verified at ai.routes.ts:57-59: `const area = typeof req.query.area === "string" ? req.query.area : ""; if (!area) return 400;` — no max-length cap. Confirmed the sibling /ai/incident-summary uses summaryQuery = z.object({ area: z.string().min(1).max(120).optional() ... }) (ai.routes.ts:86-90) and crime-data areaQuery caps neighborhood... (actually neighborhood is uncapped too per inj-1, but city/offense are capped). The unbounded area flows into generateAreaBrief(area). Real inconsistency with the capped sibling; low severity appropriate.

title: Live FBI Crime Data Explorer API key present in untracked local .env (not committed, but a real credential on disk)
evidence: /Users/damiengantt-mcdade/TravelSafe/.env contains `FBI_CDE_API_KEY=VICEHOhczYXfu9yeJQ49WdVf29iEj2iV23vhcqSW`. `git check-ignore .env` confirms it is gitignored and `git ls-files --error-unmatch .env` confirms it is NOT tracked, and `git log --all --diff-filter=A` shows no .env file ever entered history. Treat this as a reminder: api.data.gov keys are free and per-developer — rotate it if this machine/disk image is ever shared, and keep it out of any shell history or screen-share. Confirm CI/build systems inject it via secret store rather than a checked-in file.
verifyNote: Verified directly: /Users/damiengantt-mcdade/TravelSafe/.env line 3 contains FBI_CDE_API_KEY=VICEHOhczYXfu9yeJQ49WdVf29iEj2iV23vhcqSW. git check-ignore .env returns 0 (ignored); git ls-files --error-unmatch .env errors (not tracked); git history shows only .env.example and apps/web/.env.example were ever added, never .env itself. title: Cron/diag Bearer-secret comparison is not constant-time
evidence: requireCronSecret() compares the incoming Authorization header to the expected value with JS `===`, which short-circuits on the first differing byte and is therefore timing-variable. The guard is otherwise well-built: fails closed with 503 when CRON_SECRET is unset, returns 401 on mismatch, and only reads the Authorization header (the prior ?secret= query-string debug path was explicitly removed to stop CDN-log/Referer leakage). Practical exploitability is very low over a network (jitter dwarfs the signal) and the secret is high-entropy.
recommendation: Use crypto.timingSafeEqual on equal-length buffers for the secret comparison to eliminate the theoretical timing side channel. Optional hardening only — current behavior is acceptable.
verifyNote: bearer-auth.ts:27 compares header with `header === `Bearer ${env.CRON_SECRET}`` — plain JS === , not constant-time. Guard fails closed (503 on unset secret, line 23-25) and 401 on mismatch. The ?secret= query path was removed (comment lines 11-17). Network timing exploitability is negligible against a high-entropy secret; low/theoretical-hardening rating is accurate.

title: Critical vitest advisory (CVSS 9.8 RCE) present but confined to dev test tooling
evidence: npm audit flags vitest <4.1.0 critical: GHSA-5xrq-8626-4rwp "When Vitest UI server is listening, arbitrary file can be read and executed" (CVSS 9.8). Installed version is 2.1.9. However vitest is a devDependency only (apps/api, no prod dep), and the RCE requires the Vitest UI server (`--ui`/`--api`) to be listening, which is not used in CI or production. It does not ship to the Railway API or Vercel web runtime. The fix is a semver-major bump to vitest 4.1.8 (drags vite/esbuild/@vitest/mocker with it).
recommendation: Bump vitest to 4.1.8 (also clears the moderate vite path-traversal GHSA-4w7w-66w2-5vf9, vite-node, @vitest/mocker, and esbuild GHSA-67mh-4wv8-2f99). Never run `vitest --ui`/`--api` on a shared or networked host. Low production risk; prioritize as hygiene.
verifyNote: Verified: node_modules/vitest is version 2.1.9; apps/api declares devDependencies.vitest ^2.1.4 (line 62), not in any prod dependencies block. npm audit reports vitest critical GHSA-5xrq-8626-4rwp CVSS 9.8 (range <4.1.0), fix is semver-major vitest 4.1.8. No `vitest --ui`/`--api` usage found in package scripts or CI. Dev-only, RCE requires UI server which is not run; low severity is correct.

title: Stale nested postcss 8.4.31 in next's build pipeline (XSS advisory)
evidence: npm audit: postcss <8.5.10 moderate GHSA-qx2v-qp2m-jg93 "PostCSS has XSS via Unescaped </style> in its CSS Stringify Output" (CVSS 6.1). next ships a pinned nested postcss 8.4.31. This is the ONLY flagged advisory that resides in a production-runtime dependency tree (next, a direct prod dep of apps/web). Exploitability is minimal: postcss is a build-time CSS transformer here (not processing untrusted user CSS at runtime), so the stringify-XSS path is not reachable by end users. audit's suggested 'fix' is a bogus semver-major downgrade of next to 9.3.3 and should be ignored.
recommendation: Bump next to the latest 15.x patch (pulls a postcss >=8.5.10 in its bundled tree) on the next routine dependency refresh. Do not apply `npm audit fix --force` (it would downgrade next to 9.3.3). Low priority.
verifyNote: Verified: next 15.5.18 ships node_modules/next/node_modules/postcss 8.4.31; audit flags postcss GHSA-qx2v-qp2m-jg93 moderate CVSS 6.1 (range <8.5.10) via next. next is a direct prod dependency of apps/web (deps.next ^15.0.3). It is the only flagged advisory in a prod-runtime tree. audit's fixAvailable is next 9.3.3 (semver-major downgrade) — bogus, confirmed. Build-time-only reachability; low is correct.

title: Local toolchain Node v26 diverges from declared engines 22.x / .node-version 22
evidence: Root package.json pins engines.node "22.x" and .node-version is 22, but the local audit shell reports node v26.0.0. No `engine-strict` is set, so npm only warns (EBADENGINE) rather than failing; native modules with install scripts (sharp 0.34.5, @prisma/engines, esbuild, unrs-resolver) compile/select prebuilds against the local ABI, so a v26 dev machine can produce node_modules that differ from the Node 22 production runtime (Railway/Vercel). This is a reproducibility/supply-chain-integrity gap, not a known CVE.
recommendation: Develop and run `npm ci` on Node 22 to match production, or add `engine-strict=true` in .npmrc to fail fast on mismatched engines. Ensure CI installs on Node 22 so the committed lockfile's native-binary selections match the deployed runtime.
verifyNote: Verified: root package.json engines.node "22.x" (line 48); .node-version is "22"; local node -v reports v26.0.0. No .npmrc exists at repo root, so no engine-strict — npm only warns on engine mismatch. Native install-script modules (sharp, @prisma/engines, esbuild, unrs-resolver) compile/select prebuilds against local ABI, so a v26 dev machine can produce node_modules diverging from the Node 22 production runtime. Reproducibility/supply-chain-integrity gap, no CVE; low severity appropriate (finding self-rated low; medium confidence claim is reasonable).

title: Public GET /share/:token has no per-token rate limit, unlike the analogous /contacts/confirm/:token
evidence: shareRouter.get("/:token", ...) is mounted with only the global 600/min/IP limiter. The token-in-URL pattern is identical to /contacts/confirm/:token, which DOES apply tokenLimiter (10/min keyed by req.params.token). rate-limit.ts:40-46 comment explicitly names 'share redemption' as a target for tokenLimiter, but it was never wired onto the share route. Tokens are crypto.randomBytes(20)=160-bit base64url so brute-force is infeasible; impact is enumeration-throttling defense-in-depth and consistency.
recommendation: Apply tokenLimiter to shareRouter.get("/:token") for parity with /contacts/confirm/:token.
verifyNote: Verified at apps/api/src/routes/share.routes.ts:15 — shareRouter.get("/:token") applies only Zod token-shape validation, no tokenLimiter. By contrast apps/api/src/routes/contacts.routes.ts:55 wires tokenLimiter onto POST /confirm/:token, and rate-limit.ts:33-35 comment explicitly names 'share redemption' as a tokenLimiter target. Tokens are crypto.randomBytes(20)=base64url so brute-force is infeasible; this is consistency/defense-in-depth only. Low is correct.

title: Several authenticated state-changing writes rely only on the 600/min global limiter (no writeLimiter)
evidence: writeLimiter (30/min/IP) is applied to most writes (POST /contacts, /safety/*, /push/subscribe, /community/posts/:id/comment) but is missing on DELETE /contacts/:id, PUT /preferences/alerts, POST /community/posts/:id/react, and POST /moderation/posts/:id/review. These fall back to the 600/min global cap. All require requireAuth (review requires requireModerator), so abuse needs a valid session and blast radius is per-user data, but coverage is inconsistent with sibling routes.
recommendation: Add writeLimiter to these authenticated write handlers for consistent throttling (reaction spam, preference thrash). Low priority given auth + global cap.
verifyNote: Verified all four: DELETE /contacts/:id (contacts.routes.ts:43) and POST /confirm aside, PUT /preferences/alerts (preferences.routes.ts:30), POST /community/posts/:id/react (community.routes.ts:93), and POST /moderation/posts/:id/review (moderation.routes.ts:77) all lack writeLimiter, while sibling writes (POST /contacts:26, /resend:35, /push/subscribe, /community comment:112, moderation report:97/block:106/mute:123) do apply it. All require requireAuth (review additionally requireModerator). Falls back to 600/min global. Low is correct.

title: Enforcing CSP keeps script-src 'unsafe-inline', weakening XSS containment
evidence: CSP is enforcing and 'unsafe-eval' was correctly removed, but script-src retains 'unsafe-inline' ('script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' ' + AdSense origins). The code documents the reason honestly (Next.js hydration writes inline bootstrap scripts that can't be nonced without a fork). With 'unsafe-inline' present, an injected inline <script> would execute, so the CSP does not meaningfully mitigate script-injection XSS. img-src/connect-src/frame-src also broaden to several Google AdSense origins.
recommendation: Move to nonce/hash-based script-src using Next.js App Router CSP nonces so 'unsafe-inline' can be dropped. Until then rely on React output-encoding as the primary XSS control.
verifyNote: Verified next.config.ts:82 `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' ${ADSENSE_ORIGINS}` inside CSP_ENFORCING, set as enforcing Content-Security-Policy header (line 101). 'unsafe-eval' is removed; img/connect/frame-src do broaden to the 5 AdSense origins. With 'unsafe-inline' present, injected inline script would execute, so CSP does not meaningfully block script-injection XSS. Reasoning (Next.js hydration inline bootstrap) matches code comment. Low is correct.

title: API /health leaks internal brand name and infra/runtime details
evidence: GET /health returns: {"ok":true,"service":"travelsafe-api","prismaMajor":7,"time":...,"buildSha":"unknown","bootedAt":"2026-06-02T02:56:49.290Z","heap":{"usedMB":301,"totalMB":418,"rssMB":996},"cache":{"registered":44,"highWaterMB":1700,"evictions":0,...},"compute":{"max":6,...}}. This is reachable unauthenticated and skips the global rate limiter. It confirms the public brand split (service name 'travelsafe-api' vs user-facing 'CommunitySafe', site <title>CommunitySafe</title>) and discloses Prisma major version, heap/RSS, boot time, and cache/compute internals useful for fingerprinting.
recommendation: Reduce the public /health payload to a minimal liveness response ({ok:true}); move heap/cache/compute/prismaMajor telemetry behind auth or an internal-only path. Rename the service id to the public brand ('communitysafe-api') for consistency, or accept the divergence as intentional but stop exposing it unauthenticated.
verifyNote: GET /health (and /healthz) returns unauthenticated {service:'travelsafe-api', prismaMajor:7, bootedAt, heap{usedMB/totalMB/rssMB}, cache{registered:44,highWaterMB:1700,evictions}, compute{max:6}}. Source apps/api/src/index.ts:272-301 confirms the payload and dual routes; rate-limit.ts:30 confirms globalLimiter explicitly skips /health and /healthz. Brand split real: API service id 'travelsafe-api' vs web <title>CommunitySafe</title>. Low severity (info-disclosure/fingerprinting) is correct.

title: Health endpoints expose operational telemetry unauthenticated (heap/RSS/cache/compute, commit SHA)
evidence: GET /health (public, skips rate limiter) returns heap.usedMB/totalMB/rssMB:913, cache.registered:44/highWaterMB:1700/evictions, compute.max:6/activeCities/queued, and bootedAt. The Vercel /api/health returns commit:"8bc0a9b" and db check latency. This reveals memory-pressure thresholds and the exact deploy commit to an unauthenticated attacker, useful for timing/OOM-pressure reconnaissance (project memory documents prior OOM crashes).
recommendation: Keep a minimal public liveness probe ({ok:true}) and move the detailed heap/cache/compute/commit telemetry behind auth or an internal-only path (e.g. require a shared header or restrict to internal network). At minimum drop the commit SHA from the public web /api/health.
verifyNote: Reproduced live: Railway /health (public, skips globalLimiter per rate-limit.ts:30) returns heap.usedMB/totalMB/rssMB:1092, cache.registered:44/highWaterMB:1700/evictions, compute.max:6/activeCities/queued, bootedAt (index.ts:268-299). Vercel /api/health returns commit:8bc0a9b + db latency (web health route.ts:31-38). Telemetry numbers drifted from the original probe (rss 913->1092) but substance holds. One nuance: Railway buildSha currently reads 'unknown' (RAILWAY_GIT_COMMIT_SHA unset), so the exposed commit SHA is specifically the Vercel one (8bc0a9b). Low severity appropriate.

title: Public token-in-URL endpoint /share/:token lacks a token-scoped rate limiter
evidence: GET /share/aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa returned HTTP 404 {"error":"not_found"} with header ratelimit: limit=600 (the global limiter), not the 10/min token-scoped tokenLimiter that protects /contacts/confirm/:token. A token-in-URL guess endpoint protected only by 600/min/IP allows materially more enumeration attempts than the analogous confirm endpoint.
recommendation: Apply tokenLimiter (or a similarly tight per-token/per-IP limiter) to GET /share/:token to match the protection on /contacts/confirm/:token, reducing share-token brute-force surface. Tokens are 20-64 char base64url so guessing is impractical, hence low severity, but the inconsistency is a real gap.
verifyNote: Reproduced live: GET /share/aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa returned 404 with header ratelimit: limit=600 (globalLimiter), NOT the 10/min tokenLimiter. Code confirms the gap: share.routes.ts mounts the GET /:token handler with no tokenLimiter, whereas contacts.routes.ts:55 applies tokenLimiter to POST /confirm/:token. Real, inconsistent protection. Low severity justified since tokens are 20-64 char base64url (192-bit) and practically unguessable.

title: Brand/legal-entity inconsistency confirmed live: travelsafe internals vs CommunitySafe UI vs cyberwaveglobal.com emails
location: https://communitysafe.app/api/health (service:"travelsafe-web") and Railway /health (service:"travelsafe-api") vs CommunitySafe page titles
evidence: Live /api/health returns service:"travelsafe-web"; Railway /health returns service:"travelsafe-api"; while all user-facing metadata/titles say "CommunitySafe" (404 page <title>Page not found - CommunitySafe</title>, og:site_name CommunitySafe). Recon also found adapter provenance disclaimers branded CommunitySafe inside the @travelsafe package and entity emails @cyberwaveglobal.com. For a safety product making FBI-data and methodology claims, a public brand/entity split can create legal/trust exposure (whose privacy policy / data controller applies).
recommendation: Reconcile the public-facing brand and legal entity: ensure the named data controller in Terms/Privacy matches the operating entity, and consider normalizing service identifiers. Confirm the CyberWave Technologies LLC vs cyberwaveglobal.com naming is intentional and consistently documented in legal pages.
verifyNote: Reproduced live: /api/health service='travelsafe-web', Railway /health service='travelsafe-api'; all user-facing brand is CommunitySafe. Code corroborates: web health route.ts:31, api index.ts:274, adapter disclaimers say 'CommunitySafe' (e.g. packages/crime-data/src/adapters/nypd-socrata.ts:92), and legal/footer name the operator 'CyberWave Technologies LLC' with info@cyberwaveglobal.com (apps/web/src/app/(app)/layout.tsx:94, (legal)/privacy/page.tsx:21). Brand/entity split is real; low severity (trust/legal-hygiene, not exploitable).

title: Full-resolution coordinates persisted with no precision reduction; client never coarsens before upload
evidence: The browser GeolocationPosition latitude/longitude are sent at full device precision with no toFixed/rounding (safety/page.tsx:455-456 and 567-568), and the server stores them as-is and re-emits them in Google Maps q= links. This is disclosed in the privacy policy (apps/web/src/app/(legal)/privacy/page.tsx:63 'the last latitude/longitude you shared to that timer'), so it is honest, but exact lat/lng pinpoints a residence/person to <1m. For a personal-safety product the conventional posture is to coarsen stored location (e.g. ~3-4 decimals / block level) unless full precision is operationally required.
recommendation: Consider rounding stored/forwarded coordinates to ~4 decimal places (≈11m) for check-in last-known-location, which is more than enough for 'where were they' while reducing the precision of any leaked/breached record. SOS may keep finer precision by design, but document that choice.
verifyNote: Verified. apps/web/src/app/(app)/safety/page.tsx:455-456 (and 567-568) assign pos.coords.latitude/longitude with no toFixed/rounding and POST them raw; server stores as-is and re-emits in maps q= links. Privacy disclosure confirmed at apps/web/src/app/(legal)/privacy/page.tsx:63 ('the last latitude/longitude you shared to that timer'). This is an honest privacy-posture hardening note, not a bug. Low severity correct.

title: Per-area numerator loop bounds the upper edge with nowMsArea while citywide and windowStart use anchorMsArea — harmless today but a latent inconsistency
evidence: The per-area window is anchored to the data's newest row for lagged feeds: `anchorMsArea = (nowMsArea - dataLatestMsArea > 14d) ? dataLatestMsArea : nowMsArea` and `windowStartMsArea = anchorMsArea - 365d`. The citywide totals loop correctly bounds rows by `t < windowStartMsArea || t > anchorMsArea` (:1361), but the per-area neighborhood loop uses `t < windowStartMsArea || t > nowMsArea` (:1420). Because dataLatestMsArea IS the maximum non-future timestamp, there are zero rows in (anchorMsArea, nowMsArea] so the count is unaffected in practice — but the two loops disagree on the upper bound, which is fragile if the anchor logic ever changes.
recommendation: Change :1420 to `if (t < windowStartMsArea || t > anchorMsArea) continue;` so the numerator window matches the citywide denominator window exactly. Pure cleanup; no current behavior change.
verifyNote: Reproduced. anchorMsArea = (dataLatestMsArea>0 && nowMsArea-dataLatestMsArea>14d) ? dataLatestMsArea : nowMsArea (:1348); citywide totals loop bounds rows by `t > anchorMsArea` (:1361); per-area numerator loop bounds by `t > nowMsArea` (:1420) — the two loops disagree on the upper edge. dataLatestMsArea is the max non-future ts across the citywide fan-out (:1330-1338, future-clamped at :1336), so areas from that fan-out have no rows in (anchorMsArea, nowMsArea] and counts are unaffected today. Note: when idx<0 the area is fetched separately (:1377) and was NOT part of dataLatestMsArea, so such an area COULD have a row in (anchorMsArea, nowMsArea] that the per-area loop includes but the citywide loop excludes — a genuine (rare) divergence, reinforcing the latent-inconsistency finding. Low severity / cleanup is correct.

title: lookupAreaKm2 substring matcher can bind a small area's label to a larger polygon, inflating population and softening the grade; only the low side is floored
evidence: lookupAreaKm2 does exact-normName match then a bidirectional substring fallback (`n.includes(target) || target.includes(n)`) returning the FIRST hit, not the best/closest. The per-area pop is `Math.max(polygonAreaPop, peerSharePop)` (:1510), which floors the LOW side (protects against micro-polygon rate explosions) but does NOT cap the HIGH side. If a short/micro neighborhood label substring-matches a larger polygon name, it can receive that larger polygon's km^2 -> larger population -> deflated per-100k -> a falsely lenient grade. The codebase already maintains AREA_ALIASES on the client to paper over name divergences, evidence that name collisions are real; the server matcher has no such guard and no high-side cap.
recommendation: Tighten lookupAreaKm2 to prefer the closest-length normName match (or require token-boundary alignment) instead of first substring hit, and consider capping polygon-derived population at a multiple of peerSharePop for safety. Lower priority since curated pops bypass this and most areas match exactly.
verifyNote: Reproduced at code level. lookupAreaKm2 (polygon-areas.ts:110-123) does exact normName match then a bidirectional substring fallback (`n.includes(target) || target.includes(n)`, :120) returning the FIRST hit, not closest/best. Per-area pop is Math.max(polygonAreaPop, peerSharePop) (safety-score.ts:1510) which floors the LOW side (micro-polygon protection) but has no HIGH-side cap, so a short label substring-matching a larger polygon name inflates km2 -> larger pop -> deflated per-100k -> falsely lenient grade. Real mechanism but theoretical: curated pops bypass this branch (:1503), most areas hit the exact match first, and no in-repo reproduction of an actual mismatched binding was found. Low severity / low confidence appropriate.

title: Every saved-place card reverse-geocode makes a guaranteed-400 Railway round-trip before the local fallback handles it
evidence: SavedPlacesPanel PlaceRow (SavedPlacesPanel.tsx:181) calls `/geo/lookup?lat=..&lng=..` with no `q`. The Vercel handler calls `tryProxy(req,'/geo/lookup')` FIRST (route.ts:25), forwarding lat+lng but no q. The Railway route parses `z.string().min(1).parse(req.query.q ?? '')` which rejects the empty string -> ZodError -> 400 (apps/api/src/middleware/error.ts:17-19). Because !upstream.ok, tryProxy returns null and the local nearestArea path then runs. So it works, but only via a forced error: every place card costs one wasted upstream request returning 400 (log noise + latency) before falling back.
recommendation: In the Vercel /geo/lookup handler, short-circuit the lat/lng branch BEFORE calling tryProxy (or only proxy when `q` is present), since Railway has no lat/lng reverse-lookup branch and will always 400 that case.
verifyNote: Verified. SavedPlacesPanel PlaceRow calls /geo/lookup?lat=&lng= with no q (line ~181). apps/web/.../geo/lookup/route.ts:25 calls tryProxy FIRST (forwarding all params incl lat/lng but no q). Railway geo.routes.ts:12 runs z.string().min(1).max(200).parse(req.query.q ?? '') -> empty string fails min(1) -> ZodError -> errorHandler (error.ts:17-19) returns 400. tryProxy returns null on !upstream.ok (proxy-to-api.ts:69), then route.ts:30-43 runs the local nearestArea path successfully. So every place card incurs one guaranteed-400 upstream round-trip before the local fallback. Low confirmed.

title: Worker services an unstable arbitrary subset when >500 alert-enabled places exist (no orderBy/cursor)
evidence: findMany({ where: { alertsEnabled: true }, take: MAX_PLACES_PER_TICK /*500*/ }) has no orderBy and no cursor pagination. Beyond 500 alert-enabled places, Prisma returns an unordered/implementation-defined slice every tick; places outside it are never checked and never alerted, with no rotation. Latent (low at current scale) but silent.
recommendation: Add a stable cursor (e.g. orderBy id and page across ticks, or order by oldest lastAlertAt/lastSeenIncidentAt) so every alert-enabled place is eventually serviced as the user base grows.
verifyNote: Verified. proximity.worker.ts:70-74 — findMany({ where:{alertsEnabled:true}, take: MAX_PLACES_PER_TICK=500 }) has no orderBy and no cursor. Beyond 500 alert-enabled places Prisma returns a DB-default (implementation-defined, effectively unstable) slice each tick with no rotation, so the overflow is never serviced. Latent at current scale. Low confirmed.

title: cityFromLatLng returns the first bbox match by registry order; overlapping/adjacent city bboxes can route a saved place to the wrong city's adapter
evidence: cityFromLatLng iterates CITIES in registry order and returns the FIRST entry whose rectangular bbox contains the point. Bboxes are coarse rectangles and several cities are geographically adjacent/overlapping (e.g. San Francisco bbox south=37.70 and Oakland bbox south=37.69 share latitude bands across the bay; multiple metros have padded boxes). A saved place in an overlap region is bound to whichever city appears earlier in CITIES, so its proximity check pulls incidents from the wrong city's adapter and may under/over-report. nearestCityByCentroid exists for the no-match case but is not used to disambiguate multi-match.
recommendation: On multi-bbox match, disambiguate by nearest discovered-area centroid (or nearest city centroid) rather than registry order; or tighten/round the bboxes to reduce overlap.
verifyNote: Verified. cityFromLatLng (cities.ts:439-446) iterates CITIES in registry order and returns the FIRST bbox containing the point; nearestCityByCentroid (455+) is only used on no-match, not to disambiguate multi-match. Checked the cited SF/Oakland example: SF (CITIES[~5], south37.70/west-122.55/north37.83/east-122.35) and Oakland (CITIES[~17], south37.69/west-122.36/north37.89/east-122.11) genuinely overlap in lat[37.70,37.83] and a thin lng band [-122.36,-122.35]; SF precedes Oakland so points there bind to SF's adapter. Overlap band is thin (largely Bay water) so practical impact is small, but the first-match-by-registry-order logic is real. Low/medium-confidence is appropriate.

title: Vercel /api/share/[token] and /api/contacts/confirm/[token] perform no token-shape validation or per-token rate limiting (Express side does both)
evidence: The Express routes validate the token (`z.string().min(20).max(64).regex(/^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$/)`) and apply tokenLimiter (10/min/token) on confirm. The Vercel handlers that the client actually uses pass `params.token` straight into a Prisma findUnique with no shape guard and no rate limit. Tokens are 160/192-bit random so brute force is infeasible, but the lack of any per-token throttle means a leaked token can be probed/automated freely, and malformed input hits the DB unnecessarily.
recommendation: Apply the same token regex validation and a per-token rate limit to the Vercel share and confirm route handlers.
verifyNote: Confirmed with one correction to context. Express validates: share.routes.ts:13 Token = z.string().min(20).max(64).regex(/^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$/) and contacts.routes.ts:55 applies tokenLimiter. The Vercel handlers pass params.token straight into Prisma findUnique with no shape guard: apps/web/src/app/api/share/[token]/route.ts:6-7 and apps/web/src/app/api/contacts/confirm/[token]/route.ts:6-7. Re token rate limiting: middleware.ts explicitly does NOT cover /api/share/* or /api/contacts/* (not in LIMITS lines 52-82; pickLimit returns null), so these Vercel routes have neither token-shape validation nor any rate limit. low is correct (tokens are 160/192-bit, brute force infeasible).

title: Twilio sender treats any non-2xx as a generic failure and discards the error body; failed safety SMS give no actionable detail
evidence: sendSms returns `{ ok: res.ok, status: res.status }` and never reads the Twilio JSON error body. For SOS/check-in expiry — life-safety messages — a 21610 (unsubscribed recipient), 21211 (invalid 'To'), or auth (401) failure all collapse to `sms_<status>` in the receipt with no Twilio error code/message. The SOS result returns receipts to the client but the user can't tell why a contact wasn't reached. There is also no retry on transient 5xx for these high-stakes sends.
recommendation: Parse and log the Twilio response body (error code/message) into the DeliveryReceipt detail, and add a single retry for 429/5xx. Surface invalid-number/unsubscribed states to the user so they can fix the contact before an emergency.
verifyNote: Confirmed on both copies. apps/api/src/services/notifications/sms.ts:18 returns { ok: res.ok, status: res.status } and never reads the Twilio JSON error body; the web mirror apps/web/src/server/services/notifications/sms.ts:18 is identical. No retry on 429/5xx. Twilio error codes (21610 unsubscribed, 21211 invalid To, 401 auth) all collapse to a generic status. The finding states the return is {ok,status} — there is also an optional `skipped` field but that does not affect the substance. low/medium boundary; the auditor proposed low which is reasonable for a diagnostics gap on life-safety sends; I keep low.

title: Philadelphia CARTO adapter falls back occurredAt to epoch(0) instead of dropping the row
evidence: occurredAt: r.dispatch_date_time ?? new Date(0).toISOString() — a 1970 epoch fallback. dc-arcgis.ts:176-184 and detroit-arcgis.ts:139-146 drop undated rows ('epoch-fallback rows pollute the aggregator's rate compute and collapse windowDays to 0'). SQL filters WHERE dispatch_date_time IS NOT NULL so nulls are excluded upstream, but a present-but-unparseable value slips through as an epoch row; getDiscoveredAreasPhl and the legacy unwindowed aggregate count it.
recommendation: Mirror the DC/Detroit guard: parse the date and continue on NaN/<=0 rather than emitting an epoch-0 Incident.
verifyNote: Reproduced. phl-carto.ts:179 occurredAt: r.dispatch_date_time ?? new Date(0).toISOString() — a 1970 epoch fallback. dc-arcgis.ts:182-184 and detroit-arcgis.ts:144-146 both drop undated rows (NaN or <=0 timestamp). The PHL SQL at line 147 WHERE dispatch_date_time IS NOT NULL excludes true nulls upstream, so the ?? branch only triggers on a present-but-malformed value; such a row would slip through as epoch-0 (or NaN) into getDiscoveredAreasPhl / unwindowed aggregates. Real defect but narrow exposure given the SQL guard; low is correct.

title: Legacy Express /official-alerts route still mounted, hardcoded to San Diego only
evidence: app.use('/official-alerts', officialAlertsRouter) is still mounted (index.ts:315). nws.service.ts fetches area=CA and filters areaDesc to 'san diego' || 'southern california' (lines 53,64-66) regardless of any city param, then the route returns sources:['National Weather Service'] with no AMBER/USGS/traffic. The web client correctly hits the Vercel route at ${API_BASE}/api/official-alerts (api-client.ts:127), so this is dead for the current UI, but the endpoint remains publicly exposed and any legacy/native/direct consumer of the Express base would receive San-Diego-only weather data for every city.
recommendation: Either delete the legacy Express route + service, or make it return a 410/redirect to the canonical web route. If kept for back-compat, document that it is SD-only so no consumer treats it as multi-city.
verifyNote: Reproduced. apps/api/src/index.ts:315 mounts officialAlertsRouter. nws.service.ts:53 pulls area=CA and lines 64-66 filter to 'san diego'||'southern california' regardless of city, returning sources:['National Weather Service'] only (official-alerts.routes.ts:6-18), with no city param read. Web client hits the Vercel route via api-client.ts:127 (${API_BASE}/api${path}), so this is dead for current UI but remains publicly exposed and SD-only. (Note: the Express path DOES have AbortSignal.timeout at nws.service.ts:58, unlike the web path — does not affect this finding.) Low correct.

title: Latent category-case mismatch between CHP adapter and the traffic-panel filter
evidence: TrafficAlertsPanel filters incidents with a.category === 'traffic' (lowercase). The CHP adapter emits category: 'Traffic' (capital T, chp.ts:205). This does not currently surface as a bug only because the California path in state-traffic.ts:1122-1127 (buildCalifornia) spreads the CHP object and overrides category to 'traffic'. If CHP output is ever consumed without that remap, CA traffic incidents would silently fail the panel filter and the card would show 'No active road incidents' while incidents exist.
recommendation: Normalize on a single category constant (e.g. 'traffic') in chp.ts itself, or have the panel filter case-insensitively, so the remap is not load-bearing for correctness.
verifyNote: Reproduced. chp.ts:205 emits category:'Traffic' (capital T). TrafficAlertsPanel.tsx:42 filters a.category==='traffic' (lowercase). buildCalifornia (state-traffic.ts:1117-1128) spreads the CHP object and overrides category:'traffic' at line 1126, so the remap is load-bearing — without it CA incidents silently fail the panel filter. Latent, correctly rated low.

title: Missing/unknown city slug makes AMBER fall back to the full national active list
evidence: When citySlug is absent or unknown, city resolves to null (route.ts:21) and getAmberAlerts(null) (route.ts:34) omits the area param (amber.ts:45) and returns every active Child Abduction Emergency nationwide. All 44 served cities resolve in OFFICIAL_ALERTS_CITY_META so the normal UI path is safe, but a request to /api/official-alerts with no/unknown ?city would surface out-of-state AMBER alerts to the user as if local, which contradicts the stated state-scoping posture.
recommendation: When city is null, return [] for AMBER (and NWS/USGS) rather than a national pull, or require a resolvable city param; reserve the national fallback for an explicit nationwide view.
verifyNote: Reproduced. route.ts:21 resolves city=null for absent/unknown slug; route.ts:34 calls getAmberAlerts(null), and amber.ts:45 omits the area param → national active Child Abduction Emergency list. Same national fallback exists for NWS (nws.ts:54). All 44 cities resolve in city-meta.ts so the normal UI is safe; only a malformed/direct request surfaces out-of-state AMBER as if local. Low correct.

title: Honolulu (HI) has no traffic registry entry and no WSDOT-style documented rationale
evidence: Honolulu is a served city (city-meta.ts:59, state HI). HI is absent from both the keyless REGISTRY and the env-gated loop, so trafficAgencyForState('HI')→null and the panel shows the unavailable fallback. Unlike WA — which has an explicit, detailed WSDOT note (lines 1087-1100) explaining why it's intentionally omitted — HI is simply missing with no comment, so a future maintainer can't tell if it was evaluated or overlooked. The fallback copy ('state highway patrol doesn't publish a free public feed') is plausibly true for HI but unverified in code.
recommendation: Add a one-line comment documenting HI's status (evaluated/no keyless feed found) mirroring the WSDOT note, or wire HDOT's GoAkamai feed if a keyless endpoint exists.
verifyNote: Verified. Honolulu is a served city (city-meta.ts:59, state HI). HI appears in neither the keyless REGISTRY (1171-1192) nor the env-gated loop (1197-1208), so trafficAgencyForState('HI') -> null and the panel shows the unavailable fallback. Unlike WA, which has an explicit detailed WSDOT rationale comment (1087-1100), HI has no comment at all anywhere in the file, so maintainers cannot tell if it was evaluated or overlooked. Minor maintainability/documentation gap; low correctly rated.

title: Fixed 25km radius under-covers large metros and is identical for all cities regardless of sprawl
evidence: getStateTraffic is always called with the default 25km radius (route.ts:35 passes no radius). Centroids are single points (city-meta.ts), so e.g. Los Angeles (centroid 34.05,-118.32), Phoenix, and Jacksonville (one of the largest US cities by area, ~2,260 km^2) are all clipped to a 25km circle from a single downtown-ish point. Freeway incidents in outlying parts of these metros (e.g. north/west LA County, far-south Jacksonville) fall outside the radius and are dropped, while the panel still asserts it shows incidents 'near <city>'. The radius is a hardcoded constant with no per-city tuning despite metro areas spanning very different footprints.
recommendation: Consider a per-city or per-state radius (or use the city bbox diagonal) so large metros aren't silently truncated; at minimum note the 25km limit in the disclaimer.
verifyNote: Verified mechanically. DEFAULT_RADIUS_KM=25 (line 28), used as the default param of getStateTraffic (line 1246). Grep confirms the SOLE call site (route.ts:35) passes no radius, so every city of every state uses a fixed 25km haversine clip from a single downtown-ish centroid (city-meta.ts) regardless of metro footprint (e.g. Jacksonville centroid 30.33,-81.66, one of the largest US cities by area). The panel still claims incidents 'near {city}'. Real but low-impact design limitation, further bounded by MAX_ALERTS=12; the 'under-covers' impact is a reasonable inference (downgraded the auditor's medium confidence appropriately). Low severity correct.

title: next/image AVIF not enabled though config comment claims AVIF conversion
location: apps/web/next.config.ts (images block) and its comment 'Optimization gives us AVIF/WebP conversion'
evidence: The images config sets only remotePatterns; images.formats is not set, so Next defaults to ['image/webp'] (no AVIF). Live confirmation: requesting the optimizer with `Accept: image/avif,image/webp,...` still returns `content-type: image/webp` (content-length 248236) — AVIF is never produced even when the client advertises it. The config comment asserts AVIF conversion, which is not happening.
recommendation: Add `formats: ['image/avif', 'image/webp']` to the images config. AVIF typically saves a further 20-40% over WebP on photographic backdrops, compounding the savings from perf-web-1. Update the comment either way.
verifyNote: Verified: next.config.ts images block (lines 117-121) sets only remotePatterns; images.formats is absent, so Next defaults to webp-only. Live: requesting /_next/image with 'Accept: image/avif,image/webp,...' still returns content-type image/webp (content-length 248236, identical to webp-only request) — AVIF never produced. The config comment (line 113-116) asserts 'AVIF/WebP conversion'. Low is correct.

title: /safezone/trend has no per-request timeout while the heavier /safety-score does
evidence: GET /safety-score wraps the compose in withScoreTimeout (45s -> 503 warming_up). GET /trend (line 145-146) awaits getCitywideTrend/getTrendForArea directly with no timeout. getCitywideTrend does the same full city discover() + Promise.all-over-all-areas fan-out (trend-feed.ts:156-161) with limit:5000, so a cold heavy city (Cleveland ~30s, Atlanta) can hang the request up to the undici 30s body timeout per upstream page with no friendly 503. It relies solely on the edge Cache-Control and the Vercel-side proxy's own timeout.
recommendation: Apply the same withScoreTimeout-style deadline to the /trend handler so cold heavy cities return a clean 503 warming_up instead of a long hang, consistent with /safety-score.
verifyNote: Verified GET /safety-score wraps the compose in withScoreTimeout->503 warming_up (safezone.routes.ts:97-105), while GET /trend (141-150) awaits getCitywideTrend/getTrendForArea directly with no timeout. getCitywideTrend (trend-feed.ts:136-161) does the same discover()+Promise.all-over-all-areas fan-out at limit:5000, so a cold heavy city can hang up to undici's 30s body timeout per upstream page with no friendly 503, relying only on edge Cache-Control + the Vercel proxy timeout. Low is correct (inconsistency, not a security/data issue).

title: GET /community/posts/mine is an unbounded findMany with no take limit
evidence: prisma.post.findMany({ where: { authorId: req.session!.uid }, orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' }, include: { flags: true, area: true } }) has no `take`. Every other post-listing query in the codebase is capped (community feed take:50, neighborhood feed take:20, moderation queue take:50, neighborhood list cap 1000 per the v96 audit comment). This one ships a user's entire post history with related flags+area joins. Scoped per-user so bounded in practice, and backed by @@index([authorId, status]) (schema.prisma:377), but a heavy poster makes the response and join grow without limit.
recommendation: Add take: 100 (or cursor pagination) consistent with the rest of the post routes.
verifyNote: Verified. community.routes.ts:62-66 GET /posts/mine does prisma.post.findMany({ where: { authorId }, orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' }, include: { flags: true, area: true } }) with no take, while the feed at line 46 uses take:50. @@index([authorId, status]) confirmed at schema.prisma:377 (and [authorId, createdAt] at 404, which actually serves this query's orderBy). Scoped per-user and indexed, so bounded in practice; unbounded only for a heavy poster. Low severity correct.

title: Account-delete + purge cron path verified sound (web hard-deletes immediately; cron is fail-closed and 401s without secret)
evidence: The web (canonical) /api/account/delete does an immediate transactional HARD delete with full RESTRICT-FK clearing + Vercel Blob photo erasure + revocation-cache invalidation, gated by confirmEmail==session.email and literal 'DELETE' (account/delete/route.ts:28). The purge-accounts cron (for the Express soft-delete path's deletedAt rows) is correct: requireCronSecret fails CLOSED with 503 if CRON_SECRET unset and 401 otherwise. Live probe: GET https://communitysafe.app/api/cron/purge-accounts with no auth returned HTTP 401 {error}. Note the web delete is hard/immediate so the 30-day grace + purge cron only ever applies to Express-API-initiated soft-deletes (which, per finding #1, the web UI never triggers) — a benign mismatch worth noting.
recommendation: No fix required for security. Optionally reconcile the delete semantics: the privacy policy's '30-day grace then purge' applies to the Express soft-delete, but the actual UI hard-deletes instantly — make sure the privacy copy matches the immediate-deletion behavior users experience.
verifyNote: Verified. apps/web/src/app/api/account/delete/route.ts:25-34 requires session, confirmEmail===session.email and confirmText literal 'DELETE', then calls deleteAccount() which does an immediate transactional HARD delete clearing all RESTRICT FKs + Vercel Blob photo erasure + invalidateSessionRevocation (account.ts:70-138). requireCronSecret (bearer-auth.ts:18-29) fails CLOSED with 503 if CRON_SECRET unset, else 401. Live probe: GET /api/cron/purge-accounts with no auth returned HTTP 401 {error:unauthorized} (CRON_SECRET is configured in prod). I exercised the delete on my throwaway anonymous account: returned {ok:true, deletedAt:...} and the subsequent /api/auth/me with the same token returned 401 immediately (revocation-cache invalidation works), then cleaned up. The noted semantic mismatch (web hard-deletes instantly vs privacy copy's '30-day grace then purge', which only applies to the Express soft-delete path the web UI never triggers) is accurate. Info, no security fix required; the privacy-copy reconciliation recommendation is valid.

title: Brand/identity split is visible in live production artifacts (travelsafe vs CommunitySafe)
evidence: Live anonymous-session JWT decodes to a device email '...@travelsafe.local' while all user-facing copy and email subjects say 'CommunitySafe' (e.g. live-share.ts:62 'CommunitySafe — your contact is sharing their location'). Client stores the auth token under localStorage key 'travelsafe.token'. Internal identifiers/keys remain travelsafe.* while the public brand is CommunitySafe — consistent with the documented repo/brand divergence but now confirmed in generated user identifiers reachable in production.

title: JSON-LD injected via dangerouslySetInnerHTML is not </script>-escaped
evidence: dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{__html: JSON.stringify(jsonLd)}} embeds city.label/area.label. JSON.stringify escapes quotes but not the literal </script>. Labels come from the controlled registry today (not raw URL params), so not currently exploitable.
recommendation: Escape for script context: JSON.stringify(jsonLd).replace(/</g,'\\u003c').
verifyNote: Verified cities/[city]/page.tsx:107 and [neighborhood]/page.tsx:105 use dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{__html: JSON.stringify(jsonLd)}}. JSON.stringify escapes quotes but not a literal '</script>'. Confirmed jsonLd labels derive from cityBySlug()/the controlled CITIES registry (page.tsx:4,29,51,84-97), not raw URL params, so not currently exploitable. Info severity correct; the <-escaping hardening recommendation is valid defense-in-depth.

title: SECURITY_AUDIT_RETENTION_DAYS read from process.env, bypassing the zod env schema
evidence: retention.worker.ts reads `process.env.SECURITY_AUDIT_RETENTION_DAYS` and parses it ad hoc (with its own 7..730 bound), while env.ts centralizes every other config var through the Env zod schema. The var is not declared in env.ts, so it is undocumented in the single source of truth and not validated at boot.
recommendation: Add SECURITY_AUDIT_RETENTION_DAYS to env.ts (coerce.number, min 7, max 730, default 90) and consume env.SECURITY_AUDIT_RETENTION_DAYS for consistency and boot-time validation.
verifyNote: Verified: retention.worker.ts:30 reads process.env.SECURITY_AUDIT_RETENTION_DAYS and parses ad hoc with its own 7..730 bound; env.ts (lines 11-80) does not declare this var, so it bypasses the zod single-source-of-truth and is not validated at boot. Info is correct.

title: Live-share feature stores and returns no location data (token only yields expiresAt + userId)
evidence: LiveShareLink has no lat/lng/location columns and resolveSharedView returns only `{ expiresAt, userId }`; there is no endpoint to update a share's location. The web share page (apps/web/src/app/share/[token]/page.tsx) is honest about this ('Live coordinate streaming is on the roadmap'), so this is a noted stub rather than a deceptive feature — but the API surface, emails ('your contact is sharing their live location'), and method names imply live coordinates that do not exist. Flagging so it isn't mistaken for a working capability and because the email copy slightly overstates what is delivered.
recommendation: Either implement location columns + an update endpoint, or soften the email copy/method naming to match the roadmap-stub reality the share page already discloses.
verifyNote: Verified: schema LiveShareLink (schema.prisma:646-657) has only id/userId/token/expiresAt/revokedAt/createdAt — no lat/lng/location. resolveSharedView (live-share.service.ts:48-54) returns only {expiresAt,userId}. safety.routes.ts exposes create/list/revoke + share.routes.ts resolve — no location-update endpoint. Email copy (live-share.service.ts:25) says 'sharing their live location'; share page (apps/web/src/app/share/[token]/page.tsx:27) honestly discloses 'Live coordinate streaming is on the roadmap'. Noted stub, copy slightly overstates. Info is correct.

title: Citywide compose fetches per-area incidents with limit=Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (no per-area cap)
evidence: _getCitywide fans out `this.getIncidents(a.slug, { limit: Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER })` over every area (comment lines 202-204 deliberately removed the prior 500 cap to preserve Crime Map visual hierarchy). For DC (~120k rows) / Detroit (199 areas) / Oakland (134 areas) this materializes the full city incident set across the perArea arrays in one pass. It is references into the shared adapter cache (not duplicated row objects) and is gated by the per-city compute-limiter (lib/compute-limit.ts) plus the inflight dedupe, so it does not reintroduce the historical OOM, but it is the one place in the package that intentionally runs unbounded — worth a comment-anchored watch if a future city's cache grows. Note that mix.ts:14 (CITYWIDE_PER_AREA_LIMIT=1000) and getCitywideAreaStats (dispatcher.ts:361, limit 1000) deliberately do cap, so the unbounded path is the visual-count one only.
recommendation: No change required now; if a future feed pushes a single city well past current sizes, consider capping at a high but finite value (e.g. 50k/area) since the percentile risk-band only needs relative counts, not exact totals.
verifyNote: Verified. _getCitywide fans out getIncidents with limit=Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (dispatcher.ts:205) with the comment (202-204) deliberately removing the prior 500 cap for Crime Map visual hierarchy. Other paths cap: mix.ts:14 CITYWIDE_PER_AREA_LIMIT=1000 (used at mix.ts:101), getCitywideAreaStats limit:1000 (dispatcher.ts:361). The unbounded path is gated by the per-city withComputeLimit and dedupe and holds references into the shared adapter cache (not duplicated objects), so no OOM reintroduced. Accurate observation, no change needed now. Info severity correct.

title: PrismaPg adapter uses default pg.Pool sizing (no explicit max) across multiple client roots
evidence: Both `new PrismaPg({ connectionString })` calls pass no `max`/pool options, so node-postgres defaults to max 10 connections per Pool. The repo runs three independent generated clients (db, api, web) and several background workers (check-in, proximity, digest, audit-retention all started at boot per the API recon). Against a Neon/Railway Postgres with a low connection ceiling and a serverless web tier, the absence of an explicit, intentionally-small pool size (and the lack of a pooled `?pgbouncer=true` / connection_limit hint in the URL handling) can exhaust connections under fan-out. Not a defect today, but undocumented and worth setting explicitly.
recommendation: Pass an explicit pool config to PrismaPg (e.g. `max` tuned to the deployment) and document the expectation that DATABASE_URL points at the Neon pooler for the serverless web client. No code bug, informational.
verifyNote: Both new PrismaPg({ connectionString }) calls (apps/api/src/lib/prisma.ts:4, packages/db/src/index.ts:8) and the seed (seed.ts:6) pass no max/pool options, so pg defaults to max 10 per Pool. Multiple client roots exist. No code defect; informational note about explicit pool sizing and pooler URL guidance is fair. Info severity correct.

title: audit-ratios cron documents a NO_CRON_SECRET bypass env var that does not exist in the auth gate
evidence: The audit-ratios handler comment says: "Bypass available via NO_CRON_SECRET env var for local development." The shared gate requireCronSecret() has no such bypass — it fails CLOSED (503 if CRON_SECRET unset, 401 on header mismatch) and references no NO_CRON_SECRET var. The bypass was apparently removed but the comment was left behind, which could mislead an operator into thinking local cron runs can be unlocked via that var.
recommendation: Delete the stale NO_CRON_SECRET sentence from the comment (and the same wording elsewhere if present). Fail-closed is the correct behavior; just keep the docs in sync.
verifyNote: audit-ratios/route.ts:28-29 comment claims a NO_CRON_SECRET bypass for local dev. bearer-auth.ts:18-29 requireCronSecret has no such bypass (fails closed: 503 if CRON_SECRET unset, 401 on mismatch). grep confirms NO_CRON_SECRET appears nowhere in apps/web/src or apps/api/src except that comment. Stale doc confirmed; info is correct.

title: rollup-linux optionalDependency hack is correct and version-consistent (verified against lockfile)
evidence: The pin `@rollup/rollup-linux-x64-gnu@4.60.4` exactly matches the resolved rollup version in package-lock.json (both node_modules/rollup and node_modules/@rollup/rollup-linux-x64-gnu resolve to 4.60.4). rollup loads its native binary by exact-version match, so the pin must track rollup's version — it currently does. Recording it as a no findings item only to flag the maintenance coupling.
recommendation: No change needed now. When bumping rollup/vite/vitest, update this pin in lockstep or `npm ci` on the Linux CI runner will fail to find the native binary again. Consider a CI assertion (or renovate grouping) that fails if the pin and resolved rollup version diverge.
verifyNote: package-lock.json: node_modules/rollup=4.60.4 and node_modules/@rollup/rollup-linux-x64-gnu=4.60.4, exactly matching the package.json:45 pin '4.60.4'. The hack is correct and version-consistent. Maintenance-coupling note is valid. Info is correct.

title: 30-day account-purge promise is genuinely implemented and scheduled (verified, no gap)
evidence: Privacy policy promises deleted accounts "permanently purged within 30 days" (privacy/page.tsx:69,86,166). This is backed end-to-end: softDeleteAccount() sets deletedAt + obfuscates email to `deleted-<id>-<ts>@deleted.travelsafe.local` (auth.service.ts:191-196); purge-accounts cron is actually scheduled daily at 03:30 in vercel.json (`/api/cron/purge-accounts`, `30 3 * * *`), gated by requireCronSecret, queries `deletedAt < cutoff` with PURGE_GRACE_DAYS default 30; and deleteAccount() runs a complete Prisma $transaction clearing every RESTRICT-FK table (reviewActions, reports, acknowledgements, edits, reactions, comments, posts) before deleting the User (cascades handle AlertPreference, TrustedContact, blocks, mutes, push subs, check-in timers, live-share links) plus best-effort Blob photo deletion — matching exactly the record list enumerated in the policy. The separate 90-day SecurityAuditLog retention worker (retention.worker.ts) backs the policy's "Security-audit logs are retained for 90 days" line. Recorded as a positive verification, not a defect; resolves the open 'account-purge worker' item flagged in prior project memory.
recommendation: No action needed. Optionally add monitoring/alerting on the purge cron's `ok:false`/`errors[]` response so a silently failing purge (which would breach the 30-day promise) is detected.
verifyNote: Verified end-to-end. privacy/page.tsx:69,86,166 promise purge within 30 days. softDeleteAccount (auth.service.ts:189-200) sets deletedAt + obfuscates email to deleted-<id>-<ts>@deleted.travelsafe.local + bumps tokenVersion. vercel.json crons schedules /api/cron/purge-accounts at '30 3 * * *' (03:30 daily). purge route is gated by requireCronSecret, queries deletedAt < cutoff with PURGE_GRACE_DAYS default 30, batches 100 with concurrency 3, runs the full deleteAccount() cascade. deleteAccount (account.ts:70) runs a transaction clearing the RESTRICT-FK tables (reviewActions, reportsFiled, acknowledgements, edits, reactions, comments, posts) before deleting the User, plus best-effort Blob del() at :134. Separate 90-day SecurityAuditLog retention.worker.ts (DEFAULT_RETENTION_DAYS=90) backs the audit-log line. Positive verification, no defect.

evidence: The DataDisclaimer banner (shown on every score surface) carries the same three-part guidance as the Terms/Methodology: historical-reporting-only, not professional advice, and 'should not be used as the sole basis for housing, lending, insurance, or hiring decisions — verify with the cited source.' Sentry no-PII verified: apps/api/src/lib/sentry.ts:38 sendDefaultPii:false + setUser({id}) only (line 48). bcrypt verified: auth.service.ts:16 imports bcryptjs, :34 hashes. title: Safety-critical 911 disclaimer is correctly present in both legal copy and the live Personal Safety UI
evidence: Terms (line 37): 'Not a substitute for 911 or any emergency service... CommunitySafe does not contact emergency services on your behalf.' The live safety tab renders a pre-use interstitial: 'CommunitySafe\'s personal-safety tools may fail... and are not a substitute for 911 or professional emergency services. The application does not contact emergency services on your behalf. In an emergency, call 911.' (safety/page.tsx:68-70), and the SOS/check-on-me copy reiterates 'this is the trusted-contact layer, not a 911 dialer' (safety/page.tsx:430-431,481). The application does not contact emergency services on your behalf. In an emergency, call 911.' All rights reserved' but never assert or reserve the CommunitySafe name as a mark, and there is no IP/trademark clause in the Terms. title: DMCA page names an email-only "designated agent" with no evidence of US Copyright Office DMCA agent registration
evidence: dmca/page.tsx lists 'CyberWave Technologies LLC — DMCA Agent' with only an email (info@cyberwaveglobal.com) and the website. 17 U.S.C. §512(c)(2) safe-harbor protection requires the designated agent to be registered with the U.S. Copyright Office DMCA Directory (and a physical address on file). There is no physical address shown and nothing in-repo indicating registration. Until registered, the §512(c) safe harbor is not actually available despite the page implying it.
recommendation: Register the designated agent with the U.S. Copyright Office DMCA Directory and add the agent's physical mailing address to the DMCA page. Process/legal action, not code.
verifyNote: In-repo facts verified: dmca/page.tsx:30-34 lists 'CyberWave Technologies LLC — DMCA Agent' with only an email (info@cyberwaveglobal.com) and website, no physical mailing address. The §512(c)(2) Copyright Office DMCA Directory registration requirement is real, and the repo shows no evidence of registration. The registration status itself cannot be verified from the codebase; correctly framed as a process/legal note at info severity with medium confidence.

title: Legal entity and jurisdiction copy is internally consistent (CyberWave Technologies LLC, California LLC) across all legal surfaces

The TravelSafe→CommunitySafe split is confined to repo name, package names (@travelsafe/*), localStorage keys, health service ids (travelsafe-api/web), and the actual GitHub repo URL (github.com/damienmcdade/TravelSafe, correctly the real repo) — none are misleading brand claims.

Entity/jurisdiction copy is internally consistent. title: Verified-correct: timezone bucketing, freshness wording, uptick logic, and severity/trend math
evidence: hour-of-day bucketing uses Intl.DateTimeFormat in the city's IANA tz (with the '24'→0 midnight normalization) both client (TimeOfDayCard.hourInTz) and server (trend-feed.timeOfDayAnalysis via cityLocalHour), so the prior UTC-runtime bug is genuinely fixed. DataFreshnessBadge honestly captions 'newest report' (not 'synced time'), pivots to absolute date past 14d, and bails on NaN/missing timestamps. UptickTile has a correct, honestly-worded empty state and an aria-labeled drill-in. incident-summary.service.classifyTrend guards prior===0 (returns rising/stable, not a divide-by-zero), and rateRatio uses `priorCount>0 ? ... : (recentCount>0?1.5:0)`. IncidentSummaryCard's no-LLM fallback text matches the deterministic counts/window. CrimeChart's windowDays param (incl. 'all'→no param) is within the server's 1..730 bound. CSV export (TrendPanel.downloadDispatchCsv) RFC4180-escapes cells and re-fetches the full bullet set on demand.
recommendation: No action — recorded so these areas are documented as checked and sound.
verifyNote: Spot-checked the sound-areas claims and they hold: hourInTz normalizes '24'→0 via Intl.DateTimeFormat in city tz (TimeOfDayCard.tsx:111-125) mirrored server-side by cityLocalHour; DataFreshnessBadge bails on NaN/missing asOf (DataFreshnessBadge.tsx:40-42), captions 'newest report' (line 47/71), and pivots to absolute date past 14d (line 85-87); incident-summary.service.classifyTrend guards prior===0 (line 63). Correctly recorded as info; no action.

Indianapolis uses point-in-polygon geocoding against the SAME 99-polygon data file (data/indianapolis-neighborhoods.ts, 99 entries) the map renders, so feed-area labels match polygon names exactly — an ideal join with no AREA_ALIASES needed.
recommendation: No action; recorded as the positive baseline. Note that none of these four cities required entries in CrimeMap.tsx AREA_ALIASES, indicating clean polygon-to-feed name alignment (Indianapolis by construction, Atlanta/Tucson via direct NhoodName/NHA_NAME labels, Raleigh via the 6 district names matching the 6 polygons).

One minor caveat: AREA_ALIASES does not exist anywhere in the codebase (grep -rln across apps/web/src and packages found zero), so the claim 'none required AREA_ALIASES entries' is vacuously true rather than a meaningful comparison against other cities that have such entries; does not change the positive-baseline conclusion.
