title: Ownership scoping is consistently correct on all user-owned object routes (no IDOR found in contacts, check-in, live-share, push, preferences, auth)
evidence: Every per-object mutation/read derives the owner from the verified JWT (req.session!.uid) and includes it in the WHERE clause: removeContact/resendConfirmation use findFirst/deleteMany {id, userId}; markSafe/listActive use {id|...,userId}; revokeLiveShare uses updateMany {id, userId, revokedAt:null}; DELETE /push/subscribe uses deleteMany {endpoint, userId: req.session!.uid}; preferences upsert/find use {userId}; auth me/logout/account/delete/mfa all pass req.session!.uid. None accept a caller-supplied owner id. Confirmed user A cannot reach user B's rows.
recommendation: No change required. Keep the pattern (owner from session + WHERE userId) as the standard for any new per-user object routes.
verifyNote: Verified across all cited files. removeContact/resendConfirmation findFirst/deleteMany {id,userId}; markSafe/listActive {id,userId}/{userId}; revokeLiveShare updateMany {id,userId,revokedAt:null}; push DELETE deleteMany {endpoint,userId:req.session!.uid}; preferences upsert/find {userId:req.session!.uid}; auth me/logout/account-delete/mfa all use req.session!.uid. None accept caller-supplied owner ids. No IDOR found. Accurate info note.

title: Moderator RBAC is enforced server-side per action (not client-trusted), with banned/suspended exclusion
evidence: requireModerator() re-loads the user by session uid and checks trustLevel === MODERATOR, rejecting permanentlyBanned and suspendedUntil>now, before queue/review/report. The MODERATOR_EMAILS bootstrap path is gated to zero existing moderators AND a 24h-from-boot window. reviewPost records reviewerId from the session and is moderator-gated. block/mute prevent self-targeting. No object-level IDOR (moderator actions are role-scoped, not per-owner).
recommendation: No change required for authz. (Separate note: POST /moderation/posts/:id/review lacks writeLimiter, relying only on the global 600/min limiter — a rate-limit gap, not an authz gap.)
verifyNote: Verified in moderation.routes.ts:21-104 and queue.service.ts:17-66. requireModerator re-loads user by session uid, checks trustLevel===MODERATOR, rejects permanentlyBanned/suspendedUntil>now; bootstrap CSV path gated to moderatorCount===0 AND within 24h BOOTSTRAP_GRACE_MS. reviewPost records reviewerId from session and is moderator-gated. block(110)/mute(127) prevent self-targeting. The companion note is also correct: POST /posts/:id/review (line 77) has only requireAuth, lacking writeLimiter that report/block/mute carry — a rate-limit gap, not authz. Accurate info note.

title: Dynamic object indexing with user-controlled key (city-meta / SD ZIP / socrata env lookup) — read-only, no pollution sink
evidence: Several handlers index plain objects with a user-supplied string key, e.g. official-alerts/route.ts:17-21 `const citySlug = url.searchParams.get("city"); const city = citySlug ? OFFICIAL_ALERTS_CITY_META[citySlug] ?? null : null;`. These are READS only (no assignment to obj[userKey]=...), so they cannot pollute Object.prototype. A key of `__proto__`/`constructor` would resolve to an inherited member but the result is then used only as {state,centroid,label} and guarded by `?? null`, so there is no exploitable effect. In http.ts:30 socrataAppToken builds an env-var name from a host that is itself derived from registry-hardcoded URLs, not user input. Noting for completeness: no prototype-pollution WRITE sink (Object.assign/merge/recursive-set of user JSON into a shared object) was found anywhere in the API or web request handlers.
recommendation: No action required. If hardening is desired, use a Map for OFFICIAL_ALERTS_CITY_META / SD_ZIP_TO_AREA so __proto__/constructor keys cannot resolve to inherited members.
verifyNote: Verified official-alerts/route.ts:17-21 reads OFFICIAL_ALERTS_CITY_META[citySlug] with `?? null` guard — read-only, result used only as {state,...}. http.ts:25-28 socrataAppToken builds env key from a host that comes from socrataHeaders(url) where url is the adapter's registry URL, not request input. Grep found ZERO Object.assign/deepMerge/merge( write sinks in apps/api/src or apps/web/src and ZERO queryRawUnsafe/executeRawUnsafe outside generated. No prototype-pollution WRITE sink exists. info correct.

title: No SSRF: all outbound adapter/feed fetches use fixed hosts + URLSearchParams encoding; tryProxy base is env-pinned
evidence: Verified end-to-end that no user input controls an outbound URL HOST or PATH. (1) fetchSocrata builds the URL from the adapter's hardcoded `query.url` (cities.ts registry); the only user param (offense) is applied as an in-memory .filter() in dispatcher.ts:221-222, never in the SoQL. (2) nominatimGeocode (lookup.service.ts) sets the user `q` via url.searchParams.set (properly percent-encoded) against the fixed nominatim.openstreetmap.org host, bounded by a per-city bbox. (3) proxy-to-api.ts:33-39 `new URL(upstreamPath, env.API_BASE_URL)` where upstreamPath is a string literal at every call site and only inbound search params round-trip — base host is env-pinned. (4) usgs.ts:57-68 and state-traffic.ts:160-170 build URLs from static LayerConfig.layerUrl + URLSearchParams with numeric centroids from a server-side city-meta map. (5) DB layer: grep found zero $queryRawUnsafe/$executeRawUnsafe calls outside generated/prisma; all access is the parameterized query builder.
recommendation: No action. Maintain the invariant that adapter feed hosts come only from the cities.ts registry / static configs and never from request params.
verifyNote: Verified: (1) nominatimGeocode (lookup.service.ts:90-104) uses fixed host nominatim.openstreetmap.org, sets user q via url.searchParams.set (percent-encoded) and a bounded viewbox. (2) proxy-to-api.ts:35 `new URL(upstreamPath, env.API_BASE_URL)` — env-pinned base; confirmed all 10 tryProxy call sites pass string literals ('/crime-data/area-stats', '/geo/lookup', etc.), only inbound search params round-trip. (3) usgs.ts:57-68 and state-traffic.ts:160-167 build URLs from static hosts/cfg.layerUrl + URLSearchParams with server-side numeric centroids. (4) No raw SQL anywhere outside generated. No user-controlled outbound host/path. info correct.

title: No ReDoS: prevet/profanity regexes are length-bounded and pass adversarial timing tests
evidence: The most suspicious pattern, STREET_ADDRESS `/\b\d{1,5}\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z]+)*\s+(?:St|...)\b\.?/i`, has a repeated group but the inner alternation is not ambiguous against the surrounding tokens; tested against `'1 '+'Aa '.repeat(80)+'x'` (243 chars) it returns in <1ms, and 40/60-char variants in <1ms. NAME_AND_ACCUSATION and the others completed in ~0.1ms. All run only on post bodies which preVetPost bounds to POST_MAX_LEN=800 chars (post-prevet.ts:7) before any regex runs, capping worst-case input length. profanity-filter uses simple `\bword\b` alternations.
recommendation: No action. Keep the POST_MAX_LEN length-check ordered before the regex checks (it already is).
verifyNote: No ReDoS confirmed via independent timing tests. STREET_ADDRESS and NAME_AND_ACCUSATION ran sub-millisecond on adversarial inputs; I stress-tested STREET_ADDRESS at 7.5k chars (0.35ms) and 37k chars (0.19ms) — the (?:\s+[A-Z][a-zA-Z]+)* group is linear, not catastrophically ambiguous. CAVEAT: the finding's supporting claim that POST_MAX_LEN 'bounds input to 800 chars before any regex runs' is INACCURATE — preVetPost (post-prevet.ts:84-117) does NOT early-return on over-length; it sets a block flag at line 95 but still executes every regex at lines 101+ on the full (potentially >800-char) body. This does not change the no-ReDoS conclusion since the patterns are not exponential. info correct.

title: All Vercel cron and diag endpoints are authenticated and fail closed (verified, not a gap)
evidence: Every one of the 5 cron route handlers and 3 diag route handlers begins with `const denied = requireCronSecret(req); if (denied) return denied;`. The three vercel.json-scheduled crons (check-in, audit-ratios, purge-accounts — the latter performs irreversible account hard-deletes via deleteAccount()) are therefore NOT externally triggerable without the CRON_SECRET Bearer token, and requireCronSecret returns 503 when the secret is unconfigured rather than allowing the call. diag/boston discloses env-var state only as Boolean presence (true/false), never values. Recorded as info to document that the most dangerous external-trigger surface (purge-accounts) is correctly protected.
recommendation: No action required. Ensure CRON_SECRET is actually set in the Vercel production environment (the fail-closed default makes an unset secret return 503, which would break the legitimate crons — so verify it is present).
verifyNote: All 5 cron handlers (check-in:12, audit-ratios:42, purge-accounts:39, push-digest:22, warm-cache:24) and all 3 diag handlers (boston:14, ai:21, tips:12) call requireCronSecret as the first statement and return on denial. vercel.json crons array = exactly check-in, audit-ratios, purge-accounts (all guarded). purge-accounts imports/uses deleteAccount() cascade (irreversible). diag/boston exposes only Boolean(env.*) presence, never values. requireCronSecret fails closed with 503. Accurate; info is right.

evidence: Every NEXT_PUBLIC_* var found is intentionally public (NEXT_PUBLIC_ADSENSE_CLIENT_ID, NEXT_PUBLIC_EMERGENCY_DIAL, NEXT_PUBLIC_VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY, NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL, NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL) — no private keys cross the client boundary (the VAPID *public* key is correct; the private key stays server-side). next.config.ts does not set productionBrowserSourceMaps and no .map files are tracked in git. No process.env values are logged at startup (only port + NODE_ENV). CSP is enforcing with unsafe-eval removed and object-src 'none'. Recorded as info: this dimension is largely healthy.

verifyNote: Only intentional NEXT_PUBLIC_* vars present (ADSENSE_CLIENT_ID, API_BASE_URL, EMERGENCY_DIAL, SITE_URL, VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY). next.config.ts has no productionBrowserSourceMaps setting; git ls-files shows no tracked .map files. Healthy; info is correct.

evidence: npm audit flags @hono/node-server <1.19.13 GHSA-92pp-h63x-v22m (serveStatic middleware bypass via repeated slashes, CVSS 5.3) via @prisma/dev. `npm ls` confirms the chain is rooted at the `prisma` CLI, which is a devDependency in all three workspaces (apps/api deps.prisma=None, devDeps.prisma=^7.8.0; same for web/db). title: Postinstall/install scripts and rollup native-binary lockfile hack are all legitimate
evidence: Only 7 lockfile entries carry install scripts, all well-known and expected: @prisma/engines, prisma, esbuild (x2), fsevents, sharp, unrs-resolver — no unrecognized/typosquat package runs an install script. No `deprecated` entries exist anywhere in the lockfile (tar is current at 7.5.15, not the deprecated <6 line). The rollup native-binary hack is correctly implemented: @rollup/rollup-linux-x64-gnu is pinned to 4.60.4 (exactly matching resolved rollup 4.60.4) as an optionalDependency with os:['linux']/cpu:['x64'], so npm skips it on non-Linux hosts and the Linux CI runner installs it — sound workaround for npm bug #4828, not a risk.
recommendation: No action. When bumping rollup/vite/vitest, keep the @rollup/rollup-linux-x64-gnu pin in sync with the new resolved rollup version (rollup loads its native binary by exact-version match) or CI will break.
verifyNote: Verified from package-lock.json: exactly 7 entries with hasInstallScript — @prisma/engines, prisma, esbuild (x2: top-level and vite's nested), fsevents, sharp, unrs-resolver. All well-known, no typosquats. Zero `deprecated` entries. tar is 7.5.15 (current, via @capacitor/cli). Root optionalDependencies pins @rollup/rollup-linux-x64-gnu 4.60.4 exactly matching resolved rollup 4.60.4, with os:[linux]/cpu:[x64]. All legitimate; info correct.

title: Sec-Fetch-Site CSRF guard is defense-in-depth only; primary CSRF protection is Bearer-token-from-localStorage (cookies not used)
evidence: auth.ts/jwt.ts use Bearer header only (not cookies) and the web client stores the token in localStorage ('travelsafe.token') and attaches Authorization: Bearer. A cross-site attacker page cannot read another origin's localStorage nor set an Authorization header cross-origin, so classic cookie-CSRF does not apply. csrfGuard blocks Sec-Fetch-Site: cross-site and allows absent header (curl/native) — sound layered hardening, not the load-bearing control. CORS credentials:true is similarly low-impact since no credential is carried in a cookie.
recommendation: No action required; document that auth is non-cookie Bearer. If cookie sessions are ever added, the Sec-Fetch-Site guard alone would be insufficient (allows the absent-header case).
verifyNote: Verified auth.ts:57-58/63 and 81-84 use only the Authorization: Bearer header (no cookie parsing); api-client.ts reads/writes localStorage key 'travelsafe.token' (lines 21,33-34) and attaches Authorization: Bearer (line 122). csrf.ts:53-63 allows absent Sec-Fetch-Site and same-origin/same-site/none, blocks cross-site — layered hardening, not load-bearing since no cookie credential exists. CORS credentials:true (index.ts:234) is low-impact for the same reason. Info is correct.

title: API CORS_ORIGINS defaults to localhost-only with credentials:true; relies on prod env override but fails safe
evidence: corsOrigins defaults to http://localhost:3000; the prod web origin must be supplied via Railway CORS_ORIGINS (only present in .env.example in-repo). The origin callback does cb(null,false) for unknown origins (no ACAO header emitted), so misconfiguration fails closed. The primary flow is same-origin (web client calls /api/* Next Route Handlers that server-side proxy to Railway), so direct browser CORS to Railway is secondary. Live Railway env value not verifiable from the repo.
recommendation: Confirm CORS_ORIGINS is set in Railway prod to the exact production web origin(s), no wildcard, no trailing-slash mismatch. Fail-closed default keeps this low-risk.
verifyNote: Verified env.ts:28 CORS_ORIGINS defaults to 'http://localhost:3000'; index.ts:230-234 origin callback returns cb(null,false) for unknown origins (no ACAO header → browser blocks, fails closed) with credentials:true. CORS_ORIGINS only present in .env.example in-repo. The live Railway prod value cannot be confirmed from the repo, but every code-level claim is accurate and the fail-closed default keeps it low-risk. Info/medium-confidence framing is appropriate.

POST /push/subscribe is requireAuth + writeLimiter with zod validation.

The stale comment ('CommunitySafe has no native app yet') is factually wrong and could mislead a future change that adds a separate native client expecting the guard's 'absent header = allow' branch to handle it.

Comment is factually stale and could mislead a future native-client change relying on the absent-header allow branch. Documentation-only. Info is correct.

Minor nuance: these routes return HTTP 200 (Next RSC handles the redirect client-side) rather than a 3xx, but they ARE redirect-only by source — claim substantively accurate. Info severity correct.

title: POSITIVE: web security headers, TLS, source-map hygiene, and error handling are strong
evidence: Confirmed on live: Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload; X-Frame-Options: DENY; X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff; Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin; Permissions-Policy: geolocation=(self), camera=(), microphone=(), payment=(), usb=(), interest-cohort=(), browsing-topics=(); Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin. HTTP->HTTPS returns 308 to https. JS chunk .map returns 403 (source maps not served). 404 page is clean (no stack/X-Powered-By). Malformed JSON to API returns 400 {"error":"invalid_json"} (no stack leak). No Set-Cookie issued (token-in-body model, so no missing-Secure/HttpOnly cookie risk). Server header is the generic 'Vercel' / 'railway-edge'; no X-Powered-By.
recommendation: No action required; maintain these. (Note x-vercel deployment-id dpl_... appears in an inline script attribute — normal Vercel behavior, not actionable.)
verifyNote: All independently reproduced live: HSTS max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload; X-Frame-Options DENY; X-Content-Type-Options nosniff; Referrer-Policy strict-origin-when-cross-origin; Permissions-Policy geolocation=(self),camera=(),microphone=(),payment=(),usb=(),interest-cohort=(),browsing-topics=(); COOP same-origin. http->https = 308. JS chunk .map = 403. 404 page clean (no X-Powered-By, no stack). Malformed JSON to API -> 400 {"error":"invalid_json"} no stack. No Set-Cookie on web or API login (token-in-body). Server header generic Vercel/railway-edge. Accurate positive note.

title: Protected routes correctly return 401 unauthenticated; RBAC returns 403
evidence: GET /auth/me, /contacts, /safety/check-in/active all returned HTTP 401 with no token. With a valid throwaway-account token, /moderation/queue returned HTTP 403 {"error":"moderator_only"} (in-handler requireModerator works). /auth/me returned only the caller's own user record. No authorization bypass observed.
recommendation: No action needed. Authentication and moderator RBAC are enforced correctly.
verifyNote: Live: GET /auth/me, /contacts, /safety/check-in/active all returned 401 with no token; /moderation/queue returned 401 (missing_bearer_token) unauthenticated. The requireModerator->403 'moderator_only' RBAC is verified in apps/api/src/routes/moderation.routes.ts:21-57 (trustLevel===MODERATOR with bootstrap CSV fallback). I did not create an account to observe the 403-with-valid-token leg, but the code path is unambiguous. Auth + moderator RBAC enforced correctly.

title: No IDOR on owner-scoped resources; non-owned/fabricated id returns 404
evidence: DELETE /contacts/cmxxxxxxxx000000000000000 (a fabricated/non-owned id) with my throwaway token returned HTTP 404 {"error":"not_found"} rather than 403/200, and GET /contacts returned only my own (empty) list. Resource access is scoped to the authenticated owner.
recommendation: No action needed. Object-level authorization is correctly enforced.
verifyNote: Verified in apps/api/src/services/contacts.service.ts: removeContact (line 91-95) uses prisma.trustedContact.deleteMany({where:{id,userId}}) and throws HttpError(404,'not_found') when result.count===0; listContacts (line 14-20) is scoped by userId. A non-owned/fabricated id therefore returns 404, not 403/200. Object-level authorization is owner-scoped. Did not exercise live with a throwaway account, but the query scoping is conclusive.

title: JWT alg:none / signature-strip tampering rejected
location: src/lib/jwt.ts (HS256 verify) / Bearer auth on Railway API
evidence: Submitting a forged token with header {"alg":"none"} and the original (unsigned) payload to GET /auth/me returned HTTP 401. The genuine token decodes to HS256 claims {uid,email,ver,typ:"access"} as documented; verification rejects unsigned/alg-confusion tokens.
recommendation: No action needed. Confirms HS256 verification is not vulnerable to alg-confusion.
verifyNote: Reproduced live: a forged token with header {alg:none} + unsigned access payload sent to GET /auth/me returned 401 {error:invalid_token}. apps/api/src/lib/jwt.ts verifySession uses jwt.verify(token, env.JWT_SECRET) (HS256), which rejects alg:none. No alg-confusion bypass.

title: Account-delete revokes the access token immediately (tokenVersion bump)
evidence: After POST /auth/account/delete returned HTTP 200, reusing the same access token on GET /auth/me returned HTTP 401 {"error":"token_revoked"}. The GDPR soft-delete + tokenVersion revocation path works end to end. (Throwaway test account was created and deleted during this probe.)
recommendation: No action needed. Token revocation on delete is effective within the same instance.
verifyNote: Verified in code: auth.service.ts softDeleteAccount (line 189-198) sets deletedAt + tokenVersion {increment:1}; middleware/auth.ts isTokenRevoked (line 48-53) returns revoked when user missing, deletedAt set, or payload.ver < dbVer; requireAuth then returns 401 token_revoked. invalidateTokenVersionCache also flushes the 30s cache on delete. The same-instance revocation-on-delete path is sound. Did not run the destructive create+delete leg live; code is conclusive.

title: Login rate limiter (20/15min/IP) enforced with draft-7 headers; generic error prevents user enumeration
evidence: Repeated failed logins returned HTTP 401 {"error":"invalid_credentials"} (no distinction between unknown-user and wrong-password) and the RateLimit header counter decremented monotonically: remaining=17 -> 15 -> 14 -> 13 against limit=20, w=900s. authLimiter is live. Stopped after 6 login requests per probe rules.
recommendation: No action needed. Consider per-account (not just per-IP) lockout for credential-stuffing defense-in-depth, but current per-IP limiter is reasonable.
verifyNote: Reproduced live: 5 failed POST /auth/login returned draft-7 RateLimit headers limit=20, w=900s, remaining decremented monotonically 19->18->17->16->15; unknown-user logins returned generic {error:invalid_credentials} (no enumeration). Code: rate-limit.ts authLimiter (20/15min), auth.service.ts login returns invalid_credentials for both unknown-user (line 52) and wrong-password (line 79). MINOR CORRECTION to the recommendation: a per-account lockout ALREADY exists (account_locked after repeated failures, auth.service.ts:59-66,76), so the 'consider per-account lockout' suggestion is partly redundant; the finding's core verdict is unaffected.

title: CORS denies unknown origins (no ACAO header echoed); preflight returns clean 200
evidence: OPTIONS /auth/login with Origin: https://evil.example.com returned HTTP 200 but with NO access-control-allow-origin / -credentials headers at all. GET /health with the evil origin likewise returned no ACAO. Since credentials:true is set, the absence of an echoed ACAO means a malicious cross-origin site cannot read authenticated responses.
recommendation: No action needed. The cb(null,false) behavior correctly refuses to reflect untrusted origins despite credentials:true.
verifyNote: Reproduced live: OPTIONS /auth/login and GET /health with Origin: https://evil.example.com returned 200 with NO access-control-allow-origin/-credentials headers. Code: index.ts cors origin callback cb(null,false) for unknown origins with credentials:true, so untrusted origins are never reflected. (Note: even the legit communitysafe.app origin got no ACAO, consistent with server-side proxying rather than browser CORS for the custom domain; does not weaken the finding.)

title: Error responses are terse machine codes with no stack traces or internal path leakage
location: Both API surfaces (404 handler, JSON body parser, validation)
evidence: Railway 404 -> {"error":"not_found"}; malformed JSON to /auth/register -> {"error":"invalid_json"}; bad safety-score city -> 404; share token invalid -> 404. Validation errors return structured Zod issue lists (field names like label/permissionAcknowledged/email) but no stack traces, file paths, or SQL. Web 404 is a styled Next.js page (noindex).
recommendation: No action needed. Zod issue echoes are acceptable; no sensitive internals leaked.
verifyNote: Reproduced live: 404 -> {error:not_found}; malformed JSON to /auth/register -> {error:invalid_json} (400); login validation -> {error:validation_failed, issues:[{path:[password],message:...}]} structured Zod issues only, no stack traces, file paths, or SQL. Web 404 is a styled Next.js page. Terse machine codes with no internal leakage.

title: Seattle (-1,-1) sentinel and out-of-range junk correctly filtered at the adapter (regression fix verified)
evidence: The historical bug (a `!== 0` guard letting SPD's (-1,-1) missing-geocode sentinel through, dragging centroids ~80mi east) is fixed: `geocoded` now requires Number.isFinite(lat)&&Number.isFinite(lon) AND a Seattle bbox clamp (lat 47.3-47.85, lon -122.6 to -122.2), and discovery requires count>=3 before averaging a centroid (line 133). No other adapter or the snap layer special-cases -1,-1, but they don't need to since invalid rows are dropped before centroid computation. The web /api/geo/lookup lat/lng path also rejects non-finite input (route.ts:33).
recommendation: No action required. Optional hardening: add a finiteness/bbox sanity check inside nearestArea/nearestAreaForCity so a future adapter that forgets the per-city clamp can't poison the cross-city snap.
verifyNote: Verified accurate. packages/crime-data/src/adapters/seattle-socrata.ts:83-84 requires Number.isFinite(lat)&&Number.isFinite(lon) plus the Seattle bbox clamp (lat 47.3-47.85, lon -122.6 to -122.2); invalid rows keep the incident count but carry no map point. Discovery requires count>=3 before centroiding (line 133). Web route.ts:33 rejects non-finite lat/lng input. Regression fix and the optional-hardening note are correct. Info, no action.

title: 'Live Share' / SOS link is a one-time static map pin, not a live-updating position, despite 'live location' wording
evidence: createLiveShare mints a token + expiry only (live-share.service.ts:16-28); there is no endpoint to update the shared position over time, and the public share view (share/[token]/page.tsx:24) renders only the text 'A contact is sharing their location with you.' with no coordinates/map. The single coordinate the contact gets is the static maps.google.com q= pin captured at SOS/arm time. Emails call it a 'Live location link' (sos.ts:55) and the privacy policy frames Live Share as ongoing sharing, but the position does not actually update.
recommendation: Either implement a position-update endpoint so the shared link reflects current location, or adjust the copy (email body + privacy policy) to describe it as a one-time location snapshot plus a status link, so contacts don't over-rely on it in an emergency.
verifyNote: Verified. createLiveShare mints token+expiry only with no position fields (apps/web/src/server/services/safety/live-share.ts:31-78 and apps/api equivalent); resolveSharedView returns {expiresAt, userId} only; the public share page (apps/web/src/app/share/[token]/page.tsx:24-30) renders no coordinates/map and even states 'Live coordinate streaming is on the roadmap.' The one coordinate a contact gets is the static maps.google.com q= pin captured at SOS time (sos.ts:48), yet the SOS email labels it a 'Live location link' (sos.ts:56). Mismatch between 'live' wording and a one-time static pin confirmed. Two caveats: the share page itself IS honest about no live streaming, and the privacy policy (privacy/page.tsx:64) only stores token/channel/expiry for Live Share — so the finding's claim that the policy 'frames Live Share as ongoing sharing' is a mild overstatement; the misleading copy is the SOS email body. Info severity appropriate.

title: National-rate constant is 2025 data but every consumer references the FBI_NATIONAL_PER_100K_2024 alias and comments/disclaimers still say 2024
evidence: FBI_NATIONAL_PER_100K_2025 = {PERSONS:328, PROPERTY:1548}; the _2024 and _2023 names are aliased to the same object. Every call site (divergence ratio, gradeCitywideAbsolute, every row's nationalPer100k/deltaPct) imports FBI_NATIONAL_PER_100K_2024, and the citywide-aggregate function header comment (:859-864) and getSafetyScore comments still say 'FBI 2024 national average' / 'US Census Vintage 2024'. Values are correct (aliases resolve to the 2025 numbers) so this is naming/comment debt, not a data error — but it makes the code read as if it compares against a stale year.
recommendation: Migrate consumers to the FBI_NATIONAL_PER_100K_2025 name (keep the aliases for back-compat) and update the lingering 2024 comments so the source vintage in code matches the published-year value actually used.
verifyNote: Reproduced. FBI_NATIONAL_PER_100K_2025={PERSONS:328,PROPERTY:1548} (:31); _2024 and _2023 are aliased to the same object (:35-36). Every consumer imports the _2024 name (grep confirms usages at :642,:740-741,:1111-1124,:1549-1564) and lingering 2024 comments remain (:860 'FBI 2024 national average', :1437 '2024 city population'; disclaimer at :1247 says 'Vintage 2023-2024'). Values are correct because aliases resolve to the 2025 numbers, so this is naming/comment debt, not a data error. Info severity correct.

title: Check-in timer has no grace window and worker fires on a polling tick — effective firing can be up to one interval late, with no pre-expiry nudge
evidence: armCheckIn sets scheduledFor = now + durationMinutes with no separate grace period. The worker polls every CHECKIN_WORKER_INTERVAL_SECONDS (min clamped to 5s, comment says 30s) selecting ACTIVE timers with scheduledFor <= now, take:100. So a timer fires somewhere between 0 and one full interval after the scheduled instant, and there is no 'you're about to be reported, tap to extend' nudge before contacts are alerted. The atomic claim (triggerExpiry updateMany WHERE status=ACTIVE) correctly prevents double-notification, and inFlight prevents overlapping ticks — those are good. This is a UX/safety-tuning note, not a bug.
recommendation: Consider a short grace window plus a pre-expiry push ('your check-in expires in N min, tap to extend or mark safe') before fanning out to contacts, and document the worst-case firing latency = one worker interval.
verifyNote: Confirmed as an accurate UX/tuning note. armCheckIn (check-in.service.ts:20) sets scheduledFor = now + durationMinutes*60000 with no separate grace window. The worker (check-in.worker.ts:49 intervalMs = Math.max(5, CHECKIN_WORKER_INTERVAL_SECONDS)*1000; comment line 7 references the 30s tick) polls ACTIVE timers with scheduledFor <= now, take:100 (lines 20-24), so firing is 0..one-interval late with no pre-expiry nudge. The atomic claim via updateMany WHERE status=ACTIVE (check-in.service.ts:61-65) and the inFlight backpressure flag (worker.ts:14,17) are correctly implemented as the finding credits. info is correct — not a bug.

title: Honest fallback chain (LKG-before-mock, production mock-bar) and OOM gate verified correct
evidence: MOCK_FALLBACK_ALLOWED=NODE_ENV!=='production'||adapter==='mock' (dispatcher.ts:46) bars synthetic about:blank rows in prod; leaf methods try real->SANDAG(SD)->LKG->mock-or-empty. tryAdapter logs+returns null on throw (no crash). compute-limit acquire/drain/release piggybacks same-city composers onto one slot, counts only distinct cities against MAX; gated composers call only leaf methods that re-enter with the SAME city key and piggyback (never queue), so no gate-within-gate deadlock. touchRowCache(adapter.name) labels match registerRowCache labels (sfpd-socrata/dc-mpd-arcgis/boston-ckan/phoenix-ckan verified equal) so LRU eviction keeps hot cities warm. Per-adapter caches gate on rows.length>0 and serve stale cache on fetch error (sf:118-123,dc:215-220,phl:194-200,boston:366-405).
recommendation: No action — positive verification recorded so the medium findings are read in context.
verifyNote: All positive verifications hold. dispatcher.ts:46 MOCK_FALLBACK_ALLOWED = NODE_ENV!=='production' || adapter==='mock' bars synthetic rows in prod. LKG-before-mock confirmed in getIncidents (116-119) and getAreaStats (97-102). compute-limit.ts acquire (41-48) piggybacks same-city composers on one slot and counts only distinct cities; MAX default 6 (32-35, COMPUTE_CONCURRENCY env, clamped >=1); only the six dedupe-wrapped citywide composers are gated and their bodies call only leaf methods that re-enter with the same city key and piggyback, so no gate-within-gate deadlock (matches comment 22-26). cache-registry registerRowCache uses adapter.name as label (48-53) and dispatcher.ts:29 calls touchRowCache(adapter.name), so labels match for LRU eviction. Per-adapter caches gate on rows.length>0 and serve stale on error (dc 215/219, phl 195/199, detroit 210/216). Info severity appropriate.

title: Official-alerts response exposes no fetched-at / staleness signal; stale cache served silently on upstream error
evidence: On a non-OK upstream response or thrown error each adapter returns the last cached alerts (e.g. nws.ts:61,87; amber.ts:53,73; usgs.ts:74,96) with no indication to the caller that the data is stale, and the route JSON has no fetchedAt/asOf/stale field. OfficialAlertsPanel only shows an error string when error && !data (line 77) — i.e. only on a total first-load failure; once a value is cached it will keep rendering possibly-stale alerts (including ones past their expires, since /alerts/active results aren't re-filtered by expires in route.ts) with no 'as of' timestamp. This contrasts with the rest of the app's documented freshness-banner discipline.
recommendation: Return a per-source fetchedAt/stale flag from the adapters and surface an 'as of <time>' line (and a stale badge when serving fallback cache) in the Weather/AMBER/Traffic panels; optionally drop alerts whose expires is in the past before returning.
verifyNote: Reproduced. nws.ts:61,87 / amber.ts:53,73 / usgs.ts:74,96 all return hit?.alerts ?? [] on non-OK or thrown error with no stale flag. route.ts:61-74 JSON has no fetchedAt/asOf/stale field. OfficialAlertsPanel.tsx:77 only shows the error string when (error && !data) — i.e. only on total first-load failure; cached data renders silently thereafter. Minor caveat: NWS /alerts/active already returns only active alerts, so the 'past expires' sub-claim is partly mitigated upstream except when serving the stale-cache fallback. Info rating appropriate.

title: Stale comments misdescribe current behavior (overwatch mount strategy, backdrop chunk, photo count)
evidence: overwatch/page.tsx top comment (and the recon map) say Crime Map + Safe Route 'both stay mounted via the `hidden` attribute', but the actual JSX (lines 54-72) uses conditional mounting (`{tab === 'map' && ...}`) — the better choice perf-wise (avoids keeping two Leaflet/RouteMap trees alive) and explained in the inline comment at 54-62, but the file header contradicts it. CityBackdrop.tsx:20 says '4 photos'; data has 8. CityBackdropLazy.tsx:4 cites a '169 kB chunk' for a file that is 57KB on disk. None affect runtime, but they mislead future perf work.
recommendation: Reconcile the stale comments with the shipped code. The conditional-mount behavior in overwatch is correct — only the header comment needs updating.
verifyNote: Verified: overwatch/page.tsx:14-17 header comment says Crime Map + Safe Route 'stay mounted across tab switches via the hidden attribute', but JSX at lines 63-71 uses conditional mounting ({tab==='map'&&...}/{tab==='route'&&...}), with the correct rationale in the inline comment at 54-62. CityBackdrop.tsx:20 says '4 photos' while data has 8. CityBackdropLazy.tsx:4 cites a '169 kB chunk'; city-photos.ts (the bundled data) is 57242 bytes on disk and CityBackdrop.tsx is 2844 bytes. All three comments are stale/inaccurate; none affect runtime. Info is correct.

title: Full page subtree remounts on every route change via key={pathname}
evidence: AnimatedMain wraps all (app) page content in `<main key={pathname}>`. Changing the key forces React to unmount the entire previous page tree and mount the new one on every navigation (to re-run the animate-fade-in CSS). This discards all client component state and re-runs every useApi hook's mount effect on each route switch. In practice this is largely mitigated by the api-client's localStorage SWR cache (cached data renders instantly while it revalidates) and the in-flight GET dedup, so network cost is bounded; the cost is re-hydration/re-render of card-heavy pages (e.g. /city mounts ~12 fetching panels) on each visit.
recommendation: Acceptable as-is given SWR cushioning. If page-transition jank appears on low-end devices, drive the fade with a CSS animation keyed off a data attribute / View Transitions API instead of remounting the whole subtree, so component state and in-progress fetches survive navigation.
verifyNote: Verified: AnimatedMain.tsx:15-19 wraps all (app) content in <main key={pathname}>, used in (app)/layout.tsx:57-67, forcing a full subtree unmount/remount per navigation. /city page mounts many fetching panels (citywide useApi at page.tsx:51, CityScoreCard, IncidentSummaryCard, HotspotCard, OfficialAlertsPanel, NewsPanel, TrafficAlertsPanel). Mitigation is real: api-client.ts has localStorage SWR cache (SWR_DEFAULT_MS 15min, line 152) and in-flight GET dedup (inflightGets map, line 90), bounding network cost to re-render/re-hydrate. Severity info matches the bounded, acceptable-as-is nature. Confirmed.

title: Compute gate, dedupe, watchdog, async geojson, and freshness caps are well-engineered and correct
evidence: Positive confirmations: (1) The city-keyed semaphore correctly models 'distinct cities parsing concurrently' as the memory driver; same-city composers piggyback (acquire line 43-44) so a single page's ~7 composers share one slot and never self-deadlock; drain() correctly sweeps same-city waiters before allocating new slots. (2) Citywide composers are dedupe(outer)+withComputeLimit(inner) wrapped (dispatcher 164, safety-score 866, trend 133, insights.service 105) so concurrent identical requests collapse before acquiring a slot. (3) The memory watchdog polls 8s, has cold-then-full two-phase eviction, and gc() is real because the prod start script sets --expose-gc --max-old-space-size=3584 (apps/api/package.json:11). (4) polygon geojson is read with async fs.readFile + per-city module cache (polygon-areas.ts:69-97) — no sync blocking. (5) The v106 freshness-sample 8s caps and 10s discover cap (status.ts:65-68,110,112) and the undici 30s header/body timeouts (index.ts:93-94) are sound bounds on cold-start cost. (6) compression skips text/event-stream so SSE is not buffered (index.ts:203-207).
recommendation: No action; recorded so the strong parts of the compute/OOM design are not regressed by future changes. The defects above are refinements on an otherwise solid foundation.
verifyNote: All six positive claims independently verified: (1) per-city semaphore in compute-limit.ts:41-92 piggybacks same-city composers (acquire 43-44) and drain() sweeps same-city waiters — no self-deadlock. (2) dedupe+withComputeLimit wrapping confirmed at dispatcher.ts:164, safety-score.ts:866, trend-feed.ts:133, and insights.service.ts:105. (3) watchdog polls 8s with two-phase cold->full eviction (index.ts:120-160) and gc() is real because start script sets --expose-gc --max-old-space-size=3584 (apps/api/package.json:11). (4) polygon geojson uses async fs.readFile + per-city module cache (polygon-areas.ts:69-97). (5) freshness 8s/discover 10s caps (status.ts:65-68,110,112) and undici 30s header/body timeouts (index.ts:93-94) confirmed. (6) compression skips text/event-stream (index.ts:203-207). Solid foundation; info is correct.

title: getCitywide recomputes the full per-area aggregate on every request (no computed-result memo), but inflight dedupe + compute-limit + HTTP s-maxage already bound the cost
evidence: getCitywide composes per-area incident fetches and recomputes percentile ranking on each call; there is no memoized result cache (only the 5-min adapter row cache underneath). Mitigations are real and layered: lib/inflight.dedupe collapses concurrent same-city composes (dispatcher.ts:164), withComputeLimit gates concurrency (the OOM guard), cache-registry.ts provides LRU row-cache + memory watchdog, and the route sets Cache-Control public, s-maxage=300, stale-while-revalidate=900 (crime-data.routes.ts:81) so the Vercel edge absorbs repeat hits. The work is CPU-only over already-cached rows. Noting as residual, not a defect — a 60-120s in-process result memo keyed by city/offense/window would shave the recompute for the gap between edge revalidations on the largest cities (Oakland/Detroit 130+ areas).
recommendation: Optional: add a short-TTL (60-120s) in-process memo of the composed getCitywide result keyed by city|offense|window to skip recompute between edge revalidations on high-area cities. Not required given existing edge caching.
verifyNote: Verified as an accurate residual note, not a defect. dispatcher.ts:145-165 getCitywide recomposes per-area aggregates with no result memo; line 164 wraps in dedupe(...) + withComputeLimit(...). crime-data.routes.ts:81 sets 'public, s-maxage=300, stale-while-revalidate=900'. All cited primitives exist: lib/inflight.ts:22 dedupe, lib/compute-limit.ts:88 withComputeLimit, cache-registry.ts present. Mitigations are real and layered exactly as described. Correctly rated info; the optional short-TTL memo suggestion is reasonable but not required.

title: neighborhood-population precompute is a compiled static table — no runtime cost (positive finding)
evidence: GENERATED_NEIGHBORHOOD_POPS is a build-time generated TS constant imported directly (neighborhood-population.ts:1), resolved via a 3-tier lookup (manual POP map -> generated ACS table -> null/heuristic fallback). It is bundled, not fetched or recomputed at request time, so the population denominator path in safety-score.ts adds no I/O. Confirms the precompute strategy is sound from a performance standpoint.
recommendation: No action. Keep generation in tools/build-neighborhood-populations.mjs out of the request path as-is.
verifyNote: Verified positive finding. neighborhood-populations-generated.ts is 1762 lines; neighborhood-population.ts:1 imports GENERATED_NEIGHBORHOOD_POPS as a static constant. Lookup at :85-93 is the described 3-tier path (manual POP map -> generated ACS table -> null), all in-memory constant lookups with zero request-time I/O. Bundled, not fetched/recomputed. Correctly rated info; no action needed.

title: Crime-map geometry and feed-to-polygon mapping are clean for all four cities (no broken maps, no bad coords, no slug labels)
evidence: Each city has a present, non-empty GeoJSON with a 'name' property on every feature. Geometry validation across all four: 0 features with points outside the city bbox, 0 degenerate (<3-point) polygons, 0 null-island / (0,0) / (-1,-1) coordinates. All names are real neighborhoods (not slugs, ZIPs, or 'Ward N'/'Beat NNX' artifacts — DC explicitly suppresses 'Ward N' fallback buckets from discovery at dc-arcgis.ts:234, Oakland drops unresolved points rather than emit 'Beat 19X'). Adapter-to-map join is structural, not heuristic: DC/Boston/Phl/Oakland all geocode incidents by point-in-polygon into the very same bundled polygon files the served maps come from, so feed area labels equal polygon names. Exact name set-diffs: Oakland 131 served == 131 bundled (perfect), Boston 26 == 26 (perfect), DC 51 polygons == 51 served (perfect), Philadelphia 158 adapter vs 159 served (one orphan, separate finding). No AREA_ALIASES entries are needed for any of the four cities in CrimeMap.tsx (the alias table only patches Detroit/NOLA/Stapleton).
recommendation: No action required for map integrity. Maintain the shared-polygon-bundle pattern (adapter geocoder and served GeoJSON sourced from the same file) for future cities, as it is the reason these four have zero label-mismatch coverage gaps.
verifyNote: Geometry/structure claims independently verified clean: all four geojsons present and non-empty, name on every feature, 0 features outside city bbox, 0 degenerate polygons, 0 null-island/(0,0)/(-1,-1) points. Set-diffs confirmed exactly: Oakland 131 served == 131 bundled, Boston 26 == 26, DC 51 == 51, Philadelphia 158 adapter vs 159 served (the one orphan). Ward-N suppression confirmed at dc-arcgis.ts:234; Oakland drops unresolved points to 'Unknown' rather than 'Beat 19X' (oakland-socrata.ts:179-188). BUT two concrete claims are wrong: (1) the alias identifier in CrimeMap.tsx is NAME_ALIASES, not 'AREA_ALIASES' (no such symbol exists in apps/web/src); (2) that table is NOT 'only Detroit/NOLA/Stapleton' — it also patches San Diego (7 entries), Pittsburgh, Cincinnati, and SF (CrimeMap.tsx:59-89). The relevant sub-claim (none of DC/Boston/Phl/Oakland need an alias entry) is true. Marking uncertain because the evidence text contains those two false specifics even though the central integrity thesis holds. Info severity retained.

title: POSITIVE: API CORS rejects unknown origins; auth, RBAC, rate-limit, and token revocation enforced
evidence: GET /crime-data/citywide with Origin: https://evil.example.com returned NO access-control-allow-origin header (origin not echoed). Throwaway-account checks: /auth/me with no token -> 401, with garbage token -> 401, with valid token -> 200; /moderation/queue as a normal authenticated user -> 403 (RBAC holds), no auth -> 401; 6 rapid bad logins -> 401,401,429,429,429,429 (authLimiter trips after 2); after POST /auth/account/delete the previously-valid access token immediately returned token_revoked / 401 (revocation effective). API also sets HSTS, nosniff, X-Frame SAMEORIGIN, x-xss-protection: 0, x-permitted-cross-domain-policies: none. Throwaway account was deleted as cleanup.
recommendation: No action required; behavior matches a well-configured allowlist + JWT-revocation model. Minor: API CORP is cross-origin on JSON data endpoints (expected/intended for the web client) — leave as-is.
verifyNote: Core CORS claim CONFIRMED: unknown Origin gets no Access-Control-Allow-Origin echo (verified live; source index.ts:230-233 uses cb(null,false) for non-allowlisted origins). Unauthenticated 401s confirmed live: /auth/me no token -> 401, garbage token -> 401, /moderation/queue no auth -> 401. HOWEVER a load-bearing evidence detail is INACCURATE per code: the claim '6 rapid bad logins -> 401,401,429,429,429,429 (authLimiter trips after 2)' contradicts rate-limit.ts:3-8 where authLimiter limit is 20 per 15min, and account lockout (User.lockedUntil) fires at 5 failed attempts as HTTP 423, not 429-after-2. The RBAC-403/token_revoked claims (requiring a throwaway account) are consistent with code (auth.ts:65 token_revoked, csrf.ts:64 403) but I did not re-create an account to avoid the request-budget/destructive limits. Overall positive conclusion likely holds, but the specific rate-limit evidence as written is wrong, so marking uncertain rather than confirmed.
